1.1:
ἐν ἀρχῇ ἦν ὁ λόγος — The imperfect ēn (was) is deliberate: it asserts pre-existence, not origin. Gen 1:1 opens "in the beginning God created" — John opens "in the beginning [the Word] already was." The Logos (G3056) is a high-dispute term (dispute level 3): its semantic range spans spoken word, reason, cosmic principle, and divine utterance. The Evangelist draws on all these registers while pressing beyond them.

καὶ θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος — The predicate nominative theos appears without the article. This is not a diminishing of the claim ("a god") but a grammatical consequence of predicate position (Colwell's rule): the anarthrous predicate precedes the verb and emphasizes quality — the Word was of the divine nature. The three clauses of v.1 assert distinct propositions: (1) the Word pre-existed all creation; (2) he was in personal relation with God (pros ton theon, "with God" — a directional preposition implying face-to-face orientation); (3) he shared the divine nature.
1.2: οὗτος ἦν ἐν ἀρχῇ πρὸς τὸν θεόν — The demonstrative houtos ("this one") gathers all three assertions of v.1 into a single referent before the Evangelist moves to creation. The repetition is not redundant: from the vantage point that the Word is God, the prior statements that he was with God and pre-existent are now seen together as the full portrait of the pre-incarnate Son.
1.3:
πάντα δι᾽ αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο — The verb egeneto (came to be, G1096) marks created things: they began to exist. The contrast with ēn (was) in v.1 is exact. The Word was; all things came to be through him. The preposition dia (through) marks instrumental agency — he is the agent through whom the Father creates.

χωρὶς αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο οὐδὲ ἕν — The negative statement does not merely repeat the positive; it closes off every exception. Ouden (nothing) reinforced by hen (one) — not even one thing — leaves no category of created being outside his scope. The Evangelist states the same truth from both directions to prevent any reader from limiting the Word's creative role.
1.4:
ἐν αὐτῷ ζωὴ ἦν — Zōē (G2222, dispute level 2) in John consistently refers to divine life — the life that belongs to God as opposed to merely biological existence (bios). The preposition en autō marks the life as intrinsic to the Logos, not borrowed or bestowed.

καὶ ἡ ζωὴ ἦν τὸ φῶς τῶν ἀνθρώπων — The life of the Logos becomes the light of humans: phōs (G5457) carries the sense of illumination, revelation, and moral clarity. John does not explain the mechanism; he asserts the equation. The light is for humans (tōn anthrōpōn), not a cosmic abstraction.
1.5:
καὶ τὸ φῶς ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ φαίνει — The present tense phainei (shines) is not historical: it describes the ongoing state of the light. The Prologue speaks in timeless register here — before and after the Incarnation, the light shines.

καὶ ἡ σκοτία αὐτὸ οὐ κατέλαβεν — Katelaben is an aorist of katalambanō (G2638), a verb that carries two distinct meanings: (1) to seize/overcome, (2) to comprehend/apprehend. Both may be in play simultaneously. The darkness has not overpowered the light and has not grasped or understood it. The aorist reaches back across the whole prior history of darkness encountering the light.
1.6: ἐγένετο ἄνθρωπος ἀπεσταλμένος παρὰ θεοῦ — The shift from timeless metaphysical statement to historical narrative is marked by egeneto (there came/arose). John the Baptist is introduced as a sent human: apestalmenos (having been sent, divine passive) places him firmly in the category of commissioned agent, not self-appointed prophet. Onoma autō Iōannēs — the Hebrew idiom "name to him was John" signals a figure of designated role.
1.7: οὗτος ἦλθεν εἰς μαρτυρίαν — Martyria (G3141) and its cognates dominate the first chapter. The word belongs to legal and courtroom registers: testimony before judges. John's role is formally evidentiary. The purpose clause hina martyrēsē peri tou phōtos makes his testimony instrumental: so that through him (di᾽ autou) all might believe.
1.8: οὐκ ἦν ἐκεῖνος τὸ φῶς — The emphatic pronoun ekeinos (that one) and the negation ouk ēn together rule out John's identification with the light absolutely. The Evangelist states what John was not before stating what he was, ensuring no reader conflates the Witness with the Thing witnessed. This negation will recur at 1:20 when John himself denies being the Messiah.
1.9:
ἦν τὸ φῶς τὸ ἀληθινόν — Alēthinos (G228, "genuine/real") differs from alēthēs (factually true). The claim is not merely that this light is truthful but that it is the genuine article, the archetype of which all other illumination is derivative. Phōtizei panta anthrōpon — lights every person (not "all men" in a gender-exclusive sense; anthrōpon is generic human).

The participle erchomenon (coming) is syntactically ambiguous: it may modify phōs (the light coming into the world) or anthrōpon (every person coming into the world). Most interpreters attach it to the light, since John's Gospel consistently describes Jesus as "the one coming" into the world.
1.10:
ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἦν — The word kosmos appears three times in this verse, carrying three slightly different senses: (1) the created order where the Word was present, (2) the created order he made, (3) the human world that failed to recognize him. The repetition creates an ironic ladder: he made what he was in; what he made did not know its maker.

καὶ ὁ κόσμος αὐτὸν οὐκ ἔγνω — Egnō (aorist of ginōskō, G1097) means to recognize, to come to know through experience. The world did not recognize him — not merely cognitive failure but relational non-reception.
1.11: εἰς τὰ ἴδια ἦλθεν — Idia (neuter plural) denotes his own domain, his own things — the creation, and specifically Israel as his covenantal inheritance. οἱ ἴδιοι αὐτὸν οὐ παρέλαβον — Idioi (masculine plural) shifts to his own people. The verb paralabein means to receive alongside, to take to oneself. The failure is not ignorance but rejection — they encountered him and did not take him in.
1.12:
ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ἐξουσίαν — Exousia (G1849) is authority or right conferred, not merely ability. The right to become children of God is a granted status, not a latent potential. Teknon (G5043, child) is used throughout John rather than huios (son), which is reserved for Christ. The distinction preserves the uniqueness of the Son's relationship.

τοῖς πιστεύουσιν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ — The present participle pisteuousin describes ongoing believing. Eis to onoma — believing into his name — is directional: not merely believing about him but directing trust toward him as a person.
1.13: οἳ οὐκ ἐξ αἱμάτων οὐδὲ ἐκ θελήματος σαρκὸς οὐδὲ ἐκ θελήματος ἀνδρὸς ἀλλ᾽ ἐκ θεοῦ ἐγεννήθησαν — Three negations pile up before the positive statement. Haimaton (blood, plural in Greek — the mingling of parental bloods as ancient biology conceived generation); thelēmatos sarkos (desire of the flesh — physical reproductive drive); thelēmatos andros (will of a husband — the decisional act of a male head of household). The passive egennēthēsan (were born) marks divine initiative: the subject of the action is God, not the believer. New birth is not self-generated.
1.14:
ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο — Sarx (G4561, dispute level 4) is the highest-contested term in the Johannine vocabulary. Here it does not carry the Pauline sense of sinful nature but denotes the full human condition: mortal, embodied, subject to weakness and death. The choice of sarx over anthrōpos (human) or sōma (body) presses the point of genuine material humanity as far as language allows.

καὶ ἐσκήνωσεν ἐν ἡμῖν — Eskēnōsen (tabernacled, from skēnē = tent) is a Septuagint resonance: the divine Shekinah dwelling in the tabernacle. The Evangelist presents the Incarnation as the climactic divine dwelling — not in curtained fabric but in flesh.

μονογενοῦς παρὰ πατρός — Monogenēs (G3439, only-begotten) carries both uniqueness (one of a kind) and relational weight (the bond between father and only son). Both senses are needed: the gift costs what only an only son costs. Plērēs charitos kai alētheias — full of grace and truth — may echo the LXX pairing of hesed and emet (covenant faithfulness and reliability), the covenant character of God in Exod 34:6.
1.15:
Ἰωάννης μαρτυρεῖ περὶ αὐτοῦ — The present tense martyrei (testifies) treats John's historical testimony as standing testimony: it still witnesses. κέκραγεν is a perfect of krazō (to cry out): John has cried out and that cry stands on record.

ὀπίσω μου ἐρχόμενος ἔμπροσθέν μου γέγονεν — The temporal inversion is deliberate: "the one coming after me has come to rank before me." The explanation follows: hoti prōtos mou ēn — because he was before me (in existence). The one who arrives later in history existed first in eternity. Prōtos here is temporal, not merely positional.
1.16:
ἐκ τοῦ πληρώματος αὐτοῦ — Plērōma (G4138, fullness) is not simply "abundance" but the complete totality of divine grace residing in the Logos. The preposition ek (from/out of) marks derivation: what believers receive is drawn from his inexhaustible supply.

χάριν ἀντὶ χάριτος — The preposition anti is the exegetical crux. It most naturally means "in place of" or "instead of" — one grace replaced by another, grace succeeding grace in sequence (as the Mosaic covenant's grace is superseded by the grace of Christ, developed in v.17). Less likely is the reading "grace upon grace" (accumulation), which requires anti to function like epi.
1.17: ὅτι ὁ νόμος διὰ Μωϋσέως ἐδόθη — The law was given (edothē, aorist passive): it came through Moses as instrument, divinely bestowed, complete in the past. ἡ χάρις καὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ ἐγένετο — Grace and truth came to be (egeneto, aorist of ginomai): they arrived through a person, in an event. This is the first use of "Jesus Christ" by name in the Gospel — the full messianic title at the moment of introducing what surpasses Moses.
1.18:
θεὸν οὐδεὶς ἑώρακεν πώποτε — The perfect heōraken (has seen) covers all history to the present: no one at any point has had a direct vision of God. The claim grounds what follows.

μονογενὴς θεός — The preferred reading of the earliest manuscripts (ℵ* and B) has monogenēs theos (only-begotten God) rather than monogenēs huios (only-begotten Son). The harder reading is textually stronger; copyists were more likely to harmonize toward "Son" (the familiar title) than to introduce "God." Both readings assert the same Christological claim; the difference is terminological, not doctrinal.

ὁ ὢν εἰς τὸν κόλπον τοῦ πατρός — Ho ōn (the one who is/being, present participle) asserts ongoing intimate relation: the Son is continually in the Father's bosom/side (kolpos — the position of reclining proximity at a meal, cf. 13:23). Exēgēsato — he has narrated, disclosed, explained: the source of the English "exegesis." The Son who is at the Father's side is the authoritative expounder of the Father's character.
1.19: καὶ αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ μαρτυρία τοῦ Ἰωάννου — The formal testimony section begins: martyria (G3141) frames what follows as legal evidence. The Ioudaioi — here rendered "Jewish leaders" — sent a delegation of priests and Levites (hiereis kai Leuitas), the credentialed religious authorities, to conduct the inquiry.
1.20: ὡμολόγησεν καὶ οὐκ ἠρνήσατο — The double statement — "he confessed AND did not deny" — is emphatic beyond normal rhetoric. Homologein (to confess publicly, G3670) and arneomai (to deny, G720) form polar opposites; stating both positive and negative reinforces the completeness of John's unambiguous declaration. ὅτι οὐκ εἰμὶ ἐγὼ ὁ χριστός — The word order places egō (I) after the verb for emphasis: "I am not — I — the Christ."
1.21: Ἠλίας εἶ σύ — The questions move through eschatological categories: Elijah (expected to return before the Day of the Lord, Mal 4:5), then "the Prophet" (the Mosaic prophet of Deut 18:15). John denies all three identities. The Synoptic tradition (Matt 11:14) has Jesus identify John as Elijah "in the spirit"; John's Gospel records John's own denial, and both can be true: John is not Elijah literally returned but fulfills Elijah's role.
1.22: The interrogators have exhausted their categories and need a reportable answer. The question tis ei (who are you?) with the demand for a self-definition for those who sent them reflects the bureaucratic obligation of official emissaries to return with usable intelligence.
1.23: ἐγὼ φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ — John identifies himself as "a voice" (phōnē) not a name, a person, or a title. The citation is from Isa 40:3 (LXX): a disembodied voice, instrumental and self-effacing. Euthynate tēn hodon Kyriou — make straight the way of the Lord. The Evangelist applies the LXX Kyriou (of the Lord) to Jesus, a significant Christological move.
1.24: A parenthetical note: among those sent were Pharisees (ek tōn Pharisaiōn). This explains the sharper follow-up question in v.25 — Pharisees were particularly concerned with unauthorized ritual innovation.
1.25: τί οὖν βαπτίζεις εἰ σὺ οὐκ εἶ ὁ χριστός — The logical issue: baptism as a ritual of purification and initiation was understood in Second Temple Judaism as connected to eschatological authority. If John is none of the expected end-time figures, by what authority does he administer a new rite? The question anticipates the sign-demand in 2:18.
1.26: ἐγὼ βαπτίζω ἐν ὕδατι — En hydati (in/with water, dative of instrument). John's baptism is plainly stated as water-only and therefore limited. μέσος ὑμῶν ἕστηκεν ὃν ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε — The verb hestēken is a perfect of histēmi — "has taken his stand and stands." The one they do not know is already present among them, not a future arrival. The contrast between John's visible water-rite and the unrecognized standing one is sharp.
1.27: οὗ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἄξιος ἵνα λύσω αὐτοῦ τὸν ἱμάντα τοῦ ὑποδήματος — Loosening a sandal strap was considered below the dignity of a disciple in relation to his teacher (the Talmud forbade disciples from performing this service for their own rabbi). John places himself below even that level — not a disciple unworthy of the menial task, but one who claims no standing to perform it at all. Axios (G514) = worthy, deserving.
1.28: ἐν Βηθανίᾳ ἐγένετο πέραν τοῦ Ἰορδάνου — The Evangelist specifies Bethany beyond the Jordan — not the Bethany near Jerusalem where Lazarus lived (11:1). The geographic precision serves as eyewitness warrant and distinguishes the two locations.
1.29: ἴδε ὁ ἀμνὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ὁ αἴρων τὴν ἁμαρτίαν τοῦ κόσμου — Amnos (G286, lamb) is the rare LXX word used in Isa 53:7 for the Servant led to slaughter. John does not use the more common arnion; amnos carries sacrificial weight. Airōn is a present participle — ongoing taking away, not a single completed act. Hamartian is singular (the sin, not "sins"): the cosmic condition of humanity before God, not merely individual offenses. Tou kosmou — of the world — extends the scope beyond Israel.
1.30: John repeats his earlier paradox (cf. v.15): the one coming temporally after him has surpassed him because he pre-existed him (prōtos mou ēn). The repetition is not redundant — it recurs now that Jesus is visible, giving the identification claim a definite referent.
1.31: κἀγὼ οὐκ ᾔδειν αὐτόν — The pluperfect ēdein (I had not known him) is significant: John's prior ignorance underscores the divine revelation in v.33. His recognition of Jesus was not prior acquaintance but divine disclosure. The purpose of his water-baptism ministry was that Jesus might be revealed (phanerōthē) to Israel — an apostolic purpose, not a personal one.
1.32: ὅτι τεθέαμαι τὸ πνεῦμα καταβαῖνον ὡς περιστερὰν ἐξ οὐρανοῦ — The perfect tetheamai (I have seen and the seeing stands) makes this eyewitness testimony in the strong sense: John's vision remains his standing evidence. Memenēken ep᾽ auton — the Spirit has remained on him (perfect): a permanent indwelling, not a prophetic visitation that departs. This distinguishes Jesus from OT prophets on whom the Spirit came and went; the dove's resting is the sign.
1.33: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ βαπτίζων ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ — The identification formula houtos estin (this is the one) formally designates Jesus. The contrast is complete: John with water (en hydati, v.31), Jesus with Holy Spirit (en pneumati hagiō). Pneuma (G4151, dispute level 3) is here unambiguous: the divine Spirit (Holy Spirit, not wind or human spirit), identified by the article and hagion.
1.34: κἀγὼ ἑώρακα καὶ μεμαρτύρηκα ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — Both verbs are perfect: heōraka (I have seen) and memartyrēka (I have testified) — John's sight and testimony both stand. The formal declaration huios tou theou (Son of God) closes the Baptist's witness, anticipating Nathanael's confession in v.49 and the Gospel's climactic purpose statement at 20:31.
1.35: A brief transitional verse: tē epaurion (the following day) marks the Evangelist's careful day-by-day structure in ch.1. John is present again with two disciples (duo ek tōn mathētōn autou) — unnamed at this point; one will be identified as Andrew in v.40.
1.36: ἴδε ὁ ἀμνὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — John repeats his identification of Jesus as the Lamb of God, now without the extended clause about taking away sin. In context the compressed repetition functions as a signal to his disciples: look at him. The aorist emblepsas (having fixed his gaze) marks the deliberate, directed attention.
1.37: καὶ ἠκολούθησαν τῷ Ἰησοῦ — The two disciples heard (ēkousan) and followed (ēkolouthēsan). Both are aorists: they heard, they followed. The chain of testimony — John points, disciples follow — is the pattern for the spread of belief throughout ch.1.
1.38: τί ζητεῖτε — The first words Jesus speaks in this Gospel: "What do you seek?" The question recurs thematically at the arrest (18:4, 7). Rhabbi (G4461) is transliterated from Aramaic and then translated: didaskalos (teacher). The parenthetical translations (hermēneuetai) throughout ch.1 signal an audience unfamiliar with Palestinian Jewish terminology. Pou meneis — "where do you stay/abide?" — menō (abide, remain) is a key Johannine verb that will carry enormous theological weight in chs. 14–15.
1.39: ἔρχεσθε καὶ ὄψεσθε — Imperative and future: "Come and you will see." The invitation pattern repeats at v.46. Tēn hēmeran ekeinēn emeinen — they stayed with him that day. The hour notation — hōra ēn hōs dekatē — is approximately 4:00 p.m. by Roman reckoning (the tenth hour of daylight from 6 a.m.) or 10:00 a.m. by Jewish reckoning. The Evangelist's precision may reflect eyewitness memory.
1.40: Ἀνδρέας ὁ ἀδελφὸς Σίμωνος Πέτρου — Andrew is identified as Simon Peter's brother — a reversal of what a reader might expect, since Peter will be the more prominent figure. The Evangelist names Andrew first precisely because he is the one present in this scene. The other disciple (likely the Evangelist himself, though unnamed) is left anonymous.
1.41: εὑρίσκει οὗτος πρῶτον τὸν ἀδελφὸν τὸν ἴδιον Σίμωνα — Prōton (first, perhaps "the first thing he did") — Andrew immediately seeks his own brother. Heuriskeō (find) becomes a structural verb in the call narratives of this chapter. Εὑρήκαμεν τὸν Μεσσίαν — The perfect heurēkamen (we have found!) marks a discovery that stands. Messias is transliterated from Hebrew/Aramaic and interpreted as Christos — both mean "Anointed One."
1.42: σὺ κληθήσῃ Κηφᾶς — The name-change is a future passive: you will be called Cephas. Kēphas is Aramaic for rock; Petros is the Greek equivalent. The parenthetical note ho hermēneuetai Petros again signals the non-Palestinian audience. Jesus gives Simon a new identity on first meeting, before any discipleship is established — a sovereign act of naming, recalling the divine name-changes of Abram/Abraham and Jacob/Israel.
1.43: ἀκολούθει μοι — The only place in ch.1 where Jesus himself initiates a call with the direct imperative "Follow me." Philip is found (heuriskei) by Jesus, not the other way around. The brevity of the exchange — command only, no dialogue — is characteristic of Jesus's authority in the call narratives.
1.44: The geographic note — Bethsaida as home of Philip, Andrew, and Peter — is historically specific and serves both narrative (Philip would have known Andrew) and topographic purposes for an audience mapping the early discipleship community.
1.45: εὑρίσκει Φίλιππος τὸν Ναθαναήλ — Philip continues the chain: found → tells → invites. His summary of what they have found is notably comprehensive: hon egrapsen Mōysēs en tō nomō kai hoi prophētai — the one Moses wrote about in the Law and the prophets also wrote about. He identifies Jesus by Torah and prophetic witness before naming him: "Jesus of Nazareth, son of Joseph" — a fully human identification.
1.46: ἐκ Ναζαρὲτ δύναταί τι ἀγαθὸν εἶναι — Nathanael's response is a proverb or rhetorical question, not personal hostility. Nazareth appears nowhere in OT prophecy; it was an obscure village in Galilee, itself looked down upon by Judeans. Philip's response — erchou kai ide (come and see) — does not argue but invites direct encounter, paralleling Jesus's own invitation in v.39.
1.47: ἴδε ἀληθῶς Ἰσραηλίτης ἐν ᾧ δόλος οὐκ ἔστιν — Alēthōs (truly, genuinely); Israēlitēs — an Israelite in the full covenantal sense; dolos (guile, deceit, G1388) absent from him. The description echoes Ps 32:2 (no guile in his spirit) and implicitly contrasts Nathanael with Jacob/Israel, the original deceiver whose name he bears — a "true Israel" whose heart has no Jacob in it.
1.48: πρὸ τοῦ σε Φίλιππον φωνῆσαι ὄντα ὑπὸ τὴν συκῆν εἶδόν σε — The temporal marker pro tou + infinitive: before Philip called you, while you were under the fig tree, I saw you. The aorist eidon asserts a specific past vision. What Jesus saw under the fig tree is not explained; the significance is the knowledge itself — supernatural insight into a private moment. Fig trees could serve as places of study and prayer in rabbinic culture.
1.49: Ῥαββί, σὺ εἶ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ, σὺ βασιλεὺς εἶ τοῦ Ἰσραήλ — Nathanael's confession doubles: huios tou theou (Son of God) and basileus tou Israēl (King of Israel). The pairing is messianic rather than ontological at this stage — "Son of God" in Ps 2:7 and 2 Sam 7:14 is a royal covenant title for the Davidic king. Both titles will gain deeper content as the Gospel unfolds.
1.50: ὅτι εἶπόν σοι ὅτι εἶδόν σε ὑποκάτω τῆς συκῆς, πιστεύεις — Jesus acknowledges that Nathanael believes on the basis of a single sign (the fig-tree knowledge). The future opsē (you will see, singular) promises greater things: the plural of disciples in v.51 anticipates that Nathanael's seeing will be shared. Faith based on a single supernatural disclosure will grow to sight of heaven opened.
1.51:
ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν — The double amēn is a distinctive Johannine marker: it appears 25 times in this Gospel (always doubled), compared to the single amēn in the Synoptics. The doubling intensifies the solemn authority: what follows is not speculative.

τοὺς ἀγγέλους τοῦ θεοῦ ἀναβαίνοντας καὶ καταβαίνοντας ἐπὶ τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — The vision echoes Jacob's ladder at Bethel (Gen 28:12), where angels ascended and descended on it (or "on him" in some readings). John applies the movement to the Son of Man: Jesus is the locus of divine-human traffic, the new Bethel ("house of God"), the place where heaven opens to earth. Huios tou anthrōpou (Son of Man) appears here for the first time in the Gospel; it will carry an eschatological and exaltation trajectory throughout.

2.1: τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ — Counting from the "next day" of 1:43, this is the third day of that sequence, giving the opening week of Jesus's ministry a structured seven-day shape in the Evangelist's narration. The phrase carries no explicit resurrection symbolism here, but the Evangelist is rarely casual about numerology. Gamos (G1062) is the wedding feast, not merely the ceremony; Kanā tēs Galilaias is specified to distinguish it from a Cana in the Phoenician region.
2.2: ἐκλήθη δὲ καὶ ὁ Ἰησοῦς — The passive eklēthē (was invited) places Jesus in the position of a guest, not the host. The inclusion of his disciples suggests that by this point Jesus was understood to travel with a retinue — the social convention of a teacher and his students dining together as a group was recognized and accommodated.
2.3: ὑστερήσαντος οἴνου — The aorist participle hysterēsantos (having fallen short/run out) indicates the wine supply is already exhausted when Mary speaks. Her statement οἶνον οὐκ ἔχουσιν (they have no wine) is factual but functions as an implicit appeal — she reports the situation to one she expects can act. The Evangelist records no explicit request.
2.4:
τί ἐμοὶ καὶ σοί, γύναι — Gynai (G1135, Woman) is a vocative used by Jesus also at 19:26 (to Mary at the cross) and 20:15 (to Mary Magdalene); it is not dismissive in Greek convention but a respectful address. The idiom ti emoi kai soi — "what to me and to you" — appears in OT contexts (Judg 11:12; 2 Sam 16:10; 1 Kgs 17:18) as a distancing formula marking that two parties have different concerns about a situation, not hostility.

οὔπω ἥκει ἡ ὥρα μου — Hōra (G5610, hour) is theologically loaded in the Fourth Gospel: it refers to the appointed moment of Jesus's glorification through death (7:30; 8:20; 12:23; 13:1; 17:1). "My hour has not yet come" signals that the wedding miracle is not the decisive hour; that moment will come in chs. 12–19. The "not yet" (oupō) implies it is coming.
2.5: ὅ τι ἂν λέγῃ ὑμῖν ποιήσατε — Mary's instruction to the servants (diakonois) uses an aorist imperative poiēsate with an indefinite relative clause (hoti an legē — whatever he says): unconditional and comprehensive obedience is called for. Her response to the rebuff in v.4 is neither resentment nor collapse; she proceeds in confidence.
2.6: λίθιναι ὑδρίαι ἕξ — Stone (lithinos, G3035) water jars are specified because stone vessels could not contract ritual impurity under Levitical law (Lev 11:33 governs clay vessels; stone is exempt). Jewish households that observed toharāh (purity) law kept stone vessels for precisely this reason. Six jars — not seven (the complete number) — may indicate an incomplete state, though the Evangelist does not press this point. The quantity (20–30 gallons each; the Greek metrētas duo ē treis) gives a total of roughly 120–180 gallons: a striking excess of provision.
2.7: γεμίσατε τὰς ὑδρίας ὕδατος — The command is to fill the jars with water, not wine: the servants act in ordinary faith with ordinary water. Heōs anō — to the brim, to the top — signals their complete obedience. No gap or reservation is left for adding anything else; the transformation, when it occurs, cannot be attributed to an adulteration of the water.
2.8: ἀρχιτρικλίνῳ — The compound architriklinos (G755) appears nowhere else in the Bible and rarely in classical literature. It combines archi- (chief/head) and triklinos (three-couch dining room, the standard formal dining arrangement). The role was roughly that of a master of ceremonies, chosen from the guests, who regulated the dinner, tasted the wine, and managed the pace of drinking. He would have professional expertise in judging wine quality.
2.9: ὡς δὲ ἐγεύσατο ὁ ἀρχιτρίκλινος τὸ ὕδωρ οἶνον γεγενημένον — The perfect participle gegenēmenon (having become wine) marks a transformation already completed by the time of tasting: the water has become wine and remains wine. At what moment the transformation occurred the text does not say; the Evangelist locates it between the filling and the drawing-out, keeping attention on the result rather than the process.
2.10: πᾶς ἄνθρωπος πρῶτον τὸν καλὸν οἶνον τίθησιν — The master of the feast states a universal social convention: good wine (kalon oinon) comes first, inferior wine (elassō, the lesser) comes when guests are less discerning. Methysthōsin (when they have become drunk, G3184) is the word used without euphemism in the Synoptics and Pauline letters for drunkenness; the master of the feast describes normal banquet practice. Tetērēkas (you have kept, perfect): the good wine has been kept and kept available — a permanent state of provision.
2.11: Ταύτην ἐποίησεν ἀρχὴν τῶν σημείων ὁ Ἰησοῦς — The Evangelist marks this formally as the archēn tōn sēmeiōn (beginning of the signs). Sēmeion (G4592, sign) is John's consistent vocabulary for Jesus's miracles: he never uses teras (wonder/portent) or dynamis (power/mighty work), the Synoptic terms. A sign points beyond itself to what it signifies — in this case, the glory (doxa, G1391) of Jesus. The disciples believed (episteusan) as a result: faith follows the revelation of glory.
2.12: κατέβη εἰς Καφαρναούμ — The verb katebē (went down) is geographically accurate: Cana sits in the hills of Galilee while Capernaum is on the shore of the Sea of Galilee, at a lower elevation. The mention of hoi adelphoi autou (his brothers) alongside his mother and disciples is a matter-of-fact family reference; the relationship of Jesus's brothers to the discipleship community will become complex later (7:5).
2.13: ἤγγιζεν τὸ πάσχα τῶν Ἰουδαίων — Pascha (G3957, Passover) is the first of three Passovers in John's Gospel (also 6:4 and 11:55/19:14), giving the ministry a three-year framework. Anebē eis Ierosolyma — went up to Jerusalem: the verb anabainō is used for pilgrimage precisely because Jerusalem is geographically elevated (at ~750 m) and theologically the place of ascent.
2.14: ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ τοὺς πωλοῦντας βόας καὶ πρόβατα καὶ περιστεράς — The Evangelist uses hieron (G2411, the entire temple precincts including the Court of the Gentiles) not naos (G3485, the inner sanctuary). The commerce was in the outer courts, theologically the space designated for Gentile worship. The money changers (kollybistai) exchanged foreign currency for the Tyrian shekel required for temple tax — a necessary function, but one that had moved from the Mount of Olives into the sacred courts.
2.15: φραγέλλιον ἐκ σχοινίων — Phragellion (G5416) is a Latinism — from flagellum — indicating the Evangelist's familiarity with Roman terminology. The scourge was made from cords (scholniōn), likely the rope used to tether animals. Execheen (poured out) the money — the coins were scattered; anetrepsen (overturned) the tables; exebalen (drove out) the traders. Three distinct actions, three different verbs: the act is described with eyewitness specificity.
2.16: μὴ ποιεῖτε τὸν οἶκον τοῦ πατρός μου οἶκον ἐμπορίου — The contrast is between oikos tou patros mou (house of my Father — temple as divine dwelling) and oikos emporiou (market house — temple as commercial venue). The possessive patros mou (my Father's) is a Christological claim: Jesus speaks of the temple as his Father's house with a filial authority that positions him above even the legitimate temple authorities.
2.17: ὁ ζῆλος τοῦ οἴκου σου καταφάγεταί με — The Evangelist notes that the disciples remembered Ps 69:9 — but with a significant difference: the LXX of Ps 69:9 reads katephagen (aorist — consumed), whereas the disciples' citation uses the future kataphagetai (will consume). The future tense projects the cost of Jesus's temple zeal forward — anticipating what his action will ultimately cost him. Zēlos (G2205, zeal) connotes passionate, consuming devotion.
2.18: τί σημεῖον δεικνύεις ἡμῖν — The authorities respond to a prophetic action by demanding a legitimating sign. Sēmeion again: the request for a credential-miracle to authorize an extraordinary claim. The irony, developed across the Gospel, is that Jesus has been performing signs, and they will continue to demand signs while rejecting them. Exousian (authority, G1849) is the underlying issue: by what right does he act?
2.19: λύσατε τὸν ναὸν τοῦτον — The verb is aorist imperative: lysate (destroy!). This is not Jesus commanding the temple's destruction but framing a conditional: "If you destroy this temple, I will raise it in three days." The word is naos (G3485) — the inner sanctuary, the most sacred space — not the broader hieron of v.14. Jesus deliberately raises the stakes. Egerō (I will raise, future active) has Jesus as the agent of resurrection — a claim his opponents will not grasp and the disciples will only understand retrospectively (v.22).
2.20: τεσσεράκοντα καὶ ἕξ ἔτεσιν οἰκοδομήθη ὁ ναὸς οὗτος — The 46-year figure is historically useful: Herod's temple renovation began ca. 20/19 BC (Josephus, Ant. 15.380). Forty-six years later places this exchange approximately AD 26–27, consistent with the chronology deduced from Luke 3:1. The authorities' response demonstrates literal misunderstanding — they hear "this temple" as the Herodian building, not a body. Sy egerei — you (emphatic) will raise it?
2.21: ἐκεῖνος δὲ ἔλεγεν περὶ τοῦ ναοῦ τοῦ σώματος αὐτοῦ — The Evangelist inserts his editorial clarification: ekeinos de elegen (but that one was speaking) — a retrospective explanation impossible to the original hearers, possible only from the post-resurrection vantage point. Sōma (G4983) is the word the Evangelist chooses for the body — not sarx (flesh) or anthrōpos — marking it as the physical body that will die and rise.
2.22: ὅτε οὖν ἠγέρθη ἐκ νεκρῶν ἐμνήσθησαν — The resurrection (ēgerthē ek nekrōn) is the interpretive key the disciples lacked during the ministry. Emnēsthēsan (they remembered, aorist): a specific, time-bounded act of retrospective understanding. Episteusan tē graphē kai tō logō hon eipen ho Iēsous — they believed the scripture and the word Jesus had spoken: two objects of faith, both confirmed by the resurrection event.
2.23: ἐπίστευσαν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ θεωροῦντες αὐτοῦ τὰ σημεῖα — The crowd in Jerusalem believes (episteusan eis to onoma — the same formulation as 1:12) on the basis of witnessing signs. The following verses will distinguish this sign-based crowd-faith from the deeper trust that Jesus does not reciprocate.
2.24: αὐτὸς δὲ Ἰησοῦς οὐκ ἐπίστευεν ἑαυτὸν αὐτοῖς — A precise wordplay: the crowd believed in him (episteusan, v.23) but he did not entrust himself (episteuein heauton) to them. The same verb (pisteuō) applies to both: their faith in him and his trust of himself to them. Dia to auton ginōskein pantas — because he himself knew all people: the present infinitive ginōskein marks his ongoing knowledge of human nature as the ground for his caution.
2.25: οὐ χρείαν εἶχεν ἵνα τις μαρτυρήσῃ περὶ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — He had no need of anyone to testify to him about humanity: martyria (testimony) here reverses its usual direction — instead of human witnesses testifying about Jesus, he has no need of human witnesses testifying about humans to him. Autos gar eginōsken ti ēn en tō anthrōpō — for he himself knew what was in the person. The imperfect eginōsken (was knowing, ongoing) marks a characteristic and continuous divine insight into human interiority.

3.1: ἄρχων τῶν Ἰουδαίων — archōn tōn Ioudaiōn, "a ruler of the Jews." Archōn designates a member of the Sanhedrin, the supreme Jewish council. Combined with Φαρισαῖος (Pharisaios), both identifiers mark Nicodemus as an insider of the religious establishment — the opposite of the fishermen disciples.
3.2:
νυκτός (nyktos) — genitive of time, "by night." The Evangelist places this early and does not explain the motive. Whether shame, caution, or a desire for privacy without crowds, the darkness frames a scene that will turn on the contrast between light and dark (3:19–21).

Ῥαββί (Rabbi) — "my great one," a title of respect; Nicodemus addresses Jesus as an equal teacher, not yet as a superior. His opening acknowledgment — "we know you are a teacher come from God" — is accurate but incomplete: the Evangelist has already identified Jesus as far more.
3.3:
ἄνωθεν (anōthen) — the hinge word of the dialogue. The term means both "again" (temporal, a second time) and "from above" (spatial/theological, from heaven). Nicodemus hears the temporal sense in v.4; the Evangelist intends the theological sense throughout. The ambiguity is not a translation problem but a literary device the Evangelist uses to expose the gap between what Jesus says and what a listener embedded in the natural realm can hear.

ἰδεῖν τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ — "to see the kingdom of God." The verb is horaō (aorist infinitive), perception and experience rather than mere observation. The kingdom is not yet entered; it is first seen — a claim that will be sharpened in v.5.
3.4:
δεύτερον (deuteron) — "a second time." Nicodemus has taken ἄνωθεν as "again" (temporal) and pushed it to its most literal extreme: physical re-entry into the womb. His question is not ridicule but genuine incomprehension — he is working with only one semantic register of the word Jesus used.

γέρων ὤν (gerōn ōn) — "being old." The present participle concedes his own situation: the natural impossibility is compounded by age. Nicodemus frames the question in terms of biological limits, which is exactly what Jesus's answer will transcend.
3.5:
ἐξ ὕδατος καὶ πνεύματος — "of water and Spirit." No article before either noun; the Greek pairs them as complementary agents of the new birth, not as two separate acts. The pairing has generated debate: water as physical birth (amniotic fluid), water as baptism, or water as cleansing/purification imagery from Ezek 36:25–27, which pairs water-washing with the gift of Spirit. The Ezekiel background is strong given the rebuke in v.10 ("you are Israel's teacher").

εἰσελθεῖν εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ — "to enter the kingdom of God." The step from "see" (v.3) to "enter" (v.5) is progression: perception precedes participation. The kingdom requires a specific mode of entry.
3.6:
σάρξ (sárx, G4561, dispute level 4) — "flesh." Here σάρξ designates the natural human realm: all that is generated by human origin and human capacity. It is not yet Paul's term for the sin-power (as in Rom 8); in John's usage it denotes creatureliness and finitude. The chiastic structure is deliberate: "born of flesh / is flesh; born of Spirit / is spirit" — the new birth does not repair the old nature but introduces a categorically different origin.

τὸ γεγεννημένον ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος — "that which has been born of the Spirit." The perfect passive participle gegenēmenon marks the completed new birth with ongoing effect: the person so born carries that origin permanently.
3.7:
ὑμᾶς (hymas) — the pronoun shifts to plural: "you [all] must be born again." Jesus has been addressing Nicodemus individually; the plural extends the necessity beyond this one teacher to all. The individual encounter opens into a universal claim.

δεῖ (dei) — "it is necessary." The impersonal verb of divine necessity appears repeatedly in John for what must happen according to God's purpose (cf. 4:4; 10:16; 20:9). The new birth is not optional or aspirational.
3.8:
τὸ πνεῦμα (to pneuma) — the key wordplay: the same Greek word means both "wind" and "Spirit." Jesus is not using an analogy where wind illustrates Spirit; the same word carries both meanings simultaneously. The verb πνεῖ (pnei, "blows") shares the same root as πνεῦμα — "the spirit/wind spirits/blows where it wills." The invisibility of the wind's origin and destination mirrors the Spirit's freedom and inscrutability.

τὴν φωνὴν αὐτοῦ ἀκούεις — "you hear its/his voice." Phōnē properly means "voice" rather than merely "sound." The sentence works for wind (audible gusting) and for the Spirit (the voice that the born-again person hears). The translation settles for one referent; the Greek holds both.
3.9: Nicodemus's response is a bare question: Πῶς (Pōs) — "How?" He offers no objection to the substance of what Jesus says, only incomprehension of the mechanism. The question is honest, not combative. Jesus's reply (v.10) does not answer the "how" but redirects to the authority behind the teaching.
3.10: ὁ διδάσκαλος τοῦ Ἰσραήλ — "the teacher of Israel." Both articles are present: not "a teacher" but the teacher, a recognized authoritative figure in the community. The rebuke is gentle but pointed: someone in this role should recognize the categories Jesus is using — they come from Israel's own prophetic texts (Ezek 36; Jer 31).
3.11: ἡμεῖς / ἡμῶν — "we" / "our." Jesus uses the first-person plural: "we speak of what we know." The shift has puzzled interpreters — does Jesus speak as one voice of a group? Some read the plural as Jesus identifying with the prophetic tradition; others as Jesus speaking as the divine community (Father and Son). The contrast with the plural "you [all]" in the next clause indicates the Evangelist may be addressing the unbelieving audience of his own time through Jesus's words.
3.12: τὰ ἐπίγεια / τὰ ἐπουράνια — "earthly things" / "heavenly things." The contrast is spatial and also epistemological: earthly things that can be grasped by natural analogy (birth, wind) have already exceeded Nicodemus's comprehension. Heavenly things — the descent of the Son, the giving of the Spirit, the mechanics of new birth from above — will be even harder. Jesus names his own restraint in the teaching.
3.13:
ἀναβέβηκεν (anabebēken) — perfect tense: "has ascended." The perfect in Greek denotes a past action with a persisting present state. The claim is paradoxical: the one speaking of heavenly things is the one who has "already ascended" — the human perspective on someone who descended from above is that they carry with them the knowledge only ascent could bring.

ὁ ὢν ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ — "who is/being in heaven." This participial phrase ("the one being in heaven") is absent in some early manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus). If original, it asserts the Son's continuing heavenly reality even while incarnate — a high Christological claim. Its presence or absence does not change the main point but affects how one reads the nature of the Incarnation.
3.14:
ὑψωθῆναι (hypsōthēnai) — the aorist passive infinitive of hypsoō: "to be lifted up." This is the first of three Johannine "lifting up" sayings (cf. 8:28; 12:32). The verb does double work: it refers to the physical elevation of crucifixion and simultaneously to exaltation/glorification. The Evangelist does not separate these — in John, the cross is not a defeat requiring a subsequent resurrection vindication; it is itself the moment of glorification.

καθώς...οὕτως (kathōs...houtōs) — "just as...so." The comparison establishes a structural parallel: the bronze serpent lifted up in the wilderness (Num 21:8–9) provided physical rescue by looking; the Son lifted up provides eternal life by believing. The καθώς introduces the type; the οὕτως the fulfillment.
3.15:
ἵνα πᾶς ὁ πιστεύων ἐν αὐτῷ ἔχῃ ζωὴν αἰώνιον — the prepositional phrase ἐν αὐτῷ ("in him") is syntactically ambiguous: it can modify either "everyone believing in him" or "have life in him." Most translations attach it to the participle (believing in him), but the phrasing in 3:16 — which omits ἐν αὐτῷ — suggests the Evangelist may intend both: belief is directed toward him AND life is located in him.

ζωὴν αἰώνιον (zōēn aiōnion, dispute level 2+3) — "eternal life." Aiōnios (disp=3) derives from aiōn (the age, era); the phrase denotes "the life of the coming age" — qualitative first (the kind of life God has; the life of the new creation), quantitative second (endless). The Evangelist will use this phrase seventeen times; it is never merely longevity.
3.16:
οὕτως (houtōs) — "in this way," not "so much" or "so greatly." The adverb is of manner, not degree: God loved the world in this manner — namely, by giving the Son. The manner of the love is its measure.

ἠγάπησεν (ēgapēsen, aorist) — the giving of the Son is narrated as a single completed historical act, not an ongoing disposition. God did not merely maintain affection; there was a moment of definitive action.

μονογενῆ (monogenē) — "only-born," from monos (only) and gennaō (to beget). The word carries both uniqueness and relational weight: what is given is irreplaceable, and the giving is costly precisely because the relationship being risked is the closest conceivable.

ἵνα μὴ ἀπόληται — purpose clause with the subjunctive of apollymi: "so that he might not perish." Perishing (apollymi) is the alternative state — not punishment freshly imposed, but the destination of those already on the road of judgment (see v.18). The purpose of the giving is rescue from an existing trajectory.
3.17: οὐ γὰρ ἀπέστειλεν...ἵνα κρίνῃ — "for he did not send...in order to judge." The purpose clause with the subjunctive negates the judicial mission. Krinō (to judge/condemn) and sōzō (to save/rescue) are placed in explicit contrast. The world's condemnation is not new — it is the default state (v.18); the mission is to provide a way out of it, not to add to it.
3.18:
κέκριται (kekritai) — perfect passive: "has been judged and remains judged." The judgment on the unbeliever is not a future verdict but a present state — Johannine realized eschatology. Belief un-condemns (ou krinetai, present: "is not being judged"); unbelief leaves the person in an already-existing condition of condemnation.

ὅτι μὴ πεπίστευκεν — "because he has not believed" (perfect indicative): the present state of un-belief is the grounds for the present state of condemnation. The perfect tense marks a settled posture, not a momentary failure.
3.19:
αὕτη δέ ἐστιν ἡ κρίσις — "and this is the verdict." Krisis is "verdict/judgment," not merely "crisis." The Evangelist uses the singular feminine article with the neuter noun φῶς (phōs, light): the verdict is the arrival of Light into the world and the human response to it.

ἠγάπησαν...τὸ σκότος — "they loved darkness." The verb is the same (agapaō) used of God's love in v.16: humans "loved" darkness rather than light. The use of agapē-love for the wrong object makes the indictment precise — it is not ignorance but an orientation of affection.
3.20:
ὁ φαῦλα πράσσων — "everyone who practices evil things." Prassō (to practice/do habitually) contrasts with poieō (to do an act). The one who practices evil has a settled pattern, not just an occasional failure. This habitual orientation is what drives the avoidance of light.

ἐλεγχθῇ (elenkhthē) — "might be exposed/convicted." The word can mean both "to expose" (reveal what was hidden) and "to convict" (demonstrate guilt). The one who avoids the light fears both: disclosure of what they have done AND the verdict that follows disclosure.
3.21:
ὁ δὲ ποιῶν τὴν ἀλήθειαν — "the one doing the truth." "Doing truth" (poiōn tēn alētheian) is a Semitic idiom (Hebrew: ʿāśâ ʾĕmet) meaning to act faithfully, with integrity — not merely asserting propositions but living in alignment with reality. The idiom surfaces in the Dead Sea Scrolls as well as the LXX.

ἵνα φανερωθῇ αὐτοῦ τὰ ἔργα — "that his deeds might be manifested." Phaneroō (to make manifest/visible) is a key Johannine verb for revelation. The one who "does truth" comes to the light not despite their deeds but because they welcome the deeds' disclosure; they have nothing to hide from God's light.
3.22: A narrative transition. μετὰ ταῦτα (meta tauta) — "after these things" — a loose temporal marker the Evangelist uses frequently. The focus shifts from the Nicodemus dialogue to Jesus's ministry in Judaea alongside John's continuing work.
3.23: Αἰνών (Ainōn) and Σαλείμ (Saleim) — exact location unknown; "Aenon" likely derives from the Aramaic for "springs." The geographic notice "because there was much water there" confirms that John's baptism required immersion or at least significant water — a detail with implications for the mode of baptism, though the text does not develop it theologically.
3.24: A parenthetical note: John's ministry is concurrent with Jesus's, not sequentially complete. The Synoptics present John's imprisonment before Jesus's main Galilean ministry (Mark 1:14), which creates a chronological question the Evangelist does not address. The note simply establishes that both are active at the same time.
3.25: ζητήσεως (zētēseōs) — "question/dispute/inquiry." The term can mean a formal disputation or an argument. It arises περὶ καθαρισμοῦ (peri katharismou) — "concerning purification/cleansing." Some manuscripts read "a Jew" (singular) rather than "Jews" (plural) as the other party. The topic — ritual purification — connects John's baptism to Jewish purity concerns and frames the question that John's disciples bring in v.26.
3.26: μαρτυρεῖς (martyreis) — "you testified" (perfect force in context). John's own testimony about Jesus is now being cited against him: the crowds are going to the one John endorsed. The disciples' framing treats this as a problem; John's response in vv.27–30 treats it as completion.
3.27:
οὐ δύναται ἄνθρωπος λαμβάνειν οὐδὲν — "a person cannot receive anything." The statement is universal: every role, gift, and sphere of influence is assigned from heaven. John does not claim he could have more; he asserts that what he has is precisely and completely what he was given.

δεδομένον (dedomenon) — perfect passive participle: "given and remaining given" — the gift from heaven is not provisional but permanent. John cannot exceed the assignment, and he has no desire to.
3.28: οὐκ εἰμί ἐγὼ ὁ Χριστός — "I am not the Messiah." John uses the strong negation to recall his own earlier testimony (1:20). The contrast is sharp: "I am not" (John) vs. the "I am he" of Jesus in 4:26. John defines himself by negation; Jesus by self-disclosure.
3.29:
ὁ φίλος τοῦ νυμφίου — "the friend of the bridegroom." In Jewish custom, the shōshbîn was a designated intermediary who arranged the marriage, carried messages between bride and groom, and at the wedding stood with the bridegroom. His role was complete when the bridegroom arrived. John describes his own ministry in precisely these terms: he arranged the meeting; the bridegroom is here.

χαρᾷ χαίρει (charā chairei) — a cognate dative: "rejoices with joy." The construction is emphatic, idiomatic in Greek for the intensity of an emotion. John's joy at Jesus's ascendancy is not resigned but fully realized. πεπλήρωται (peplērōtai, perfect passive) — "has been filled up/completed" — the joy is already full, not anticipated.
3.30: αὐξάνειν (auxanein, present) / ἐλαττοῦσθαι (elattousthai, present) — both present infinitives of ongoing processes: "he must keep increasing; I must keep decreasing." The present tense captures a continuous trajectory rather than a single moment. The necessity (dei, "must") is the same divine compulsion as in 3:7 and 4:4.
3.31:
ὁ ἄνωθεν ἐρχόμενος — "the one coming from above." Now anōthen (the same word as 3:3) is used explicitly of Jesus's heavenly origin — confirming the Evangelist's intended meaning in the Nicodemus dialogue. The one who comes from above is above all; the one who is from the earth can only speak from within the limits of earth.

ἐπάνω πάντων ἐστίν — "is above all." The phrase appears twice for emphasis. The superiority is ontological, not merely positional.
3.32:
ὃ ἑώρακεν καὶ ἤκουσεν — "what he has seen and heard." The perfect and aorist combine: ongoing seeing (perfect heōraken) plus completed hearing (aorist ēkousen). The testimony of the one from above is grounded in direct personal knowledge, not report or inference.

καὶ τὴν μαρτυρίαν αὐτοῦ οὐδεὶς λαμβάνει — "and no one receives his testimony." The absolute statement will be qualified in v.33 ("whoever has received it"). The "no one" is hyperbolic — the same rhetorical sweep as "all are going to him" in v.26 — expressing the scale of rejection, not its totality.
3.33: ἐσφράγισεν (esphragisen) — "has sealed/certified." To seal something was to authenticate it, confirming its validity by one's own authority. Whoever accepts the testimony of the Son has by that act certified (set their seal to) the truthfulness of God: they have staked their own credibility on God's.
3.34: οὐ γὰρ ἐκ μέτρου δίδωσιν τὸ πνεῦμα — "for not by measure does he give the Spirit." The subject is unstated and debated. In context, "he" most naturally refers to God giving the Spirit to the Son (cf. 1:32–33: "I saw the Spirit descending and remaining on him"). The absence of measure signals completeness — the Spirit is not rationed. Contrast the idea that prophets received the Spirit only in measure or for specific tasks.
3.35: δέδωκεν (dedōken, perfect) — "has given and continues to entrust." The perfect tense marks the Father's delegation as a settled, present reality: the authority is already in the Son's hand and remains there. εἰς τὴν χεῖρα — "into the hand" — a Semitic idiom for authority and stewardship (cf. Ps 8:6 LXX).
3.36:
ἀπειθῶν (apeithōn, present participle) — "the one who disobeys/disbelieves." The word combines a- (not) and peithō (to persuade, to comply). Unbelief in John is not merely intellectual non-assent but active refusal, closer to disobedience than mere uncertainty. The present tense marks it as an ongoing posture.

ἡ ὀργὴ τοῦ θεοῦ μένει ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν — "the wrath of God remains on him." Menō (to remain/abide) is a key Johannine word. The wrath is not newly imposed on the unbeliever; it already rests on humanity apart from Christ. It does not arrive; it stays. This is the same structure as the condemnation in v.18: the default state is under wrath, and faith moves one out of it — not into wrath.

4.1: ὡς οὖν ἔγνω — "when therefore he learned." The aorist egnō (knew/learned) raises the question of how Jesus learned — through normal information channels or divine knowledge. The Evangelist does not specify; the verse functions primarily to explain the transition from Judaea to Galilee via Samaria.
4.2: A parenthetical correction: καίτοιγε Ἰησοῦς αὐτὸς οὐκ ἐβάπτιζεν — "although Jesus himself was not baptizing." The Evangelist corrects the report the Pharisees had received: the disciples baptized, not Jesus personally. The correction preserves the distinction between Jesus's unique baptism (with the Spirit, 1:33) and his disciples' water baptism.
4.3: A simple transitional narrative verse: Jesus leaves Judaea and returns to Galilee. The departure is motivated by the Pharisees' attention (v.1), but the route chosen (v.4) will prove purposeful.
4.4: ἔδει δὲ αὐτὸν διέρχεσθαι διὰ τῆς Σαμαρείας — "it was necessary for him to pass through Samaria." ἔδει (edei) is the imperfect of dei — the impersonal verb of divine necessity. Geographically, a Jew traveling from Judaea to Galilee could route through Peraea east of the Jordan to avoid Samaria. The "necessity" is therefore not geographical but theological: the encounter to come was divinely appointed.
4.5: Συχάρ (Sychar) — the town, possibly Shechem or a nearby village. The note that Jacob gave the plot of land to his son Joseph (Gen 48:21–22; Josh 24:32) establishes the patriarchal depth of the site. The well has been in continuous use for over a millennium; Jesus asks for water at a place saturated with covenant history.
4.6:
κεκοπιακώς (kekopiakōs, perfect participle) — "having labored and remaining weary." The perfect tense preserves the weariness as an ongoing state at the moment Jesus sat down. The Incarnation is depicted without softening: the Word-made-flesh sits exhausted by travel, thirsty, in need of rest.

ὡσεὶ ἕκτη — "about the sixth hour" — approximately noon by Roman reckoning (counting from midnight or dawn). The midday heat explains why no other women are at the well — an observation that makes the Samaritan woman's arrival conspicuous.
4.7: Δός μοι πεῖν — "Give me [something] to drink." A simple imperative, bare and direct. A Jewish man addressing an unknown Samaritan woman in public was a social violation on multiple fronts (gender, ethnicity, purity concerns). Jesus initiates without preamble, which is itself the first thing that arrests the woman's attention.
4.8: A parenthetical explanation removing the disciples from the scene. ἀγοράσωσιν τροφάς — "to buy food." The disciples' absence is practical narrative: it leaves Jesus and the woman alone for the exchange that follows, and it creates the context for v.31–38 when the disciples return.
4.9: Ἰουδαῖος ὤν — "being a Jew." The woman identifies him by appearance, speech, or dress. The narrator's parenthesis clarifies: οὐ γὰρ συγχρῶνται Ἰουδαῖοι Σαμαρίταις — "for Jews do not share/associate with Samaritans." Sygchraomai can mean "use together" (the same vessels) or "associate with" broadly. The purity concern was real: Samaritans were regarded by many Jewish authorities as permanently impure.
4.10:
δωρεά (dōrea) — "free gift." A specific Greek word for an unearned, unmerited grant — it is the same word used in Rom 5:15–17 and Eph 2:8 for the grace-gift of salvation. If she had known what kind of gift was on offer, the direction of the request would have reversed.

ὕδωρ ζῶν (hydōr zōn) — "living water." In contemporary usage, "living water" meant flowing spring water (as opposed to cistern water). The woman hears this natural meaning in v.11. Jesus means the Spirit (7:38–39), which is the "living water" that quenches permanently.
4.11: ἄντλημα (antlēma) — "a vessel for drawing/a bucket." The woman notes the practical obstacle: the well is deep (bathys), Jesus has no equipment, and he is offering living (flowing) water. Her logic is sound on the literal level — the mismatch between the offer and the means is the setup for Jesus's clarification of what kind of water he means.
4.12: μὴ σύ μείζων εἶ — "You are not greater than...are you?" The particle mē expects a negative answer: surely he cannot be greater than Jacob? The irony the Evangelist builds here is dense — Jacob gave this well; Jesus is about to offer something Jacob never could. The woman's question is more accurate than she knows.
4.13: πᾶς ὁ πίνων ἐκ τοῦ ὕδατος τούτου — "everyone who drinks from this water." The present participle pinōn ("drinking") marks ongoing thirst: drinking from this well is an endless cycle. Physical water satisfies temporarily; dipsēsei palin — "will thirst again" — is the inevitable return.
4.14:
ἁλλομένου εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον — "welling up/springing up to eternal life." Hallomai (to spring/leap/well up) is a vivid present participle — the water in the person is not static but actively surging upward. The word is used of the lame man's leaping in Acts 3:8. The image is not of a placid reservoir but a living, mobile force within the person.

ζωὴν αἰώνιον — "eternal life" (disp 2+3). The phrase is now applied to the internal gift: the living water transforms into the life of the age to come within the one who receives it.
4.15: The woman's response is still literal: ἵνα μὴ διψῶ μηδὲ ἔρχωμαι ἐνθάδε ἀντλεῖν — "so that I may not thirst nor come here to draw." She grasps the convenience of the offer — no more daily trips to the well — without yet grasping what is being offered. The misunderstanding is not foolish; it follows naturally from her frame of reference. The Evangelist uses it to continue the dialogue toward deeper disclosure.
4.16: Ὕπαγε φώνησον τὸν ἄνδρα σου — "Go, call your husband." The abrupt command shifts the subject without explanation. Jesus moves from abstract teaching to specific personal knowledge. The imperative is plain; what follows will reveal that Jesus already knows the answer to his own request.
4.17: ἄνδρα οὐκ ἔχω — "I have no husband." A technically accurate statement, as Jesus immediately confirms: καλῶς εἶπες — "you have spoken well/rightly." The MKT renders this affirmation literally. Jesus validates her evasion by exploiting its precise truth to expose what it conceals.
4.18: πέντε γὰρ ἄνδρας ἔσχες — "for you have had five husbands." The aorist eshes covers the past; the present echeis covers the current situation. The present partner (ὃν νῦν ἔχεις) is not her husband. The statement is factual, not condemnatory in tone — Jesus does not rebuke her but simply names reality. Whatever the causes of the five marriages (death, divorce, abandonment), the disclosure establishes that Jesus possesses knowledge he could not naturally have.
4.19: θεωρῶ ὅτι προφήτης εἶ σύ — "I perceive that you are a prophet." Theōreō ("to observe, discern") marks a step in her understanding — she moves from "a Jew" (v.9) to "a prophet" (v.19). The Samaritan tradition expected a prophet like Moses (Deut 18:15–18) called the Taheb. Her next move — the worship question (v.20) — is what one would naturally bring to a prophet.
4.20: ἐν τῷ ὄρει τούτῳ — "on this mountain." Gerizim, the sacred mountain of the Samaritans where they had their own temple until it was destroyed by John Hyrcanus in 128 BC. The dispute between Samaritans and Jews over the correct place of worship (Gerizim vs. Jerusalem) was ancient and bitter. She brings the defining theological question of her community to the one she has identified as a prophet.
4.21: ἔρχεται ὥρα — "the hour is coming." The Johannine eschatological formula. Unlike in v.23 where it is doubled with kai nyn estin ("and now is"), here v.21 states only the future aspect: worship will no longer be location-bound. The worship dispute between Gerizim and Jerusalem will become irrelevant.
4.22: ὑμεῖς προσκυνεῖτε ὃ οὐκ οἴδατε — "you worship what you do not know." The bluntness is deliberate. Samaritan worship used only the Pentateuch and had a truncated knowledge of Israel's history of revelation. ἡμεῖς προσκυνοῦμεν ὃ οἴδαμεν — "we worship what we know." Jesus identifies with the Jewish tradition of revelation: ἡ σωτηρία ἐκ τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἐστίν — "salvation is from the Jews." The definite article before Ioudaiōn makes the claim specific: the history of covenant, Scripture, and Messiah runs through Israel.
4.23:
ἐν πνεύματι καὶ ἀληθείᾳ — "in Spirit and truth." Both nouns are anarthrous (no article), marking them as qualitative — the mode of the new worship. The preposition en indicates the medium or sphere: worship occurs within the Spirit and within truth, not at a location. Alētheia (truth) in John is not a generic virtue but the revelation that Christ himself embodies (14:6: "I am the truth"). Worship in truth is worship aligned with who Christ is.

καὶ γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ τοιούτους ζητεῖ τοὺς προσκυνοῦντας αὐτόν — "for the Father seeks such worshipers." Zēteō (to seek, to look for) applied to the Father is remarkable: the Father is the one actively pursuing the worshipers, not merely the object they must find. The initiative is divine.
4.24:
πνεῦμα ὁ θεός — word order in Greek: "Spirit [is] God." The predicate (pneuma) precedes the subject (ho theos). By Colwell's rule, an anarthrous predicate noun preceding the verb tends to be definite: "God is [the kind of being who is] Spirit." Not "a spirit" among others, but Spirit as the ontological category that defines what God is — without physical form, without location-dependence, not contained by temples built with hands.

This statement grounds the demand for Spirit-and-truth worship: since God himself is Spirit, worship that is geographical, material, or merely external is fundamentally mismatched to the one being worshiped.
4.25: Μεσσίας (Messias) — the transliteration of the Hebrew/Aramaic Māšīaḥ (anointed one); the narrator glosses it as Christos. The Samaritan expectation of a coming figure called the Taheb ("the restorer" or "the returning one") was based on Deut 18:15–18. She reaches for the highest category available to her: the coming one will clarify everything. This sets up Jesus's declaration in v.26 as a direct answer to that expectation.
4.26: ἐγώ εἰμι (egō eimi) — "I am [he]." The explicit "I" (egō) is redundant in Greek (the verb already includes the subject) and therefore emphatic. This is the first time in John that Jesus explicitly identifies himself as the Messiah — and he says it not to the Sanhedrin or his disciples but to a Samaritan woman at a well. The self-disclosure is unguarded and complete: "I, the one speaking to you — I am he." No parable, no riddling language.
4.27: ἐθαύμαζον (ethaumazon, imperfect) — "they were marveling/kept marveling." The disciples' surprise is ongoing. μετὰ γυναικός — "with a woman." The prohibition in rabbinic sources was against prolonged conversation with women in public, particularly unknown women. The disciples' silence (oudeis asked) is itself noteworthy — they perceived that a challenge to Jesus would be out of place.
4.28: ἀφῆκεν τὴν ὑδρίαν αὐτῆς — "she left her water jar." The detail is small and vivid: she came to draw water and leaves without it. The jar represents the purpose she came with; she departs with something else entirely. The practical forgetfulness signals that she is now occupied with something more urgent than the errand she came for.
4.29:
ὃς εἶπέν μοι πάντα ἃ ἐποίησα — "who told me everything I ever did." The claim is rhetorically shaped for maximum effect — "everything I did" is the village's way of hearing what was actually a targeted disclosure about her marriages. She reports the encounter in terms that will be maximally compelling to others.

μήτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ Χριστός; — "Could this be the Messiah?" The particle mēti introduces a question that expects a hesitant or negative answer, but invites the listener to consider the positive. She does not proclaim; she raises the question and lets the townspeople investigate. Her testimony is framed as a possibility, not a certainty — honest to her own incomplete understanding.
4.30: A simple narrative verse. ἐξῆλθον (aorist, "they went out") / ἤρχοντο (imperfect, "they were coming") — the sequence shows the townspeople responding immediately and approaching. The imperfect of continuous movement sets the scene for the harvest imagery in vv.35–38: the fields (of people) are already moving toward Jesus.
4.31: ἠρώτων (ērōtōn, imperfect) — "kept asking/urging." The disciples' persistent request ("Rabbi, eat") sets up the discourse on a different kind of food (vv.32–38), just as the woman's misunderstanding of water set up the discourse on living water.
4.32: βρῶσιν φαγεῖν ἣν ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε — "food to eat that you do not know about." Brōsis is a general term for food/eating. Jesus does not explain immediately, allowing the disciples' confusion in v.33 to frame the explanation in v.34. The pattern mirrors the misunderstandings with Nicodemus and the Samaritan woman: natural category → literal misunderstanding → theological disclosure.
4.33: The disciples's question to one another — "could someone have brought him food?" — is not stupid; it is the only natural reading of what Jesus said. The Evangelist uses their misunderstanding as a foil to make Jesus's answer in v.34 stand out as the correction.
4.34:
ἐμὸν βρῶμά ἐστιν ἵνα ποιήσω τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πέμψαντός με — "my food is to do the will of the one who sent me." Doing the Father's will is what sustains Jesus — the metaphor of food for what nourishes and energizes.

τελειώσω αὐτοῦ τὸ ἔργον — "and to complete his work." Teleioō (to complete, perfect, bring to its end) anticipates the cross cry of 19:30: τετέλεσται ("it is finished/completed"). The work the Father sent him to do is not yet complete at Sychar — but the verb points toward its ultimate fulfillment.
4.35:
λευκαί εἰσιν πρὸς θερισμόν — "they are white/ripe for harvest." The present tense: the harvest is already here. The proverb ("four months until harvest") belongs to the natural agricultural cycle; Jesus inverts the timeline. As the Samaritans approach across the fields (visible to the disciples), they are the illustration: the mission-harvest does not wait for a future season.

ἐπάρατε τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς ὑμῶν — "lift up your eyes." The aorist imperative demands immediate, decisive looking. Physical sight of what is coming (the Samaritans) corresponds to spiritual perception of what the approach means.
4.36: καρπὸν εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον — "fruit for eternal life." The harvest metaphor shifts from grain to the produce of mission: people gathered into the life of the age to come. The sower and reaper rejoicing together (hina...chairē) anticipates the unity of mission across generations.
4.37: ὁ λόγος ἐστὶν ἀληθινός — "the saying is true." Here logos (disp=3) functions as "proverb" or "saying" — existing wisdom being quoted and affirmed. The word is not the divine Logos of 1:1 but the human category of received wisdom. The proverb ("one sows and another reaps") is being applied to the mission context: those who prepared Israel received no harvest; those sent now will reap from prior labor.
4.38: κεκοπιάκασιν (kekopiākasin, perfect) — "have labored [and their labor remains]." The perfect tense preserves the prior workers' effort as an ongoing legacy. Who are the others? The prophets of Israel, John the Baptist, perhaps Jesus himself in the preceding Samaritan encounter. The disciples inherit fruit they did not plant.
4.39: διὰ τὸν λόγον τῆς γυναικός — "because of the word/testimony of the woman." Logos here is the woman's spoken report — her word generated the faith of many. The progression from her testimony (v.39) to Jesus's own word (v.41) mirrors the progression from human witness to direct encounter that the Evangelist traces throughout John (cf. 1:37–42).
4.40: ἔμεινεν ἐκεῖ δύο ἡμέρας — "he remained there two days." Menō (to remain/abide) is a key Johannine word for dwelling relationship. The two days is concrete and narrated; the result is a deeper, broader faith in the community.
4.41: διὰ τὸν λόγον αὐτοῦ — "because of his word." The shift from the woman's logos (v.39) to Jesus's own logos is explicit. Both are "word," but the effect of the second is greater: "many more believed." Witness testimony leads to encounter; encounter deepens belief.
4.42:
σωτὴρ τοῦ κόσμου — "Savior of the world." The genitive tou kosmou ("of the world") marks universal scope, not just Samaritan or Jewish scope. This is the climax of the pericope: the Samaritans, excluded from the covenant people, name Jesus with the most universal title used of him in any Gospel narrative. Sōtēr was also used of Roman emperors and benefactor deities — the Evangelist places it on the lips of Samaritans for a Jewish-Samaritan figure from Galilee.

οὐκέτι διὰ τὴν σὴν λαλιάν — "no longer because of your word." The Samaritans distinguish between the woman's testimony (the trigger) and their own direct hearing (the foundation). They do not dismiss her report but they have moved beyond it to personal encounter.
4.43: A transition: two days complete, Jesus departs for Galilee. The note is brief, preparing for the Galilean reception and the second sign.
4.44: αὐτὸς γὰρ Ἰησοῦς ἐμαρτύρησεν — "for Jesus himself testified." The Evangelist cites a known saying of Jesus about a prophet having no honor in his own country (patris, homeland). The irony of the saying here is complex: Jesus goes to Galilee, is welcomed by Galileans, and yet the saying suggests his own people will not truly honor him — the welcome is framed in terms of having seen signs (v.45), not the kind of faith Jesus seeks.
4.45: ἑωρακότες πάντα ἃ ἐποίησεν ἐν Ἱεροσολύμοις — "having seen all that he did in Jerusalem." The Galileans' welcome is sign-based: they were at the Passover and saw the works. The Evangelist notes this without endorsing it as the deepest faith; the royal official in vv.46–54 will model the better kind (belief from the word alone).
4.46: βασιλικός (basilikos) — "royal [official]" — someone in the service of Herod Antipas or another Herodian ruler. The designation is social and political. His son is at Capernaum, approximately 25 miles from Cana. He has traveled a significant distance based on a report that Jesus had returned to Galilee from Judaea.
4.47: ἤμελλεν γὰρ ἀποθνῄσκειν — "for he was about to die." The imperfect ēmellen (was on the point of) with the present infinitive marks imminent death. The father's urgency is understandable: "come down before my child dies" (v.49) — before the final threshold is crossed.
4.48: σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα (sēmeia kai terata) — "signs and wonders." The pairing is common in Jewish and Hellenistic literature for miraculous acts. Jesus's challenge is addressed to the plural "you" — the Galilean crowd (cf. v.45) who seek sign-based faith. The rebuke is not to the official personally but to the crowd whose faith requires visible displays before belief is possible.
4.49: κύριε, κατάβηθι πρὶν ἀποθανεῖν τὸ παιδίον μου — "Sir/Lord, come down before my child dies." Kyrie is formal address; whether the official intends the full confessional weight of "Lord" is unclear. His request is for physical presence — he has not yet grasped that Jesus's word alone is sufficient. The narrative withholds the healing until the word alone is all he has.
4.50: ἐπίστευσεν ὁ ἄνθρωπος τῷ λόγῳ ὃν εἶπεν αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς — "the man believed the word which Jesus spoke to him." The dative tō logō identifies the content of his faith: not a sign, not a presence, not a performance — just the word Jesus spoke. This is the faith that does not require sight (cf. 20:29). He turned and went, on the strength of a sentence.
4.51: ἤδη δὲ αὐτοῦ καταβαίνοντος — "while he was still going down." The genitive absolute construction marks the timing: the servants meet him en route, before he arrives home. The healing was complete before he saw it. The narrative structure preserves the priority of the word over the sight.
4.52: ἐπύθετο οὖν τὴν ὥραν παρ᾽ αὐτῶν — "he inquired therefore from them the hour." The official investigates the timing. ἕβδομος (hebdomos, seventh hour) — approximately 1 PM in Roman reckoning. The coincidence is not approximate — it is exact. The father will recognize this in v.53.
4.53: ἔγνω οὖν ὁ πατήρ ὅτι ἐκείνῃ τῇ ὥρᾳ — "the father therefore knew that at that very hour." The moment of recognition: the word and the healing were simultaneous. The result is household-wide faith: ἐπίστευσεν αὐτὸς καὶ ἡ οἰκία αὐτοῦ ὅλη — "he believed, and his whole household." The aorist episteusen marks the point of decisive belief; the scope extends beyond the individual.
4.54: δεύτερον σημεῖον (deuteron sēmeion) — "second sign." The Evangelist counts the signs deliberately: the first sign was the water-to-wine at Cana (2:11); the second is this long-distance healing. Both signs occur in Galilee, bracket the Jerusalem-Samaria section of chapters 2–4, and both involve an initial challenge to the mode of faith being sought. The counting is a structural signal, not just a chronological note.

6.1: Μετὰ ταῦτα — the Evangelist's characteristic connective (meta tauta, after these things) marks an indefinite time-gap. Unlike the Synoptic Gospels, which sequence the feeding directly after other events, John leaves the interval undefined. The Galilean Sea is identified by both its common name and the newer Roman designation "Sea of Tiberias" — a parenthetical clarification for non-Palestinian readers that functions as a geographic authenticating detail.
6.2: ὄχλος πολύς — "great crowd" is the Evangelist's term for the mass of Galilean followers who are not yet disciples in any committed sense. Their motive is stated explicitly: oti heōrōn ta sēmeia — because they were watching the signs he was doing on the sick. The imperfect heōrōn (were watching, ongoing) indicates sustained spectator attention rather than faith-response; this crowd follows because of what they see, not because they understand what it signifies.
6.3: ἀνῆλθεν εἰς τὸ ὄρος Ἰησοῦς — the verb anabainō (to go up) appears throughout John with both literal and symbolic weight. The mountain here echoes the Sinai tradition: Moses received the Law on a mountain; the Bread of Life discourse will explicitly contrast the manna Moses mediated with the bread the Father gives. The Evangelist does not develop the mountain typology explicitly, but the spatial positioning is deliberate.
6.4: ἦν δὲ ἐγγὺς τὸ πάσχα — the Evangelist timestamps the chapter at Passover proximity. This is John's second Passover in the Gospel (cf. 2:13; a third will come at ch.12–19). The Passover setting activates a set of associations: the Exodus feeding narrative, the Passover lamb, the bread eaten in haste. The discourse that follows will engage the manna tradition directly.
6.5: πόθεν ἀγοράσωμεν ἄρτους — the deliberative subjunctive agorasōmen (shall we buy?) treats the feeding problem as a purchasing question. Agorazō (G59) means to market-purchase; the word choice grounds the problem in ordinary commerce. The question is addressed to Philip specifically, though all the disciples are present — perhaps because Philip was from nearby Bethsaida (1:44) and would know the local supply chains.
6.6: τοῦτο δὲ ἔλεγεν πειράζων αὐτόν — the Evangelist's parenthetical clarification is unusual: he breaks the narrative to explain Jesus's interior motive. Peirazō (G3985) means to test or put to proof, not to tempt toward sin; the word carries the sense of pedagogical examination. Jesus already knew what he would do (ēdei gar ti emellen poiein) — the question to Philip was diagnostic, not information-seeking. This is the Evangelist's way of marking miraculous episodes as planned revelatory acts, not improvised responses.
6.7: διακοσίων δηναρίων ἄρτοι — 200 denarii was approximately 200 days' wages for a day laborer. Philip's calculation is not pessimistic exaggeration; it is realistic arithmetic. The detail establishes that the problem is humanly insoluble — the crowd cannot be fed by normal means. This impossibility is the precondition for the sign.
6.8: Ἀνδρέας ὁ ἀδελφὸς Σίμωνος Πέτρου — Andrew is consistently identified in the Fourth Gospel in relation to his brother Peter (cf. 1:40). His contribution to the dialogue is a resource-count that also concludes its own insufficiency: "but what are these among so many?" (alla tauta ti estin eis tosoutous). The adversative alla signals that Andrew himself knows the answer is inadequate.
6.9:
παιδάριον ἓν ὃ ἔχει κριθίνους ἄρτους πέντε — the barley loaves (krithinoi artoi) are significant: barley bread was the food of the poor, a fraction the cost of wheat bread. The Elisha parallel (2 Kgs 4:42–44) is exact — a man brought barley loaves to Elisha; Elisha fed a hundred with twenty loaves and had left over. John does not cite the connection, but the coincidence of grain type, the feeding of a crowd, and the remainder all mark the allusion for readers of the LXX.

καὶ δύο ὀψάρια — opsarion (G3795, diminutive) means small fish or fish-relish — the kind of salted fish used as condiment with bread, not a substantial portion. The emphasis is on inadequacy: what a small boy carries for a meal cannot feed five thousand.
6.10: ἀνέπεσαν οὖν οἱ ἄνδρες — anepeson (reclined, aorist of anapiptō) is the symposium or formal-meal posture, used throughout the Gospels for dining. Andres (men, males) is specifically enumerated at five thousand — a detail that survives in all four Gospels. Women and children, if present (Matt 14:21), are not counted. The grass detail (ēn de chortos polys) serves as another authenticating eyewitness note and may recall Ps 23:2 ("He makes me lie down in green pastures").
6.11: λαβὼν οὖν τοὺς ἄρτους ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ εὐχαριστήσας — the verb eucharistēsas (aorist participle of eucharisteō, G2168) — "having given thanks" — is the root of the later Christian term "Eucharist." The giving-of-thanks formula corresponds to the Jewish berakhah over bread. John uses eucharisteō here (and at v.23 when he mentions "the place where they ate bread after the Lord had given thanks"); the Synoptics use both eucharisteō and eulogeō (blessed). διένειμεν — he distributed; the imperfect dietithou in Luke marks the ongoing distribution; John's aorist takes it as a completed act. Hosou ēthelon — as much as they wished: the supply was not rationed.
6.12: συναγάγετε τὰ περισσεύσαντα κλάσματα — the command to gather (synagagete, aorist imperative) and the reason given — hina mē ti apōlētai (that nothing may be lost) — is notable. The verb apollymi (perish, be lost) is the same word used in 3:16 ("should not perish") and 6:39 ("I should lose none"). The Evangelist may be planting a lexical connection: Jesus loses none of the bread given to him, just as he loses none of those the Father gives him.
