6.13: δώδεκα κοφίνους κλασμάτων — twelve baskets (kophinos = a large wicker basket, the type associated with Jewish travelers in the ancient world) filled with fragments from five barley loaves. The number twelve exceeds the starting supply by multiples. Whether the twelve baskets correspond symbolically to the twelve tribes of Israel or the twelve disciples, the Evangelist does not say; the number stands as numeric surplus impossible to attribute to waste recovery.
6.14: οὗτός ἐστιν ἀληθῶς ὁ προφήτης ὁ ἐρχόμενος εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "the Prophet" (ho prophētēs with the definite article) refers to the Mosaic prophet of Deut 18:15–18, whom Israel expected at the end of days ("the LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me"). The Evangelist has John the Baptist deny this identity at 1:21 ("Are you the Prophet? He said, No"). Now the crowd assigns it to Jesus on the basis of the feeding sign — Moses fed Israel in the wilderness with manna; this man has fed multitudes with bread. The sign is correctly read as prophetic; the conclusion is not entirely wrong, only insufficient.
6.15: ἁρπάζειν αὐτὸν ἵνα ποιήσωσιν βασιλέα — harpazein (G726, to seize, snatch, take by force) is a violent word — used for robbery, abduction, and forcible seizure. The crowd's intent is to install Jesus as king by force (harpazein... poiēsōsin basilea). Jesus perceives this (gnous, having known) and withdraws. The response shows the gulf between the crowd's messianic category (nationalist king who solves food scarcity) and what Jesus offers. ἀνεχώρησεν πάλιν εἰς τὸ ὄρος — he withdrew again to the mountain; autos monos (himself alone) emphasizes solitary retreat from the crowd's pressure.
6.16: Ὡς δὲ ὀψία ἐγένετο — the temporal marker "when evening came" (opsia, late afternoon) signals the disciples' descent to the sea without Jesus. The two movements in this section — crowd below, Jesus on the mountain alone, disciples on the sea in darkness — set the spatial and moral contrasts for the epiphany that follows.
6.17: σκοτία ἤδη ἐγεγόνει — "darkness had already come" — the pluperfect egegonei marks the darkness as an established condition at the time of the disciples' embarkation. The Evangelist's use of skotia (G4653, darkness) is rarely incidental; throughout the Gospel the term carries the register of spiritual opposition to the light (1:5; 3:19; 12:35). Here the physical darkness of night is the setting, but the language activates the broader associations.
6.18: ἥ τε θάλασσα ἀνέμου μεγάλου πνέοντος διεγείρετο — the sea was being aroused (diegeireto, imperfect passive) by a strong wind blowing (anemou megalou pneontos, genitive absolute). The passive suggests an external force agitating the water. Pneuma (G4151, wind/spirit, dl=3) in its literal register here; but the Evangelist's pneuma vocabulary is wide. Nothing in the grammar signals double meaning, but readers of the prologue will note the term.
6.19: σταδίους εἴκοσι πέντε ἢ τριάκοντα — 25 to 30 stadia is approximately 3–4 miles, which corresponds to the midpoint of the crossing, consistent with Josephus's estimate of the lake's width at 40 stadia. The imprecision ("25 or 30") reads as eyewitness memory rather than schematic number. θεωροῦσιν τὸν Ἰησοῦν περιπατοῦντα ἐπὶ τῆς θαλάσσης — theōrousin (present: they are seeing, ongoing perception) and peripatōn (walking, present participle: continuous walking on the sea). The terror (ephobēthēsan, they were frightened) is stated without elaboration.
6.20: ἐγώ εἰμι· μὴ φοβεῖσθε — the bare egō eimi without predicate (I AM; not "it is I" in the Greek) resonates with the Septuagintal rendering of the divine name in Isa 43:10, 25; 51:12; Exod 3:14. The syntactic form can be read as simple self-identification ("It is I") — which is how the disciples receive it — but the Evangelist deploys the phrase at crisis moments where divine identity is at stake. The attached command mē phobeisthe (stop being afraid, present imperative of prohibition) echoes the theophany formula: "Fear not, I AM" appears in divine-appearance contexts throughout the LXX (Gen 15:1; 26:24; Isa 41:10).
6.21: εὐθέως ἐγένετο τὸ πλοῖον ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς — "immediately the boat was at the land" (eutheōs egeneto). This compression — the boat instantly arrives at its destination — is handled as a matter of fact without elaboration. Whether it is a second miracle (instantaneous transport) or a compressed narrative description of a rapid completion of the voyage is left unresolved. The Evangelist does not signal which, and the Greek permits either reading.
6.22: The extended narrative of vv.22–24 tracks the crowd's realization that Jesus has left: only one boat was there, Jesus did not board it, yet he is gone. The logical impossibility leads them across the lake to Capernaum. The detail that other boats came from Tiberias (ek Tiberiados ploia) is incidental authentication of how the crowd was able to follow.
6.23: ἄλλα ἦλθεν πλοιάρια ἐκ Τιβεριάδος — a parenthetical geographic note. Ploiaria (diminutive, small boats) from Tiberias — the new Herodian city on the western shore — arrived near the feeding site. The parenthetical phrase "near the place where they ate the bread after the Lord had given thanks" reuses eucharistēsantos tou Kyriou — the Evangelist twice uses the eucharist-vocabulary in connection with the feeding (vv.11 and 23).
6.24: The crowd's search for Jesus is driven by the same motive as their initial following (v.2): sign-and-bread interest. The Evangelist uses zētountes ton Iēsoun (seeking Jesus) — the verb zēteō in John often marks problematic searching, motivated by self-interest or hostile intent, in contrast to the coming (erchomai) that characterizes genuine belief.
6.25: Ῥαββί, πότε ὧδε γέγονας — "Rabbi, when did you get here?" The perfect gegonas (have you come to be/arrived) treats the question as about his achieved presence. They address him as Rabbi (teacher), the appropriate honorific for a miracle-worker in their understanding. Jesus does not answer the question — he addresses the motive beneath it.
6.26: ζητεῖτέ με οὐχ ὅτι εἴδετε σημεῖα — "you seek me not because you saw signs." The aorist eidete (you saw) is contrasted with what they actually experienced: hoti ephagete ek tōn artōn — "because you ate from the loaves." The Evangelist's critique is precise: they saw the sign-event but did not perceive it as a sign pointing beyond itself. Sēmeion (G4592) in John always means a significant act that reveals something about the actor and calls for faith-response; they experienced only the bread. Echortasthēte — you were satisfied/filled to capacity (from chortazō, to feed until full, used also of animals grazing).
6.27:
ἐργάζεσθε μὴ τὴν βρῶσιν τὴν ἀπολλυμένην — ergazesthe (work, present imperative) picks up the labor vocabulary. Brōsis (G1035, food/the act of eating) rather than artos (bread); the term is broader. Apollymenēn (present participle: perishing, being destroyed) — the same root as apollymi in 3:16. Two categories of food are named: perishable food and food that endures (menousan) to eternal life.

αἰώνιον (G166, aiōnios, dl=3) — rendered "eternal" throughout this chapter. The dispute is whether the word is primarily temporal (everlasting, without end) or qualitative (belonging to the age to come, eschatological life of a different order). The Johannine context supports both: eternal life is the life that does not end, but it begins now (6:47: "the one who believes has eternal life," present tense). σφραγίζω — "the Father has sealed him" (sfragizō, aorist indicative): to set a seal on is to authenticate, authorize, confirm as genuine. God's seal on the Son is the divine endorsement of his mission and identity.
6.28: τί ποιῶμεν ἵνα ἐργαζώμεθα τὰ ἔργα τοῦ θεοῦ — the crowd's question rephrases Jesus's exhortation using the plural erga (works, deeds). They hear a program of religious activity. The plural is important: they imagine a list of qualifying actions. Jesus will answer with the singular.
6.29: τοῦτό ἐστιν τὸ ἔργον τοῦ θεοῦ — "this is the work of God" — singular ergon. The plural request (works) is met with a single work. Hina pisteuēte eis hon apesteilen ekeinos — "that you believe in the one he has sent." Pisteuō eis (believe into/toward) is the directional construction John uses for salvific faith — not merely belief-that but trust directed toward a person. All religious doing is sublimated into one posture: believing into the Sent One.
6.30: τί οὖν ποιεῖς σὺ σημεῖον — the crowd demands a sign to justify belief, despite having just witnessed the feeding. The question reveals their interpretive framework: one spectacular feat is not enough; Moses gave sustained daily manna for forty years. Their implicit calculus is that Jesus's credentials require matching or exceeding Moses's sign. The irony is that they are asking for a sign while standing in the aftermath of one.
6.31: τοὺς πατέρας ἡμῶν τὸ μάννα ἔφαγον ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ — the citation ("He gave them bread from heaven to eat") blends Ps 78:24 and Exod 16:4. The crowd ascribes the giving to Moses: kathōs estin gegrammeron — "as it is written." Jesus will correct this in v.32: Moses did not give; the Father gives. The Exodus manna tradition serves as the paradigm the crowd applies to evaluate Jesus, and which Jesus will reframe and surpass.
6.32: οὐ Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὸν ἄρτον ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — the emphatic negation corrects both the subject and the tense. (1) Subject: not Moses but my Father (ho patēr mou) gives. Moses was instrument; the Father was giver — even for the manna. (2) Tense: edōken (he gave, aorist — past, the crowd's framing) vs. didōsin (he gives, present — ongoing now). The shift from aorist to present is the exegetical move: the true bread from heaven is not a historical provision that ended; it is a present gift.
6.33: ὁ γὰρ ἄρτος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν ὁ καταβαίνων ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — "the bread of God is the one coming down from heaven." The present participle katabainōn (coming down, ongoing descent) identifies the bread as a person — "and giving life to the world" (zōēn didous tō kosmō). Kosmos (the world) marks the scope of the life-giving: not Israel alone, not the wilderness generation, but the entire human world. This is the bread that surpasses manna.
6.34: κύριε, πάντοτε δὸς ἡμῖν τὸν ἄρτον τοῦτον — the request parallels the Samaritan woman's request at 4:15: "Sir, give me this water always." Both speakers understand the referent literally and want a permanent solution to physical need. The irony in both cases is that they are requesting the very person they are addressing, without realizing it. Pantote (always) intensifies the request: a sustained supply, like the manna that fell every morning.
6.35: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος τῆς ζωῆς — the first fully articulated I AM with predicate in this chapter. The double construction: ho erchomenos pros me ou mē peinasē (the one coming to me will certainly not hunger — double negative ou mē with aorist subjunctive, emphatic futurity) and ho pisteuōn eis eme ou mē dipsēsei pōpote (the one believing in me will certainly never thirst). Coming and believing are presented as parallel acts: both are directional orientation toward Jesus. The hunger/thirst imagery recasts physical appetite as the proper image of spiritual need — not a metaphor chosen arbitrarily but one that activates the entire discourse about bread that began with the feeding miracle.
6.36: ἀλλ᾽ εἶπον ὑμῖν ὅτι καὶ ἑωράκατέ με καὶ οὐ πιστεύετε — "you have seen me and yet you do not believe." The perfect heōrakate (you have seen and the result stands) followed by the present ou pisteuete (you are not believing). The contrast between seeing and believing runs through the Gospel (cf. 20:29). The crowd has seen both Jesus and his signs; their unbelief is therefore not due to ignorance of evidence but to something else — which Jesus explains in vv.37–44.
6.37: πᾶν ὃ δίδωσίν μοι ὁ πατὴρ πρὸς ἐμὲ ἥξει — "all that the Father gives me will come to me." The pronoun pan ho (all that) is neuter singular — a collective construction treating those given as a unified whole (contrast pantes hoi, all the ones). The coming is stated as certainty: hēxei (future indicative, will come). The following clause balances this with an equally certain reception: ton erchomenon pros me ou mē ekbalō exō — "the one coming to me I will certainly not drive out" (double negative again, aorist subjunctive). Divine initiative in giving and human act of coming are held together without resolving their relationship.
6.38: ὅτι καταβέβηκα ἀπὸ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — the perfect katabebēka (I have come down and the result stands, I am here as one who has come down). The descent is a completed event with present consequence. The purpose stated is double: ouch hina poiō to thelēma to emon (not to do my own will) — alla to thelēma tou pempsantos me (but the will of the one who sent me). The mission of the Son is defined by the Sender's will, not independent agenda.
6.39: ἵνα πᾶν ὃ δέδωκέν μοι μὴ ἀπολέσω ἐξ αὐτοῦ — "that of all he has given me I should lose none." The verb apollymi (lose, destroy) reappears: the Father's will is that the Son lose nothing of what the Father has given. Anastēsō autō en tē eschatē hēmera — "I will raise it up at the last day" (eschatē hēmera). This is the first of four appearances of "the last day" in this chapter (vv.39,40,44,54). The phrase is Johannine — John uses it for the day of resurrection and judgment where the other Gospel writers use "that day" or "the age to come." The future tense resurrection-promise is the guarantee of non-loss.
6.40: πᾶς ὁ θεωρῶν τὸν υἱόν — theōrōn (present participle of theōreō, to behold, observe attentively) is stronger than mere seeing (horaō); it involves sustained, contemplative attention. Kai pisteuōn eis auton — and believing in him (the second present participle, simultaneous action). The two present participles describe the continuous posture of the believer: ongoing beholding and ongoing believing. The consequence: echē zōēn aiōnion (has eternal life, present tense — already in possession) and anastēsō auton egō tē eschatē hēmera (I myself will raise him up at the last day, emphatic egō).
6.41: ἐγόγγυζον οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι περὶ αὐτοῦ — egongyzon (imperfect of gongyzon) is the LXX term for Israel's wilderness murmuring — used in Exod 16 and Num 11 for Israel's complaints against Moses and God over food. The Evangelist's choice of this word activates the typological parallel deliberately: the crowd who wanted more bread (v.34) now grumbles, just as Israel grumbled for bread in the wilderness. The irony is sharp — those who are supposed to receive the bread from heaven are murmuring against the one who is the bread from heaven.
6.42: οὐχ οὗτός ἐστιν Ἰησοῦς ὁ υἱὸς Ἰωσήφ — the objection is genealogical: they know his father and mother. The familiarity functions as a disqualification: how can a man whose parents are known claim to have come down from heaven? This is the scandal of the Incarnation stated from the outside. The Evangelist does not answer the objection directly — Jesus responds by redirecting attention from origin to invitation and the Father's drawing activity.
6.43: μὴ γογγύζετε μετ᾽ ἀλλήλων — "stop murmuring among yourselves" (present imperative of prohibition = stop an action in progress). The command uses the same gongyzo word (now the imperative) and by doing so explicitly identifies their behavior as the wilderness-murmuring pattern. The reproach is implicit: they are acting like Israel in the desert, who was judged for that murmuring.
6.44: οὐδεὶς δύναται ἐλθεῖν πρός με ἐὰν μὴ ὁ πατὴρ ὁ πέμψας με ἑλκύσῃ αὐτόν — "No one is able to come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him." Helkysē (aorist subjunctive of helkō, G1670) means to draw or drag — the word is used literally in 21:6,11 for dragging fish nets, and in 12:32 where Jesus says "when I am lifted up I will draw all to myself." The force of helkō is more than mere invitation; it carries the idea of active, effective pulling. This verse grounds the failure to believe (v.36) and the certainty of those who do come (v.37) in the Father's initiative. The theological weight of the verse is sustained; the Evangelist does not soften it.
6.45: ἔστιν γεγραμμένον ἐν τοῖς προφήταις — "It stands written in the Prophets" (esti gegramenon, perfect passive — a fixed, standing text). The citation is from Isa 54:13 LXX: kai esontai pantes didaktoi theou (they shall all be taught by God). Jesus applies a restoration promise made to future Israel to the present gathering of disciples. πᾶς ὁ ἀκούσας παρὰ τοῦ πατρός — "everyone who has heard from the Father and learned" comes to Jesus. The sequence matters: hearing is prior to coming; the Father's instruction is what issues in movement toward the Son.
6.46: οὐχ ὅτι τὸν πατέρα ἑώρακέν τις εἰ μὴ ὁ ὢν παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ — v.45 might suggest the crowd has equal access to the Father; v.46 corrects this. No one has seen the Father — the perfect heōraken (has seen, ever seen) rules it out universally — except the one who is from God. Ho ōn para tou theou (the one who is from God, present participle of being) uses the same ōn that appeared in 1:18 ("the one who is in the bosom of the Father"). Only the Son is in that position of vision; all teaching about the Father that reaches humans comes through him.
6.47: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν — the double amen formula is unique to the Fourth Gospel (the Synoptics use single amen). It functions as an authoritative preamble: "I tell you with solemn guarantee." ὁ πιστεύων ἔχει ζωὴν αἰώνιον — "the one who believes has eternal life." The present tense echei (has, possesses now) is emphatic. This is John's characteristic realized eschatology: eternal life is not merely future but a present possession of the believer. The tense cannot be collapsed into future expectation without distorting the grammar.
6.48: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος τῆς ζωῆς — the I AM declaration from v.35 is repeated now at the center of the discourse, after the theological exposition of vv.36–47. The repetition is not redundant; it functions as a refrain that anchors the doctrinal argument. The discourse began with a crowd wanting bread; it arrives here at the identification of Jesus himself as that bread. What they requested at v.34 (give us this bread always) he now re-identifies as himself.
6.49: οἱ πατέρες ὑμῶν ἔφαγον ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ τὸ μάννα καὶ ἀπέθανον — "your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness and died." This is the fourth sustained reference to the manna narrative in the discourse (after vv.31, 32, 38, 49). The datum is stated bluntly: even those who ate the miraculous divine provision died. The manna was a sign of grace but not the source of life that transcends physical death. The contrast with v.50 is sharpened by this acknowledgment of manna's limitation.
6.50: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καταβαίνων ἵνα τις ἐξ αὐτοῦ φάγῃ καὶ μὴ ἀποθάνῃ — the purpose clause hina tis... phagē kai mē apothanē (that one may eat and not die) states what the manna could not provide. Phagē is aorist subjunctive of phagō (to eat), the ordinary word; the shift to trōgō (more physically specific) comes in v.54. The comparison with v.49 is direct: those who ate manna died; those who eat this bread will not die.
6.51: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ζῶν ὁ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καταβάς — the third I AM of the chapter, now with the expanded predicate: not "the bread of life" but "the living bread" (ho artos ho zōn, the bread that is itself alive, living). The participle zōn (living, present) distinguishes this from merely bread that produces life; this bread has life in itself. ἡ σάρξ μου ἐστίν — "this bread is my flesh." Sarx (G4561, dl=4) here denotes the full physical human body — the flesh in which the incarnate Word is present — not sinful human nature. The word choice is as maximal as possible: not body (sōma), not self (psychē), but flesh — the most concrete, physical designation. Hyper tēs tou kosmou zōēs — "for the life of the world": the giving is substitutionary in direction (for, on behalf of) and universal in scope (the world).
6.52: ἐμάχοντο οὖν πρὸς ἀλλήλους — emachonto (imperfect of machomai, to fight, quarrel, strive) indicates an ongoing heated dispute, not a momentary confusion. The argument is conducted within the crowd: "How can this man give us his flesh to eat?" The question takes the claim literally and finds it offensive, even abhorrent. Jesus does not clarify that he means something metaphorical — he intensifies the claim in v.53.
6.53: ἐὰν μὴ φάγητε τὴν σάρκα τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου καὶ πίητε αὐτοῦ τὸ αἷμα — the condition is double: eating the flesh of the Son of Man AND drinking his blood. Sarx (dl=4) again, now paired with haima (blood, G129) — two substances that are maximally physical and maximally associated with sacrificial death. Jewish law strictly prohibited the consumption of blood (Lev 17:14; Gen 9:4); the demand to drink blood would have been heard as shocking in the extreme. Jesus does not withdraw the claim; he escalates it. Zōēn ouk echete en heautois — "you have no life in yourselves." The consequence of non-eating is stated negatively: the absence of life.
6.54: ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα — from this verse through v.58, the verb for eating shifts from phagō/esthiō to trōgō (G5176). Trōgō carries the sense of munching, gnawing, chewing — a more concrete, physical action than the generic eating verbs. The shift is not accidental; it reinforces the physical reality of the flesh being spoken of against any purely spiritualizing interpretation. Whether this applies primarily to the Eucharist, to appropriating Christ by faith, or to both is a question the Evangelist does not close. What he does rule out is a reading that treats the flesh and blood as mere metaphor with no bodily referent. κἀγὼ ἀναστήσω αὐτὸν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ — the promise of resurrection at the last day appears again (third occurrence in the chapter), now attached specifically to the one who eats and drinks.
6.55: ἡ γὰρ σάρξ μου ἀληθής ἐστιν βρῶσις καὶ τὸ αἷμά μου ἀληθής ἐστιν πόσις — "my flesh is real food and my blood is real drink." The adjective alēthēs (true, real, genuine, G227) distinguishes this food from any substitute or symbol. Some manuscripts read alēthōs (truly, adverb) — either reading makes the same assertion. The grammar declares that the flesh and blood constitute actual, not figurative, nourishment — whatever the precise mode of that nourishment is.
6.56: ὁ τρώγων μου τὴν σάρκα καὶ πίνων μου τὸ αἷμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μένει κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ — the consequence of eating and drinking is mutual indwelling: en emoi menei kagō en autō (remains in me and I in him). The verb menō (G3306, abide/remain/dwell) is John's characteristic term for the union between believer and Christ, and between Christ and the Father (15:4–10). The flesh-and-blood language opens into the menō language: physical eating produces ontological union. The structure parallels the vine and branches metaphor of ch.15.
6.57: καθὼς ἀπέστειλέν με ὁ ζῶν πατὴρ κἀγὼ ζῶ διὰ τὸν πατέρα — "just as the living Father sent me and I live because of the Father." The kathōs... kagō (just as... so also I) construction creates an analogy: the Son's relation of life-from-the-Father is the model for the believer's relation of life-from-the-Son. Dia ton patera (because of the Father, literally "through the Father") — the Son's life is derived, not self-generated; similarly ho trōgōn me kakeinos zēsetai di᾽ eme — "the one who feeds on me will live because of me." The chain of derived life descends: Father → Son → believer.
6.58: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ἐξ οὐρανοῦ καταβάς, οὐ καθὼς ἔφαγον οἱ πατέρες καὶ ἀπέθανον — the manna contrast recurs for the sixth and final time, now as the discourse's closing summary. The definitive comparison: "not as the fathers ate and died." The discourse began with the crowd citing manna (v.31); it closes with Jesus re-citing the manna only to mark the final contrast. The repetition throughout has been building the argument: the manna was genuine provision, genuinely from God, but it could not give eternal life. This bread can and does.
6.59: Ταῦτα εἶπεν ἐν συναγωγῇ διδάσκων ἐν Καφαρναούμ — the Evangelist closes the discourse with a setting notation: he said these things while teaching in the synagogue at Capernaum. The synagogue setting is significant: this is not private instruction to disciples but public teaching in the principal institution of Jewish communal life. The audience was therefore wider and more institutionally Jewish than the lakeside crowd; the offense of the flesh-and-blood teaching was delivered in a formal religious context.
6.60: Σκληρός ἐστιν ὁ λόγος οὗτος — "This is a hard saying." Sklēros (G4642) means hard, rough, harsh, offensive — not merely difficult to understand but objectionable, unacceptable to those hearing it. The complaint comes not from the crowd but from polloi ek tōn mathētōn autou (many of his disciples) — those who had been following him. Tis dynatai autou akouein — "who can accept/hear it?" The verb akouō (G191) here means to receive, to accept, not merely to hear with the ear.
6.61: εἰδὼς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν ἑαυτῷ ὅτι γογγύζουσιν — "knowing within himself that they were grumbling." The participle eidōs (knowing, from oida) indicates divine inner knowledge — Jesus knows without being told. The wilderness-murmuring vocabulary (gongyzosin) returns, now applied to his own disciples. The word choice is deliberate and withering: not just the hostile crowd but his followers are behaving like Israel in the desert.
6.62: ἐὰν οὖν θεωρῆτε τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἀναβαίνοντα ὅπου ἦν τὸ πρότερον — "what then if you see the Son of Man ascending where he was before?" The question is conditional and rhetorical. Anabainonta (ascending, present participle) — the verb anabainō that opened the chapter (Jesus went up the mountain, v.3) now points to the eschatological ascent. Hopou ēn to proteron — "where he was before" — pre-existence is asserted: the Son of Man has a prior heavenly location. The implied argument: if you stumble at incarnate flesh, what will you do when you see him return whence he came? The offense of the Incarnation is not resolved by the Ascension; if anything, the Ascension confirms the entire claim.
6.63: τὸ πνεῦμά ἐστιν τὸ ζῳοποιοῦν, ἡ σὰρξ οὐκ ὠφελεῖ οὐδέν — "the Spirit is the one giving life; the flesh profits nothing." Pneuma (G4151, dl=3) and sarx (G4561, dl=4) stand in antithesis. The verse has been read as a qualification of vv.51-58: if "flesh profits nothing," perhaps the flesh-and-blood language there was purely metaphorical. But this reading misunderstands the antithesis. The sarx that profits nothing here is not the flesh of Christ spoken of in the discourse; it is the natural human capacity to understand spiritual realities apart from divine revelation. Flesh = unregenerate human perception; Spirit = the divine capacity that gives life. τὰ ῥήματά μου πνεῦμά ἐστιν καὶ ζωή ἐστιν — "the words I have spoken are spirit and are life." Rhēmata (spoken utterances, G4487) rather than logos (word); Jesus refers to the specific discourse just delivered. These utterances are characterized as pneuma and zōē — they are received through the Spirit and they communicate life.
6.64: ἀλλ᾽ εἰσὶν ἐξ ὑμῶν τινες οἳ οὐ πιστεύουσιν — "but there are some of you who do not believe." The Evangelist adds his parenthetical note: ēdei gar ἐξ ἀρχῆς ὁ Ἰησοῦς τίνες εἰσὶν οἱ μὴ πιστεύοντες — "for Jesus had known from the beginning which ones were not believing" (present participle). The foreknowledge of Jesus encompasses both unbelief and betrayal: kai tis estin ho paradōsōn auton (and who the one about to betray him is). The future participle paradōsōn points to Judas, identified at v.71.
6.65: οὐδεὶς δύναται ἐλθεῖν πρός με ἐὰν μὴ ᾖ δεδομένον αὐτῷ ἐκ τοῦ πατρός — the statement of v.44 is restated in different form: "no one can come to me unless it has been given to him from the Father." The passive periphrastic ē dedomenon (it has been given and stands given) places the enabling firmly on the divine side. The discourse began with the Father giving the Son bread to distribute; it ends with the Father giving certain people to the Son. Both the gift of bread and the gift of persons are divine initiatives.
6.66: ἐκ τούτου πολλοὶ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ἀπῆλθον εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω — "from this time many of his disciples went away to the things behind." Eis ta opisō (to the things behind) is the expression for reverting: they turned back, retreated from following. Ouketi met᾽ autou periepatoun — "no longer walked with him." The verb peripateō (to walk with, G4043) is the term used throughout the Gospels for discipleship as accompanying. They stopped accompanying him. The mass departure is not explained or lamented by the Evangelist; it is stated as the consequence of the teaching.
6.67: μὴ καὶ ὑμεῖς θέλετε ὑπάγειν — "You do not also want to go away, do you?" The particle mē before the question expects the answer "no" — but the question is genuinely open. The Twelve are addressed as a group. Hypagein (to go away, depart, withdraw) uses the same verb that will later describe Judas's departure (13:3). The question does not deny that they could leave; it invites their own declaration.
6.68: κύριε, πρὸς τίνα ἀπελευσόμεθα; ῥήματα ζωῆς αἰωνίου ἔχεις — Simon Peter's response is not a statement of unqualified understanding but of exclusive commitment: "Lord, to whom shall we go? You have words of eternal life." Rhēmata — the spoken utterances, picking up the same word from v.63 where Jesus called his words "spirit and life." Peter does not say he understood the bread-and-blood discourse; he says that Jesus alone has the words that give eternal life (zōēs aiōniou). The logic is relational and exclusionary: there is nowhere else to go, not because all alternatives are examined and rejected, but because the words here cannot be found elsewhere.
6.69: καὶ ἡμεῖς πεπιστεύκαμεν καὶ ἐγνώκαμεν — "and we have believed and have come to know." Both verbs are perfect tense: pepisteukamen (we have believed and our belief stands) and egnōkamen (we have come to know and that knowing stands). The order — first believe, then know — is the Johannine epistemology: faith precedes and grounds understanding. ὅτι σὺ εἶ ὁ ἅγιος τοῦ θεοῦ — "that you are the Holy One of God." Ho hagios tou theou — not "the Christ" (the Synoptic form of the confession at Caesarea Philippi) but "the Holy One of God," a title that marks consecration to and participation in the divine holiness. The title appears in Mark 1:24 in the mouth of a demon; Peter here uses it as acclamation.
6.70: οὐκ ἐγὼ ὑμᾶς τοὺς δώδεκα ἐξελεξάμην — "Did I not choose you, the Twelve?" The emphatic egō (I) stresses Jesus's own initiative in the election. Exelexamēn (aorist: I chose, a completed act). Despite the choosing, one of the Twelve is diabolos — not "a devil" in the sense of a fallen angel but the diabolic one, the slanderer/accuser. The article diabolos in Greek is the title given to Satan; its application to a human being is jarring. Jesus says he chose all twelve knowing what one of them would do (v.64 confirmed his foreknowledge).
6.71: ἔλεγεν δὲ τὸν Ἰούδαν Σίμωνος Ἰσκαριώτου — the Evangelist's parenthetical identification of Judas: "He was speaking of Judas son of Simon Iscariot." Iskariot is likely a geographic designation — from Kerioth in southern Judea — making Judas the only non-Galilean among the Twelve. The participle mellōn auton paradidōnai (about to betray him) uses the impersonal future participle: the betrayal is not yet accomplished but already in view. The word paradidōmi (G3860, to hand over, betray, deliver up) is the term used throughout the Passion narrative. Its introduction here, at the moment of the mass defection, places Judas's betrayal within the wider pattern of departures from Jesus that ch.6 has narrated.

7.1: μετὰ ταῦτα (meta tauta) is the Evangelist's typical narrative connector — "after these things" — marking a new episode without specifying the exact interval. περιεπάτει (imperfect of G4043 peripateō) is continuous: Jesus was going about in Galilee over a period. The imperfect of ζητεῖν + ἀποκτεῖναι ("seeking to kill") mirrors it: the Judean leaders' lethal intent is not a single decision but an ongoing disposition, set up in the aftermath of John 5.
7.2: σκηνοπηγία (G4634, from skēnē, tent, + pēgnymi, to fasten/pitch) is the Greek term for the Feast of Tabernacles — literally "tent-pitching." It was the seven-day harvest festival of Tishri (September–October), during which pilgrims constructed and lived in temporary shelters. The Evangelist identifies it as "the feast of the Jews" (hē heortē tōn Ioudaiōn) — his standard distancing formula — without yet indicating which day matters most (that comes at v.37).
7.3: ἀδελφοί (G80, brothers) — already established as separate from the circle of disciples (John 2:12); the Evangelist will confirm their unbelief at v.5. Their imperative μετάβηθι (metabēthi, "depart/move away") implies they regarded Galilee as too marginal for the kind of visibility Jesus needed. ἔργα (G2041, works) picks up the language of chapters 5–6; they want the works performed publicly so the μαθηταί (disciples, G3101) in Judea can see them — assuming Jesus's goal is maximum audience, which his response refutes.
7.4: ἐν κρυπτῷ (G2927, in secret) vs. ἐν παρρησίᾳ (G3954, openly/publicly) — the brothers frame the choice as a PR logic: no one who seeks public recognition acts in secret. Their irony is that Jesus has been deliberately avoiding the kind of self-display they are urging. The verb φανέρωσον (G5319 aorist imperative, "show/manifest yourself") recurs in John's theology as a divine disclosure term (cf. 1:31; 2:11); here the brothers use it in a worldly register.
7.5: οὐδὲ γὰρ οἱ ἀδελφοὶ αὐτοῦ ἐπίστευον εἰς αὐτόν — the Evangelist's parenthetical aside. The imperfect ἐπίστευον (G4100) marks persistent, ongoing unbelief, not a single refusal. Eis auton (into him) is the Johannine construction for full, trusting belief — what the brothers lack is not merely intellectual assent but this orientation of the whole person toward Jesus.
7.6: ὁ καιρὸς ὁ ἐμός (ho kairos ho emos) — the Evangelist uses G2540 kairos (appointed season, right occasion) here and in v.8, in contrast with the brothers' time which is "always ready." This is different from G5610 hōra ("his hour") which appears at v.30 — kairos indicates the divinely ordered moment for a particular act; hōra carries the weight of the hour of death-and-glorification. πάντοτε ἕτοιμός ἐστιν (always ready, present tense) signals that the world operates without this constraint: for those aligned with it, any occasion will do.
7.7: ὁ κόσμος ὑμᾶς οὐ δύναται μισεῖν — the world cannot hate the brothers because they belong to it; hatred of those who are its own would be self-contradiction. ἐμὲ δὲ μισεῖ (but me it hates) — the adversative is sharp. The reason: ὅτι ἐγὼ μαρτυρῶ (because I testify, G3140 present active), his ongoing witness that the world's ἔργα (works) are πονηρά (G4190, evil). Martyreō is a key Johannine verb; here it explains not devotion but hostility — truthful witness provokes hatred.
7.8: ὁ καιρὸς ὁ ἐμὸς οὔπω πεπλήρωται — the perfect tense of πληρόω (G4137, to fill/fulfill) is significant: "my time has not yet been brought to its fullness." The use of the perfect (result-state) rather than a simple present intensifies the idea of completeness: the kairos will only be real when it has arrived in its full form. This is not hesitation but theological precision about timing. Some MSS read οὔπω (not yet) vs. οὐκ (not), a variant noted in Ellicott; the weight of manuscript evidence favors οὔπω.
7.9: A transitional verse: Jesus remains in Galilee after sending his brothers ahead. The imperfect ἔμεινεν (G3306, remained) reinforces that this was not a momentary pause. The brevity matches the verse's function — a narrative spacer that makes the secret journey of v.10 deliberate, not coincidental.
7.10: ἐν κρυπτῷ (G2927, in secret/privately) vs. φανερῶς (G5320, openly) — two adverbs that bracket the paradox of v.10: he went, but not publicly. The construction ὡς ἐν κρυπτῷ ("as it were secretly") marks the manner of travel, not concealment of identity. Jesus arrives at the feast that he told his brothers was not his time — but the Evangelist has already established why: it was not the right kairos for the public self-presentation the brothers wanted; arriving quietly is different from not arriving.
7.11: οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι (hoi Ioudaioi) — the Evangelist's term here (as in vv.1,13,15) for the Jerusalem religious establishment, not the crowds. Their imperfect ἐζήτουν (G2212, were seeking) continues the hostile searching of v.1. The phrase Ποῦ ἐστιν ἐκεῖνος; ("Where is that man?") — ekeinos carries a distancing force, almost contemptuous: "that person."
7.12: γογγυσμὸς (G1112, murmuring/grumbling) echoes the vocabulary used in the LXX for Israel's wilderness murmuring against Moses and God (Exod 16:7–12; Num 14:27). The Evangelist deploys it to mark divided, suppressed opinion in the crowds. The two camps: ἀγαθός (G18, good/morally upright) vs. πλανᾷ τὸν ὄχλον (he deceives the crowd, G4105 present indicative: is leading astray). ὄχλος (G3793) here appears twice — the MKT renders "the people"; the word refers to the common crowd of festival pilgrims, distinct from hoi Ioudaioi.
7.13: παρρησίᾳ (G3954, bold/open speech) — no one was speaking publicly about him because of τὸν φόβον τῶν Ἰουδαίων (the fear of the Judean authorities). The word parrēsia carries a political weight: it is the speech of the free person who stands in the assembly without hedging. Its suppression here is a symptom of the social coercion exercised by the Jerusalem establishment.
7.14: ἱερόν (G2411, the temple complex — courts and precincts, not the naos/inner sanctuary) — same distinction noted at 2:14 vs. 2:19 in chapters 1–2. The Evangelist notes Jesus arrived εἰς μέσον τῆς ἑορτῆς (in the middle of the feast, the Chōl Moēd), not on the opening days. ἐδίδασκεν (imperfect, G1321: was teaching) marks sustained activity.
7.15: Πῶς οὗτος γράμματα οἶδεν μὴ μεμαθηκώς; — γράμματα (G1121, literally "letters") refers to scribal learning, the rabbinic education acquired under an accredited teacher. The Judean leaders' amazement is at the incongruity: he has the rhetorical competence of a trained rabbi without the formal credential. This is distinct from ignorance; they are not surprised he can read, but that he commands the interpretive tradition without institutional authorization.
7.16: ἡ ἐμὴ διδαχὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐμή — a grammatical paradox: "my teaching is not mine." Both uses of emē (G1699, mine) are identical in form but different in force: the first identifies it as his, the second denies ownership. The resolution: it is genuinely his — he teaches it — but its source is the one who sent him, so it does not originate from him. This mirrors the Johannine pattern throughout: the Son does nothing of himself (5:19); he speaks the Father's words. ἀλλὰ τοῦ πέμψαντός με (but of the one who sent me) — the participial form "the one who sent me" is a near-formula for the Father across the discourse.
7.17: ἐὰν τις θέλῃ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ ποιεῖν — the condition for knowing whether Jesus's teaching is from God is moral, not epistemic: "if anyone wills to do his will." The verb θέλῃ (G2309, subjunctive: wills/is willing) is not mere intention but active orientation; it is the posture of the will that precedes knowledge. γνώσεται (G1097, future: will come to know) — knowing is the outcome of this alignment, not its prerequisite. The Evangelist here encapsulates a fundamental Johannine epistemology: moral will governs theological perception.
7.18: ὁ ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ λαλῶν τὴν δόξαν τὴν ἰδίαν ζητεῖ — the one who speaks from himself seeks his own δόξαν (G1391, glory/reputation). The test of authentic messenger-ship is the object of the seeking: self-glorification (G2398 idian, his own) vs. the glory of the one who sent him. ἀδικία (G93, unrighteousness/injustice) is absent from the one who seeks the Sender's glory — the claim shifts the conversation from biographical to ethical: his motives are verifiable.
7.19: Οὐ Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὸν νόμον; (Has not Moses given you the law?) — the perfect δέδωκεν (G1325, has given, result-state: it stands given to you) contrasts with what follows: and yet none of you keeps it. νόμον (G3551, dl=3) — "law" throughout the chapter; the dispute level reflects the law's range across Torah, Mosaic code, and broader principle. The rhetorical charge is precise: they appeal to Moses while seeking to kill the one Moses pointed toward (5:46).
7.20: δαιμόνιον ἔχεις (G1140, you have a demon) — the crowd's retort is a standard deflection: those attributed with unusual claims were routinely accused of demonic possession (cf. Matt 11:18; John 8:48). The ὄχλος here (the festival crowd, G3793) is distinct from the Jerusalem residents of v.25 who know about the murder plot; these pilgrims are genuinely unaware and so genuinely incredulous at the claim.
7.21: ἓν ἔργον ἐποίησα (one work I did) — the singular ērgon refers to the healing of John 5:1–16 on the Sabbath in Jerusalem. πάντες θαυμάζετε (G2296, you all marvel) — thaumazete is presented as an ongoing state: they continue to be astonished over it. The verse is brief but pivots the discourse to the circumcision-sabbath argument that follows.
7.22: Μωϋσῆς δέδωκεν ὑμῖν τὴν περιτομήν — περιτομή (G4061, circumcision) has priority over Sabbath by Torah logic: if the eighth day of a male infant's life falls on a Sabbath, circumcision proceeds. Jesus's argument here cites the law's own internal hierarchy. οὐχ ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ Μωϋσέως ἐστίν, ἀλλ᾽ ἐκ τῶν πατέρων (not from Moses but from the fathers) — circumcision predates Sinai (Gen 17); Moses encoded it but did not originate it.
7.23: ὅλον ἄνθρωπον ὑγιῆ ἐποίησα — G3650 holon (the whole, every part) + G5199 hygiē (whole/sound) together: "I made a whole person completely well." The logic inverts the circumcision precedent: if partial physical modification (circumcision of one member) overrides Sabbath to fulfill Torah, how much more does making the entire person whole? χωλεύετε (lit. "do you limp/are you angry", G5520 — the verb used here is for being angry) — the irritation is presented as inconsistency, not moral failure per se.
7.24: μὴ κρίνετε κατ᾽ ὄψιν — κρίνετε (G2919, present imperative: stop judging) κατ᾽ ὄψιν (G3799, according to appearance/face). Surface compliance with Sabbath versus the healing of a whole person: the appearance-based ruling condemns what the deeper logic of the law permits. δικαίαν κρίσιν κρίνατε (G1342 + G2920: judge a righteous judgment) — the aorist imperative here calls for a single definitive act of right assessment; dikaios (G1342 dl=4) in this range means "equitable / consistent with the norm of the law."
7.25: τινὲς ἐκ τῶν Ἱεροσολυμιτῶν (some of the Jerusalem residents) — distinct from the Galilean pilgrims of v.20; these locals know the authorities' intent. Their question Οὐχ οὗτός ἐστιν ὃν ζητοῦσιν ἀποκτεῖναι; (Is this not the one they are seeking to kill?) opens a new layer of irony: he speaks publicly and they do nothing. The imperfect ζητοῦσιν (were seeking) is continuous.
7.26: παρρησίᾳ λαλεῖ (he speaks publicly/boldly, G3954) — the same word suppressed in v.13 now describes Jesus's open teaching in the temple. The Jerusalemites' inference — μήποτε ἀληθῶς ἔγνωσαν οἱ ἄρχοντες (could the rulers have truly come to know?) — captures the gap between what the authorities know and how they act. ἄρχοντες (G758, rulers = the Sanhedrin elite) are the ones whose silence is politically significant.
7.27: ἀλλὰ τοῦτον οἴδαμεν πόθεν ἐστίν (but this man — we know where he is from) — the Jerusalemites immediately correct their own question. The messianic expectation they invoke — ὁ δὲ χριστὸς ὅταν ἔρχηται, οὐδεὶς γινώσκει πόθεν ἐστίν (when the Christ comes, no one knows where he is from) — reflects a strand of Second Temple expectation that the Messiah would appear suddenly and obscurely, not from a known family and village. John does not confirm or deny the tradition as such; the irony operates on another level — they think they know where Jesus is from and are wrong about what that knowledge means.
7.28: ἔκραξεν (G2896, aorist: cried out) — the verb marks a sudden, forceful elevation of voice; the Evangelist uses it for moments of urgent Christological declaration (cf. 1:15; 7:37; 12:44). κἀμὲ οἴδατε καὶ οἴδατε πόθεν εἰμί (both me you know, and where I am from you know) — the concessive irony: Jesus grants their claim of knowing. What follows ("I have not come from myself…") undercuts what their knowing amounts to: they know the Galilean; they do not know the one who sent him, who is ἀληθινός (G228, true/genuine, not merely truthful but authentically real).
7.29: κἀγὼ οἶδα αὐτόν (and I know him) — emphatic egō placed first; the contrast is absolute. His knowing is based on: ὅτι παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ εἰμι (because I am from him, G3844 denoting origin: from beside him). The preposition para (from beside/from the side of) carries relational weight — not merely factual origin but essential proximity. κἀκεῖνός με ἀπέστειλεν (and that one sent me) — the perfect form of the relationship with the Father grounds the sending.
7.30: ἐζήτουν οὖν αὐτὸν πιάσαι (they were seeking therefore to seize him) — imperfect of repeated failed attempts. The reason for restraint: ὅτι οὔπω ἐληλύθει ἡ ὥρα αὐτοῦ (because his hōra had not yet come). Here G5610 hōra replaces the kairos of vv.6,8 — hōra is the appointed hour of death-and-glorification, the eschatological moment toward which the entire Gospel is oriented (cf. 12:23; 13:1; 17:1). The crowd's inability to seize Jesus is not tactical but theological: the hour governs the narrative.
7.31: ἐκ τοῦ ὄχλου δὲ πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν (many of the crowd believed in him) — the Johannine pisteuein eis construction; this is crowd-faith in its provisional form. Their criterion: σημεῖα (G4592, signs) — would the Messiah do more sēmeia than this man? The Evangelist consistently uses sēmeia, not dynameis (mighty works); signs point beyond themselves to the one performing them.
7.32: ἤκουσαν οἱ Φαρισαῖοι τοῦ ὄχλου γογγύζοντος (the Pharisees heard the crowd murmuring) — G1111 gongyzontos (present participle: as they were murmuring), a slightly different form from the G1112 noun of v.12 but same root. The institutional response: ἀπέστειλαν ὑπηρέτας (sent officers/temple servants, G5257) to arrest him — the machinery of official suppression is set in motion, ironically during a feast celebrating God's provision.
7.33: ἔτι χρόνον μικρὸν μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰμι (still a little chronos I am with you) — G5550 chronos here, not kairos or hōra: bare duration, the neutral "time." Jesus frames the officers' mission as already irrelevant; his departure is on a different timetable than their arrest plan. πρὸς τὸν πέμψαντά με (toward the one who sent me) — going to the Father is the goal of the departure, not escape from arrest.
7.34: ζητήσετέ με καὶ οὐχ εὑρήσετε (you will seek me and not find) — two futures: zētēsete (G2212) and heurēsete (G2147). The seeking after he is gone will be fruitless — not because he hides but because the destination (ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγώ, where I am) is inaccessible to those who have refused the path that leads there. This is not vindictiveness but consequence: the seeking without the will to do God's will (v.17) produces nothing.
7.35: ἡ διασπορά (G1290, the Diaspora — the technical term for Jews scattered among Gentile nations) — the Judean leaders' confusion generates an ironic prophecy: they joke that perhaps he will go and teach Ἕλληνας (G1672, Greeks/Gentiles). The Evangelist records this as dramatic irony; the mission to the Gentiles does follow from Jesus's work, though not in the way they intend. Diaspora is a Jewish term; its use here signals they are not imagining a Jewish audience among the nations but a Gentile one.
7.36: Τίς ἐστιν οὗτος ὁ λόγος (What is this logos?) — G3056 logos here functions as "saying/utterance," the common Greek sense, not the cosmic Word of 1:1. The authorities' scorn does not answer the question; it registers puzzlement (θαυμάζω implied in the repetition of the saying). Their inability to parse the departure-language reflects the epistemological failure named at v.17: they have not willed to do the Father's will.
7.37: ἐν δὲ τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ μεγάλῃ τῆς ἑορτῆς (on the last great day of the feast) — the Evangelist marks this moment with unusual temporal weight; eschata hēmera and megalē together are superlatives. Whether the seventh or eighth day is debated; the Mishnah describes a water-libation ceremony during Tabernacles (Sukkah 4:9–10) in which water from the Pool of Siloam was poured out — providing immediate context for the living water imagery. ἔκραξεν (cried out, G2896 aorist) — the voice elevated for the crowd; φωνῇ μεγάλῃ (with a great/loud voice) amplifies the urgency of the invitation.
7.38: ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμέ, καθὼς εἶπεν ἡ γραφή (the one who believes in me, as the Scripture said) — the citation formula kathōs eipen hē graphē (as the Scripture said) is used for a text the audience should recognize, but no single OT passage says rivers of living water will flow from within him (or "from his belly," G2836 koilia). The likely referents are Zech 14:8 (living waters flowing from Jerusalem), Ezek 47:1–12 (the river from the temple), and Isa 58:11 (like a spring whose waters do not fail). The citation is thematic rather than verbatim; John treats the composite of water-from-the-holy-place imagery as a single scriptural witness.
7.39: τοῦτο δὲ εἶπεν περὶ τοῦ πνεύματος (G4151, dl=3) — the Evangelist's explanatory aside is rare and important: he tells us what the saying means. Pneuma can mean wind, breath, or spirit; here it is definitively the Spirit given to believers. The explanatory clause οὔπω γὰρ ἦν πνεῦμα, ὅτι Ἰησοῦς οὐδέπω ἐδοξάσθη (not yet was [the] Spirit, because Jesus had not yet been glorified) does not assert the Spirit did not exist, but that this specific gift — the Spirit given to those who believe — awaits the glorification of Jesus. δοξάζω (G1392, glorify) — the Johannine term for death-and-exaltation; the Spirit's coming is indexed to that event (cf. 16:7).
7.40: ὁ προφήτης (the Prophet, with definite article) — distinct from "a prophet"; this refers to the prophet-like-Moses promised in Deut 18:15–18 ("a prophet from among you, from your brothers, like me"). The crowd identifies Jesus with this figure, not yet with the Messiah — in the Second Temple period these were sometimes distinguished (cf. John 1:20–21 where the Baptist is asked separately about "the Prophet" and "the Christ").
7.41: Ἄλλοι ἔλεγον Οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ χριστός (others said, This one is the Christ) — a step beyond "the Prophet." The objection immediately raised: μὴ γὰρ ἐκ τῆς Γαλιλαίας ὁ χριστὸς ἔρχεται; (the Messiah does not come from Galilee, does he?) — the Greek particle mē expects a negative answer. The messianic expectation grounded in Mic 5:2 and 2 Sam 7 requires a Davidic-Bethlehemite origin, which Galilean provenance seems to contradict. The Evangelist records this without correction — the irony is that the objectors are theologically right (the Messiah does come from Bethlehem) but factually uninformed about Jesus's actual birth.
7.42: ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος Δαυίδ (from the seed of David) and ἀπὸ Βηθλέεμ (from Bethlehem) — the crowd cites Mic 5:2 without naming it. γραφή (G1124, scripture) invoked as the authority. The poignant detail: in citing scripture correctly, they disqualify themselves from recognizing its fulfillment because they do not know Jesus was born in Bethlehem. The Evangelist never narrates the birth of Jesus; he lets this misapprehension stand as a sign of broader spiritual misreading.
7.43: σχίσμα οὖν ἐγένετο (G4978, a schism therefore arose) — schisma (from schizō, to split/tear) is used here, in 9:16, and in 10:19 of divisions caused by Jesus. The word implies a structural split in a previously unified group, not just disagreement. It is the Evangelist's marker that Jesus's presence forces a decision that cannot be deferred.
7.44: Verse 44 repeats the motif of v.30: οὐδεὶς ἐπέβαλεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτὸν τὰς χεῖρας (no one laid hands on him). Here v.30's reason (his hour had not come) is not restated — the Evangelist lets the structural parallel speak. The τινὲς ἐξ αὐτῶν (some of them) who wanted to seize him are presumably those who denied his messianic claim on geographical grounds (v.41).
7.45: ὑπηρέται (G5257, officers/temple servants) return to the chief priests and Pharisees. The question Διὰ τί οὐκ ἠγάγετε αὐτόν; (Why did you not bring him?) reveals the Sanhedrin expected a successful arrest. The Greek construction places the question's weight on dia ti (why), implying failure to obey orders; the officers' answer (v.46) reverses the authority structure entirely.
7.46: Οὐδέποτε οὕτως ἐλάλησεν ἄνθρωπος (Never did a man speak thus) — the officers sent to arrest Jesus return with a testimony instead. οὐδέποτε (G3763, never, at no time) is absolute; the officials sent by those who rejected him become unwilling witnesses. The word ἐλάλησεν (G2980, spoke) — not "did," not "performed signs," but simply "spoke": the content of his speech alone was sufficient to arrest their mission.
7.47: Μὴ καὶ ὑμεῖς πεπλάνησθε; (Have you also been led astray?) — G4105 perfect passive of planaō (to deceive/lead astray): the state of having been deceived. The Pharisees frame the officers' astonishment as seduction rather than discernment. ὑμεῖς (you — emphatic) marks the scandal: even those they sent cannot be trusted. The question accuses; it does not inquire.
7.48: Μή τις ἐκ τῶν ἀρχόντων ἐπίστευσεν εἰς αὐτόν (Has any of the rulers believed in him?) — the rhetorical strategy is argument from authority: since no Sanhedrin member or Pharisee believes, the crowd's belief is discredited. The particle mē expects a negative answer. The Evangelist holds this up to dramatic irony: Nicodemus (v.50) is one of them and will speak, and Joseph of Arimathea, also a member, will act at the burial (19:38).
7.49: ὁ ὄχλος οὗτος ὁ μὴ γινώσκων τὸν νόμον ἐπάρατοί εἰσιν — ἐπάρατος (G1944, accursed) applied to the crowd who do not know the nomos. The Pharisees' logic: ignorance of Torah is itself a form of covenant exclusion. But the irony cuts back: they themselves have just misapplied Torah logic (v.23–24), and will attempt to judge without hearing (v.51). The claim that Torah-literacy protects from deception is being undercut by their own behavior in this very scene.
7.50: λέγει Νικόδημος πρὸς αὐτούς, ὁ ἐλθὼν πρὸς αὐτὸν πρότερον (Nicodemus says to them — he who came to him before) — the Evangelist identifies Nicodemus by his prior visit (John 3:1–15), now intervening as a voice of procedural fairness within the Sanhedrin. εἷς ὢν ἐξ αὐτῶν (being one of them) — this is the crux; he speaks from within the body, not as an outsider. His intervention is not yet a declaration of faith, but it is a public dissent.
7.51: Μὴ ὁ νόμος ἡμῶν κρίνει τὸν ἄνθρωπον (Does our law judge a man?) — Nicodemus invokes the nomos (G3551) they have just deployed against the crowd. The procedural principle: the law requires ἀκοῦσαι παρ᾽ αὐτοῦ (hearing from him first) and determining what he does before judgment. Deut 1:16–17 and 17:4 require full inquiry before verdict. This is legal argument from within their own framework, making it harder to dismiss.
7.52: Ἐρεύνησον καὶ ἴδε (G2045, search and see) — the imperative ereunēson (search the scriptures) is ironic: they tell Nicodemus to search the very scriptures he has implicitly defended as procedural authority. ὅτι προφήτης ἐκ τῆς Γαλιλαίας οὐκ ἐγείρεται (that a prophet does not arise from Galilee) — their textual claim is actually weak; Jonah was from Gath-hepher in Galilee (2 Kgs 14:25). The Evangelist again records their scripture-citation as self-undermining.
7.53: The verse closes the Tabernacles episode: everyone went to their own home (εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ). A textual note: this verse begins the pericope adulterae (7:53–8:11), which is absent from the earliest and best manuscripts of John (P66, P75, Codex Vaticanus, Codex Sinaiticus) and appears in various locations across the manuscript tradition. The vast majority of textual critics regard it as a non-Johannine insertion, likely authentic tradition from another context. The MKT includes it but its linguistic style differs markedly from the surrounding chapters.

5.1: ẹορτὴ τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "a feast of the Jews." Many manuscripts add the article ("the feast"), which would identify this as Passover and anchor the episode in the Johannine chronology of three Passovers. The anarthrous reading ("a feast") is generally preferred by modern editors and leaves the festival unspecified. The Evangelist's purpose is not calendrical but theological: Jesus goes up to Jerusalem for an occasion that will produce a conflict about Sabbath and sonship.
5.2: Βηθεσδά (Bēthesda) — an Aramaic name meaning "house of mercy" or "house of grace" (bēth ḥesed). Josephus and the Copper Scroll both locate a pool near the Sheep Gate; archaeological excavation has identified a twin-pool complex with five colonnades — four around the perimeter and one crossing between the two basins, matching the Evangelist's detail of five stoai (porticoes). The precision signals an author who knew Jerusalem before 70 CE.
5.3: The verse lists the disabled: τυφλῶν, χωλῶν, ξηρῶν (blind, lame, withered/paralyzed). The phrase "waiting for the moving of the water" and all of v4 are absent from the oldest manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, P66, P75) and are almost certainly a scribal gloss explaining why the sick gathered there. The Evangelist's original account describes the scene without offering the popular explanation; the healing will come from Jesus alone.
5.4: This verse does not appear in the earliest manuscripts and is regarded by textual critics as a later addition. It attributes the healing power of the pool to an angel periodically stirring the water — a belief current among those who used it. The Evangelist's account requires no such mechanism; Jesus heals by direct command. The absence from P66, P75, Sinaiticus, and Vaticanus is decisive for its inauthenticity.
5.5: τριάκοντα καὶ ὀκτὼ ἔτη — thirty-eight years. The number is precise; the Evangelist selects a detail that underscores the severity and duration of the man's condition rather than a round figure. Some have noted that Israel wandered thirty-eight years between Kadesh-Barnea and the Zered crossing (Deut 2:14), but no such parallel is developed here. The point is helplessness so long-established that healing has become practically unimaginable.
5.6: θέλεις ὑγιὴς γενέσθαι; — "Do you wish to become well?" The verb θέλω (thelō) carries deliberate volition: Jesus engages the man's will, not merely his medical need. The same word returns in v40 where it indicts those who "are not willing" to come to him. The question frames the entire discourse as a matter of will: divine authority is available, but human receptivity must respond.
5.7: κύριε, ἄνθρωπον οὐκ ἔχω — "Sir, I have no man." The man answers the question he understood: he interprets Jesus' offer as help into the pool, and his reply reveals complete social isolation. κράβαττον (krabbaton) — a loanword from the Latin grabatus, a common soldier's or poor man's sleeping mat, not a formal bed. The Evangelist's choice signals poverty alongside illness; the mat is all this man owns.
5.8: ἔγειρε (egeire, present imperative) — "Rise." This verb carries a double register throughout the Fourth Gospel: physical rising and resurrection from death (cf. vv. 21, 25, 28–29; John 2:19–22; 11:23–24). Its use here anticipates the resurrection discourse of vv. 21–29. Jesus issues three imperatives in sequence — rise, take up your mat, walk — the three together describe full reversal of the man's condition. No formula, no ritual, no intermediary.
5.9: εὐθέως (eutheōs) — "immediately." The adverb is a standard marker of divine power acting without delay in healing accounts. The appended note — ἦν δὲ σάββατον ("now it was a Sabbath") — is the narrative hinge. The healing is complete before the controversy is revealed. The word order withholds the Sabbath detail until after the miracle, so the reader registers the collision rather than anticipating it.
5.10: οὐκ ἔξεστίν σοι ἆραι τὸν κράβαττόν σου — "it is not lawful for you to carry your mat." Ἰξεστιν is the language of halakhic ruling — permitted or not permitted under Torah as interpreted. Prohibition against carrying objects on the Sabbath is grounded in Jer 17:21–22 and developed in m. Shabbat 7:2. The leaders address the healed man, not the healer — policing compliance, not asking about the miracle.
5.11: ὁ ποιήσας με ὑγιὴ — "the one who made me well." The healed man grounds his obedience in the authority of the one who restored him: the power to give health carries authority to give commands. This echoes the logic running through the whole discourse — the Son acts under the Father's authority (v19), and the healed man acts under the Son's. A chain of delegated authority binds the episode to the theological argument that follows.
5.12: The leaders' question — τίς ἐστιν ὁ ἄνθρωπος ὁ εἰπών σοι… ("who is the man who told you to...") — edits the miracle out entirely. They quote only the command to carry the mat, ignoring "the one who made me well." The contrast with the healed man's formulation in v11 is deliberate: the leaders cannot see past the halakhic violation to the healing power that produced it.
5.13: ἐξένευσεν (exeneusen) — "had slipped away" or "had withdrawn quietly." This rare verb (unique in the NT) implies deliberate, unobtrusive departure rather than dramatic exit. The crowd (ὄχλου ὄντος) made withdrawal possible. The healed man genuinely did not know who healed him (v13b) — the anonymity is not evasion; it belongs to the pattern of Jesus acting without seeking personal recognition (cf. v41).
5.14: μηκέτι ἁμάρτανε — "sin no more" (present imperative of prohibition with μηκέτι = cease an ongoing action). The link between this man's illness and sin is asserted here without being universalized; contrast John 9:2–3, where Jesus explicitly denies that disability is caused by personal sin. Here the connection is individual. χεῖρόν τι σοι γένηται — "something worse may happen to you" — deliberately unspecified; whatever is worse than 38 years of paralysis lies outside the pool of mercy.
5.15: ἀναγγέλλω (anangelld) — "reported/announced." The verb's prefix (ana-) suggests conveying information up to an authority. The man's motive is not characterized as betrayal by the Evangelist — he may be legitimating his Sabbath-breaking by citing his authority, or simply answering the question he was asked. What follows (v16) is escalated hostility against Jesus, not condemnation of the healed man.
5.16: ἐποίει (epoiei) — imperfect tense: "was doing" or "kept doing." This is not limited to the Bethesda healing; the imperfect signals habitual practice — Jesus regularly healed on the Sabbath. The leaders' ἐδίωκον ("persecuted/pursued") is also imperfect: an ongoing campaign rather than a single reaction. Two imperfects compress what was an extended conflict into a single summary verse, preparing for the discourse that follows.
5.17: ὁ πατήρ μου ἕως ἄρτι ἐργάζεται — "My Father works until now/up to this very moment." ἕως ἄρτι asserts continuous divine activity without Sabbath cessation — a claim familiar in Second Temple sources (Philo, Leg. All. 1.18, argues God never ceases to work). ὁ πατήρ μου — "my Father" (not "our Father") — the singular possessive is the theological provocation the leaders immediately identify in v18.
5.18: ἴσον ἑαυτὸν ποιῶν τῷ θεῷ — "making himself equal with God." ἴσος (isos) means equal in rank, status, or nature — not merely analogous. This is the leaders' own reading of Jesus' claim, not an external theological commentary. They understood "my Father works and I work" as asserting parity, not subordination. The phrase ίδιον πατέρα — "his own Father" (not just "the Father") — is the specific formulation they found intolerable: an exclusive, unique filial relationship.
5.19: οὐ δύναται ὁ υἱὸς ποιεῖν ἀφ' ἑαυτοῦ οὐδέν — "the Son is not able to do anything from himself." ἀφ' ἑαυτοῦ ("from himself") is the key phrase, repeated in v30: the Son's inability to act independently is not a limitation of power but of relation — he acts always in union with the Father, never in autonomy. The double negative οὐ…οὐδέν is emphatic: nothing whatsoever. The paradox is that this absolute dependence is also absolute identity — "whatever the Father does, the Son does likewise (ὁμοίως)."
5.20: φιλεῖ γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ τὸν υἱόν — "for the Father loves the Son." The verb is φιλέω (phileō), the word for close relational affection — the warmth of persons who belong together. The Evangelist uses ἀγαπάω (agapaō) for God's love for the world (3:16) and the Son (3:35); here phileō emphasizes intimacy as the ground of total disclosure. πάντα δείκνυσιν αὐτῷ — "shows him all things" — present tense: continuous, ongoing revelation between Father and Son.
5.21: ṥσπερ…οὕτως — the structural "as…so" that undergirds the Son's equality throughout this discourse. ζωοποιεῖ (zōopoiei) — "gives life" — a compound of zōē (life) + poieō (make); it is used in the LXX and NT for the divine prerogative of creating and restoring life (cf. Rom 4:17; 1 Cor 15:22). That the Son gives life "to whom he wills" (οὓς θέλει) signals sovereign discretion — the same thelō that reappears in vv. 39–40.
5.22: κρίσιν πᾶσαν δέδωκεν τῷ υἱῷ — "has committed all judgment to the Son." The perfect δέδωκεν marks a completed, permanent delegation. Πᾶσαν — "all" — is absolute; no residual divine judgment exists outside the Son. The verse moves from life-giving (v21) to judgment as the two ultimate divine acts: to create and to adjudicate. Both are now the Son's.
5.23: ἵνα πάντες τιμῶσιν τὸν υἱόν — "so that all may honor the Son." The purpose clause (ἵνα) reveals why judgment was delegated: equal honor. Τιμάω in a Mediterranean honor-shame context is not sentimental respect but public acknowledgment of a person's status. The negative is blunt: ὁ μὴ τιμῶν τὸν υἱὸν οὐ τιμᾶ τὸν πατέρα — "the one not honoring the Son does not honor the Father." There is no route around the Son to the Father.
5.24: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν — the double "truly truly" (unique to the Fourth Gospel) introduces a claim of singular weight. μεταβέβηκεν ἐκ τοῦ θανάτου εἰς τὴν ζωήν — "has crossed over from death into life." Metabebēken is perfect tense: a completed transfer with permanent result. The crossing has already happened for the one who hears and believes — not a future event but a present accomplished state. εἰς κρίσιν οὐκ ἔρχεται — "does not come into judgment" — the eschatological verdict has already been resolved for the believer.
5.25: ἔρχεται ṥρα καὶ νῦν ἐστιν — "an hour is coming and now is." The phrase "and now is" is critical and absent from v28. The spiritual resurrection Jesus describes here is not only future but present: the spiritually dead already hear the Son's voice and live. This is realized eschatology — the future hour has broken into the present. Οἱ νεκροί here are the spiritually dead; οἱ ἐν τοῖς μνημείοις (v28) are the physically buried. The Evangelist distinguishes carefully between the two resurrections.
5.26: ζωὴν ἐν ἑαυτῷ — "life in himself." This is the uniquely divine attribute of self-existent life — aseitas, life whose source is in itself and depends on nothing outside. The Father has this intrinsically; the Son has it by the Father's grant (ἔδωκεν, aorist: a definite act of giving). The tension between the derivative reception (he was granted it) and the self-existent character (once given, it is life-in-himself) is deliberate: it preserves both the Son's eternal equality with the Father and his relational distinction within the Godhead.
5.27: υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου — strikingly, this phrase appears without the article — "a son of man" rather than "the Son of Man." The anarthrous form invokes the Danielic figure (Dan 7:13) who receives dominion and judgment from the Ancient of Days, while emphasizing the full humanity of the judge: authority to execute judgment (κρίσιν ποιεῖν) belongs to him because he is human, not only because he is divine. The double nature is the ground of judicial authority.
5.28: μὴ θαυμάζετε τοῦτο — "do not be amazed at this." The command to stop marveling prepares for a still greater claim. Critically, this verse says ἔρχεται ṥρα ("an hour is coming") without "and now is" — distinguishing physical resurrection (vv. 28–29) from spiritual quickening (v25). οἱ ἐν τοῖς μνημείοις — "those in the tombs" — is the language of physical burial. The shift from vv. 24–27 (present spiritual reality) to vv. 28–29 (future physical event) is the Evangelist's two-register structure for the resurrection theme.
5.29: ἀνάστασιν ζωῆς…ἀνάστασιν κρίσεως — "resurrection of life…resurrection of judgment." The phrase echoes Dan 12:2 ("some to everlasting life and some to shame and everlasting contempt"). Κρίσεως here means "judgment" — the KJV "damnation" is too loaded; it is the verdict of the divine court, which for these will be adverse. ἀγαθά / φαῦλα — "good/evil (things practiced)" — φαῦλος (phaulos) means base or worthless rather than the more dramatic kakos; the contrast is between deeds that are genuinely good and deeds that are fundamentally hollow.
5.30: οὐ δύναμαι ἐγὼ ποιεῖν ἀπ' ἐμαυτοῦ οὐδέν — "I am not able to do anything from myself." The formula from v19 returns in the first person, now applied specifically to judgment. καθὼς ἀκούω κρίνω — "as I hear, I judge" — the Son's judgment is derivative: he hears and renders the Father's verdict. Δικαία — "just" — the judgment is just precisely because it has no personal agenda; οὐ ζητῶ τὸ θέλημα τὸ ἐμόν — "I do not seek my own will" — justice is defined as the subordination of self-will to the Father's.
5.31: ἡ μαρτυρία μου οὐκ ἔστιν ἀληθής — "my testimony is not true." This is not a denial of truthfulness but an invocation of the legal principle: under Torah, a single witness cannot validate his own claim (Deut 17:6; 19:15 requires two or three witnesses). Jesus concedes the formal objection before presenting four witnesses in sequence (the Father, John, the works, the scriptures). The verse is transitional: it acknowledges the rule before demolishing the objection.
5.32: ἄλλος ἐστιν ὁ μαρτυρῶν περὶ ἐμοῦ — "there is another who testifies about me." Ἄλλος (allos) means another of the same kind — a divine witness rather than a human one. The verse refers to the Father (v37 makes this explicit), not yet to John the Baptist. οἶδα ὅτι ἀληθής ἐστιν — "I know that his testimony is true" — Jesus affirms the Father's testimony without waiting for the audience to accept it; the claim is declarative, not defensive.
5.33: ὑμεῖς ἀπεσταλκατε πρὸς Ἰωάννην — "you have sent to John" (perfect: the mission stands completed). Both verbs in this verse are perfect tense — apestalikate (you have sent) and mem arturēken (he has borne witness) — marking completed, abiding actions. Jesus appeals to a testimony they themselves solicited: the delegation to John (1:19–28) was their own initiative, and John's answer is part of the record they cannot erase.
5.34: ἐγὼ δὲ οὐ παρὰ ἀνθρώπου τὴν μαρτυρίαν λαμβάνω — "but I do not receive the testimony from man." Jesus does not rest his case on human witness, however reliable. The verb λαμβάνω here means "accept as the primary basis." His purpose in citing John's testimony is explicitly pastoral: ἵνα ὑμεῖς σωθῆτε — "that you might be saved" — an appeal to what they already acknowledged.
5.35: ἐκεῖνος ἦν ὁ λύχνος — "he was the lamp." Λύχνος (lychnos, lamp) is carefully distinguished from φῶς (phōs, light) — John 1:8 says John was not the light. A lamp is derivative: it is lit from another source and gives off borrowed light. ὁ καιόμενος καὶ φαίνων — "the one burning and shining" — two participles: the lamp burns (consumes itself) while shining. ἀγαλλιαθῆναι πρὸς ṥραν — "to rejoice for a season" — ἀγαλλιάω is ecstatic, exultant joy; they basked in John's excitement briefly but did not follow his witness to its conclusion.
5.36: ἐγὼ δὲ ἔχω τὴν μαρτυρίαν μείζω τοῦ Ἰωάννου — "I have the testimony greater than John's." The comparative μείζω does not diminish John but subordinates him within a hierarchy of witnesses. τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἔδωκέν μοι ὁ πατὴρ ἵνα τελειώσω αὐτά — "the works the Father gave me to complete (teleioō)." The verb τελειόω (bring to completion/perfection) reappears in the Passion narrative ("it is finished," 19:30), linking these works here to their culminating act.
5.37: ὁ πέμψας με πατὴρ ἐκεῖνος μεμαρτύρηκεν περὶ ἐμοῦ — "the Father who sent me, that one has borne witness (perfect) about me." The perfect mem arturēken marks completed, standing testimony. οὔτε φωνὴν αὐτοῦ πώποτε ἀκηκόατε οὔτε εἶδος αὐτοῦ ὑράκατε — "you have never heard his voice nor seen his form." The accusation is not that the Father has withheld testimony, but that they lack the faculty to receive it — their capacity for hearing God has not been formed.
5.38: τὸν λόγον αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἔχετε ἐν ὑμῖν μένοντα — "you do not have his word abiding (menō) in you." Mένω (abide, remain, take up residence) is one of the Evangelist's signature verbs (cf. John 15:4–10; 1 John 2:14). The word is not merely heard or stored; it must take up permanent residence and shape the person from within. Their failure is not lack of Scripture but lack of the word's active indwelling — the letter without the life.
5.39: ἐρευνᾶτε τὰς γραφάς — most likely indicative: "you search the Scriptures" (describing their practice, not commanding it). ὅτι ὑμεῖς δοκεῖτε ἐν αὐταῖς ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἔχειν — "because you think that in them you have eternal life." Δοκεῖτε marks this as their assumption, not endorsement by Jesus. The Scriptures are not wrong; the assumption that possessing the text equals possessing the life is wrong. ἐκεῖναί εἰσιν αἱ μαρτυροῦσαι περὶ ἐμοῦ — "these are the ones testifying about me" — the Scriptures' purpose is witness, not self-contained life-delivery.
5.40: οὐ θέλετε ἐλθεῖν πρός με — "you are not willing to come to me." The obstacle is named as θέλω (will), not capacity. This is the same verb Jesus used in v6 ("do you wish to be made well?"). Those who are unwilling to come reproduce the spiritual condition of the man who had been 38 years unable to reach the water: both suffer from a failure of the right kind of wanting. The verse is the moral center of the discourse.
5.41: δόξαν παρὰ ἀνθρώπων οὐ λαμβάνω — "I do not receive glory from men." Δόξα (glory) here is the public honor and acknowledgment that constitute the social currency of the ancient honor-shame world. Jesus is not indifferent to truth; he is structurally indifferent to human opinion as a basis for his identity or mission. This detachment makes his testimony trustworthy and his diagnosis of them (v42) credible: he is not competing with them for the same currency.
5.42: τὴν ἀγάπην τοῦ θεοῦ οὐκ ἔχετε ἐν ἑαυτοῖς — "you do not have the love of God in yourselves." Τὴν ἀγάπην τοῦ θεοῦ is an objective genitive: love for God, not God's love for them. The Shema (Deut 6:4–5) demanded total love for God as the founding covenant commandment. Jesus says they lack this — a withering charge given that the Pharisees wore the Shema on their foreheads in phylacteries. ἐγνωκα ὑμᾶς — perfect tense: "I have come to know you, and this is my settled knowledge."
5.43: ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ πατρός μου — "in the name of my Father." To come in someone's name is to act as their authorized representative, under their authority and on their behalf. Jesus comes representing the Father; his credentials are the Father's commission. That they refuse him while ready to accept an autonomous claimant (en tō onomati tō idiō, "in his own name") reveals an inversion: they distrust authenticated authority and prefer self-assertion.
5.44: δόξαν παρ' ἀλλήλων λαμβάνοντες — "receiving glory from one another." The present participle is causal: the ongoing exchange of human honor among themselves is why they cannot believe. τὴν δόξαν τὴν παρὰ τοῦ μόνου θεοῦ οὐ ζητεῖτε — "you do not seek the glory that comes from the only God." Τοῦ μόνου θεοῦ — "the only God" — the uniqueness of God exposes the absurdity of preferring human validation. The social economy they inhabit is a closed system that excludes God.
5.45: ἔστιν ὁ κατηγορῶν ὑμῶν Μωϋσῆς — "there is the one accusing you — Moses." Κατηγορέω is legal terminology (to bring charges in court). The reversal is sharp: Moses, the mediator of Torah on whom they stake their hope (ἠλπίκατε, perfect: "on whom you have set your hope"), becomes the prosecutor. The perfect tense marks settled, established trust — but that trust is misplaced if Moses' writings lead to Jesus and they refuse to follow.
5.46: περὶ γὰρ ἐμοῦ ἐκεῖνος ἔγραψεν — "for concerning me, that one wrote." The emphatic pronoun ἐκεῖνος ("that one") points to Moses as a specific historical figure and named authority. The claim is comprehensive: Moses wrote about me — not incidentally, but as the forward-pointing content of his writing. No particular Mosaic texts are itemized; the claim is global. Jesus places himself as the telos of the entire Pentateuch.
5.47: τοῖς ἐκείνου γράμμασιν…τοῖς ἐμοῖς ῥήμασιν — "his writings…my words." Γράμματα (grammata) are letters, written texts — the fixed, authoritative Mosaic corpus. Ẕήματα (rhēmata) are spoken utterances. The contrast is not written vs. oral per se, but the established text they already possess and revere vs. the present voice speaking to them now. If they cannot read the Christ-testimony in what they already have, they have no faculty left for receiving the direct word of the one Moses pointed toward.

8.1:
Textual note: The section John 7:53–8:11 (the pericope adulterae) is absent from the oldest and most reliable manuscripts: P66, P75, Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus, and most early Syriac and Coptic witnesses. Where it does appear, many MSS mark it with an asterisk or obelos to signal doubt. Internal style and vocabulary also diverge from the Evangelist's usual register. The scholarly consensus is that the Evangelist did not write this passage; it is an ancient floating tradition about Jesus that entered the Gospel text at various points in transmission. Commentary on vv.1–11 treats the passage as historically credible tradition while noting that its original language and theology cannot be confidently assigned to the Fourth Evangelist.

Ὄρος τῶν Ἐλαιῶν — the Mount of Olives. In Johannine chronology this is unusual; the Synoptics place Jesus there repeatedly during Passion Week (Luke 21:37–38), but the Evangelist does not otherwise describe a night retreat to Olivet. The detail matches the Lukan practice of Jesus teaching in the temple by day and lodging on the Mount at night — further evidence that this tradition preserved Synoptic-type material.
8.2: ὄρθρου (orthrou) — "at dawn," the earliest part of the morning. The return to the temple at the first light of day establishes a pattern (consistent with Luke 21:38, where all the people came early to hear him). καθίσας ἐδίδασκεν — "he sat down and began teaching." The seated posture is the recognized posture of rabbinic teaching; the imperfect edidasken indicates continuous activity, not a single pronouncement. The crowd gathered (pās ho laos proserchetō, "all the people kept coming to him") — the scene before the interruption is one of sustained, welcomed teaching.
