8.3: οἱ γραμματεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι — "the scribes and the Pharisees." This phrase is characteristic of the Synoptic Gospels, not of the Evangelist, who consistently uses "the Jews" for institutional opposition. Its appearance here is further stylistic evidence that the pericope derives from a different tradition. The word μοιχεία (moicheia, adultery) covers sexual intercourse by a married (or betrothed) woman with a man other than her husband; Mosaic law treated it as a capital offense involving both parties (Deut 22:22–24), but only the woman is brought here.
8.4: ἐπ᾽ αὐτοφώρῳ (ep' autophōrō) — "in the very act," literally "caught in the deed itself." The technical legal term implies witnesses were present. Under Mosaic law both parties to adultery were to be executed (Lev 20:10; Deut 22:22), yet no man is brought forward — a conspicuous gap that the questioners do not address. Διδάσκαλε (Didaskale) — "Teacher," the honorific address that frames the exchange as a legal consultation rather than an accusation.
8.5: ἐν δὲ τῷ νόμῳ ἡμῖν Μωϋσῆς ἐνετείλατο — "Now Moses in the law commanded us." The citation is Deut 22:23–24 (betrothed woman) or Lev 20:10, which mandates death for adultery without specifying the mode; Deut 22:24 specifies stoning for the betrothed case. The questioners present this as a settled legal requirement: λιθοβολεῖσθαι (lithoboleisthai, to be stoned). The trap is legal: if Jesus endorses stoning, he appears to support action that Roman law forbade Jews to carry out (cf. 18:31 — Jews had no power of execution under Rome); if he opposes it, he contradicts Moses.
8.6: τοῦτο δὲ ἔλεγον πειράζοντες αὐτόν — "this they were saying testing him." The present participle peirázontes identifies the motive explicitly: not justice for the woman but a legal snare to manufacture grounds for accusation (κατηγορεῖν). κατεκύπτω (katekypsen) — "stooped down" or "bent over." Then τῷ δακτύλῳ ἔγραφεν εἰς τὴν γῆν — "was writing on the ground with his finger." The imperfect ēgraphen (kept writing) indicates a sustained act rather than a single mark. The content of the writing is never stated; numerous traditions (the names of the accusers' sins, OT scripture) are speculative. What the verb does is give Jesus a visible posture of non-engagement — he refuses the terms of their dilemma.
8.7: ἐπέμενον ἐρωτῶντες — "they kept pressing, questioning." Two imperfects side by side: they persisted; Jesus had been writing. He cannot permanently ignore a direct question. ἀνέκυψεν (anekypsen, "he straightened up") is the same verb used in v.10. ὁ ἀναμάρτητος ὑμῶν — "the one among you who is without sin." Anamártētos (G361) is a hapax in the New Testament — it appears nowhere else. The adjective does not specify which sin; it does not say only the specific sin of adultery disqualifies. Its scope is general. πρῶτος ἐπ᾽ αὐτὴν βαλέτω λίθον — "let him be first to throw a stone at her." Jesus has not dismissed the law; he has placed its application back on the conscience of those who invoke it.
8.8: πάλιν κατακύψας ἔγραφεν εἰς τὴν γῆν — "again, having stooped down, he was writing on the ground." The repetition of the posture (κατακύψας, "having bent over") and the resumed writing signal that Jesus is done speaking; the matter is now in their hands. The imperfect ēgraphen again: he was still writing as they processed what he had said. The act of writing may function as deliberate aversion of gaze — not watching them face the weight of their own consciences.
8.9: εἷς καθ᾽ εἷς — "one by one," a distributive idiom. ἀρξάμενοι ἀπὸ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων — "beginning from the elders." The departure of the oldest first is natural: the most experienced have the most to be examined by, and perhaps the most visible social standing to lose. κατελείφθη μόνος — "was left alone." The perfect passive kateleiphthē gives finality to the abandonment: he was left, and the woman was in the middle (the spatial note is in the Greek — οὖσα ἐν μέσῳ). The accusers are gone; the accused remains.
8.10: ἀνακύψας (anakýpsas) — "having straightened up." The Evangelist uses the same verb for Jesus's rising in v.7, giving the two moments a visual parallel. Γύναι (Gynai) — "Woman," the same address Jesus uses to his mother at Cana (2:4) and to Mary at the tomb (20:15); not dismissive but neutral and respectful in this register. ποῦ εἰσιν — "where are they?" The question invites the woman to register what has happened. οὐδείς σε κατέκρινεν — "has no one condemned you?" Katakrinō is the stronger verb — not krinō (to judge, evaluate) but to condemn with sentence.
8.11: Οὐδείς, Κύριε — "No one, Lord/Sir." Kyrie here is ambiguous: it can be the respectful address "Sir" or the fuller Johannine confessional title "Lord." The woman's answer is bare and understated. οὐδὲ ἐγώ σε κατακρίνω — "neither do I condemn you." Jesus uses the same verb katakrinō the questioners had invoked by implication. The statement is not a declaration of innocence — it is a suspension of condemnation, releasing her from the immediate legal sentence. ἀπὸ τοῦ νῦν μηκέτι ἁμάρτανε — "from now on no longer sin." Present imperative of hamartanō with the negative mēketi: stop sinning — or, cease the ongoing practice. The instruction presupposes the reality of the sin without dwelling on it.
8.12: ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ φῶς τοῦ κόσμου — "I am the light of the world." A predicated ego eimi — one of the seven great "I am + predicate" sayings in the Fourth Gospel. The immediate context is the Feast of Tabernacles (7:2); part of that celebration involved lighting four massive golden menorahs in the Court of Women (Mishnah Sukkah 5:2–3), whose light reportedly illuminated all Jerusalem. Jesus delivers this claim in or near that court (v.20) in the days following that festival — the imagery is not accidental. φῶς τῆς ζωῆς — "the light of life." The genitive is both attributive ("life-giving light") and appositive ("light, that is, life"). The Evangelist opened the Gospel with precisely this pairing: "In him was life, and the life was the light of men" (1:4).
8.13: σύ περὶ σεαυτοῦ μαρτυρεῖς — "you are testifying about yourself." The Pharisees invoke legal principle: Mishnaic law (mKet. 2:9; mBB 5:7) disqualified self-testimony. The verb martyreis (present tense) marks their objection as a procedural challenge, not a substantive refutation of what he said. ἡ μαρτυρία σου οὐκ ἔστιν ἀληθής — "your testimony is not true (valid)." Alēthēs in this legal context means "valid, legally admissible" — not that his testimony is factually false, but that it fails the two-witness requirement.
8.14: ἐγώ οἶδα πόθεν ἦλθον καὶ ποῦ ὑπάγω — "I know where I came from and where I am going." The verb is oida (perfect with present force: I have seen and so I know). The knowledge is rooted in his origin and destination — categories no human witness could verify or falsify. This is the fundamental asymmetry: the legal principle applies among equal parties; it cannot be applied to the one whose testimony uniquely concerns his own divine origin. ὑμεῖς δὲ οὐκ οἴδατε — "but you do not know." The same verb, the same perfect form — but opposite. Their ignorance of his origin is not lack of information; it is a failure of recognition that runs deeper than evidence.
8.15: ὑμεῖς κατὰ τὴν σάρκα κρίνετε — "you judge according to the flesh." Kata tēn sarka — "according to the flesh" — in the Johannine register established at 3:6: sárx is the human realm of natural perception, social categories, and surface appearance. Judgment kata sarka is judgment operating entirely within creaturely categories: what can be seen, verified by social standing, and evaluated by the two-witness rule. ἐγὼ οὐ κρίνω οὐδένα — "I judge no one." This is not a permanent renunciation of judgment (cf. v.16; 5:22, 27–30), but a claim about his current mission: the Son came not to condemn the world but to save it (3:17).
8.16: ἀληθινή (alēthinē) — "true, genuine, real." The Evangelist distinguishes alēthēs (factually true, honest) from alēthinos (genuine, belonging to ultimate reality — the real thing as opposed to the copy or shadow). Jesus's judgment is alēthinos: not just accurate but of a different ontological order. ὅτι μόνος οὐκ εἰμί — "because I am not alone." The basis of the judgment's validity is not a second human witness but the presence of the Father. The two-witness principle is fulfilled by Father and Son — not by two human observers.
8.17: ἐν τῷ νόμῳ δὲ τῷ ὑμετέρῳ — "in your law." The possessive hymeterō ("your") is notable: Jesus speaks of the Torah as theirs, not as foreign. The reference is to Deut 17:6 and 19:15 — the rule requiring two or three witnesses to establish a matter. δύο ἀνθρώπων ἡ μαρτυρία ἀληθής ἐστιν — "the testimony of two humans is true (valid)." Jesus is applying their own procedural rule against their objection: by the Torah standard, two witnesses suffice. He and the Father constitute two witnesses — and by that standard the legal challenge fails.
8.18: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ μαρτυρῶν περὶ ἐμαυτοῦ — "I am the one who testifies about myself." The predicated ego eimi here is functional: one witness. καὶ μαρτυρεῖ περὶ ἐμοῦ ὁ πέμψας με πατήρ — "and the Father who sent me testifies about me." Two witnesses, as the law requires. The Father's testimony is given through the works Jesus does (5:36) and through the divine voice at his baptism and transfiguration (Synoptic parallels); in the Evangelist's framework, the works Jesus performs are the Father's own witness.
8.19: ποῦ ἐστιν ὁ πατήρ σου — "where is your father?" Their question is sarcastic — they cannot see the Father, so they reject the claim of a second witness. The question also carries an implicit slur: where is this father you keep invoking, implying illegitimacy or delusion. εἰ ἐμὲ ᾔδειτε, καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου ᾔδειτε ἄν — "if you knew me, you would know my Father also." The pluperfect ēideite in both clauses: the knowledge of Jesus and the knowledge of the Father are inseparable — one cannot know the Father by another path than through the Son (cf. 14:7).
8.20: ἐν τῷ γαζοφυλακίῳ — "near the treasury," or more precisely "the treasury-room area." The gazophylakion was located in the Court of Women, where thirteen trumpet-shaped collection receptacles stood and where the great lamps of Tabernacles had been lit. Jesus teaches in this very space, surrounded by the symbols his claim is now overturning. οὔπω ἐληλύθει ἡ ὥρα αὐτοῦ — "his hour had not yet come." The Evangelist's repeated phrase (cf. 7:30) marks divine timing: what the crowd or the authorities attempt cannot succeed until Jesus himself moves toward it. The "hour" in Johannine theology is the crucifixion-glorification (cf. 12:23, 27; 13:1; 17:1).
8.21: ὑπάγω (hypagō) — "I am going away." The Evangelist uses this verb repeatedly for Jesus's departure through death (7:33; 13:3; 14:2; 16:5, 7). The audience hears it as physical departure; the Evangelist intends the whole arc of death, resurrection, and return to the Father. ζητήσετέ με καὶ ἐν τῇ ἁμαρτίᾳ ὑμῶν ἀποθανεῖσθε — "you will look for me and you will die in your sin." The singular hamartia here (v.21) shifts to plural in v.24 — the singular frames their fundamental failure of orientation, the plural encompasses all its expressions. The seeking is too late: the one who could give life will be gone.
8.22: μήτι ἀποκτενεῖ ἑαυτόν — "Will he kill himself?" The speculation is darkly ironic. The Evangelist has already told readers that Jesus will indeed be "lifted up" through death — but his death will be an act of self-giving, not suicide. Under Jewish law, self-murder was a grave violation; the thought is contemptuous. But the Evangelist allows the irony to stand: in a sense they are correct that no one can take his life from him — he lays it down of himself (10:18). Their mockery accidentally touches the truth.
8.23: ὑμεῖς ἐκ τῶν κάτω ἐστέ, ἐγὼ ἐκ τῶν ἄνω εἰμί — "you are from below; I am from above." The spatial contrast is the Evangelist's characteristic vertical ontology: κάτω (below) = the world as it presently is, cut off from God; ἄνω (above) = the sphere of God's presence. The same contrast governs the Nicodemus dialogue (3:3, 31). ὑμεῖς ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἐστέ, ἐγὼ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου — the synonymous parallel: "of this world" and "from below" are interchangeable. Jesus is not a product of this world's generative process; his origin is categorically other.
8.24: ἐὰν γὰρ μὴ πιστεύσητε ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι — "for unless you believe that I am." The phrase egō eimi here has no predicate; "he" is supplied by most translations but is absent from the Greek. This is the absolute ego eimi — the divine name of Exod 3:14 (LXX: egō eimi ho ōn, "I am the one who is"). The same construction appears at 8:28 and — most explicitly — at 8:58. To miss its resonance here is to miss the claim: Jesus is identifying himself with the God who spoke to Moses. The consequence of unbelief is death in sins (plural now — all the acts of sin that flow from the core failure of not knowing him).
8.25: σὺ τίς εἶ — "Who are you?" After the ego eimi of v.24, the question is deliberately obtuse or genuinely bewildered. τὴν ἀρχὴν ὅ τι καὶ λαλῶ ὑμῖν — the reply is among the most debated in John. The Greek is compressed: tēn archēn can be accusative of time ("from the beginning") or an adverb meaning "at all" or "primarily." The most defensible reading: "Exactly what I have been telling you from the beginning." Jesus points back to the whole discourse — his claims have not changed; the revelation has been consistent. Demanding a new or different answer misunderstands how he speaks.
8.26: πολλὰ ἔχω περὶ ὑμῶν λαλεῖν καὶ κρίνειν — "I have many things to say about you and to judge." The potential for judgment is enormous — Jesus holds much that is true about them that could be spoken as condemnation. But his restraint is deliberate: ὁ πέμψας με ἀληθής ἐστιν — "the one who sent me is trustworthy/true." Alēthēs here: the Father is reliable, and it is only what he has heard from the Father that Jesus speaks to the world. He limits himself to the Father's commission, not to the full extent of what could be said.
8.27: οὐκ ἔγνωσαν ὅτι τὸν πατέρα αὐτοῖς ἔλεγεν — "they did not know that he was speaking to them of the Father." The Evangelist steps back to note the failure of comprehension explicitly. The verb egnōsan (aorist of ginōskō) marks the moment: there was a point of potential recognition that did not happen. The gap between what Jesus says and what they hear is not a communication failure on his part; the Evangelist has been documenting this failure of perception throughout the chapter.
8.28: ὅταν ὑψώσητε τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — "when you lift up the Son of Man." Hypsoō (G5312) carries Johannine double meaning: the physical lifting on the cross (crucifixion) and the exaltation-glorification — the Evangelist has established this in 3:14 ("the Son of Man must be lifted up as Moses lifted up the serpent"). The audience hears only execution; the Evangelist means both. τότε γνώσεσθε ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι — "then you will know that I am." The bare ego eimi again (cf. v.24), and the knowing it produces is triggered by the cross event itself. Crucifixion becomes revelation — a signature Johannine paradox.
8.29: ὁ πέμψας με μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἐστιν· οὐκ ἀφῆκέν με μόνον — "the one who sent me is with me; he has not left me alone." The aorist aphēken ("has left") is negated: there has been no moment of abandonment. This stands in deliberate contrast with the cry of dereliction in the Synoptics (Matt 27:46 // Mark 15:34 — "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?"); the Evangelist's Passion does not include that cry. Here the claim is total: τὰ ἀρεστὰ αὐτῷ ποιῶ πάντοτε — "I always do the things that please him." Pantote (always) is absolute: the obedience is without exception.
8.30: ταῦτα αὐτοῦ λαλοῦντος πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν — "as he was speaking these things, many believed in him." The present participle lalountos places the believing in the midst of the discourse — it was happening while the words were still being spoken. The Evangelist marks this transition because vv.31–59 will show that "believing" exists on a spectrum: many who "believed" in v.30 will by v.59 be attempting to stone him. The Evangelist is about to interrogate the quality and durability of faith.
8.31: τοὺς πεπιστευκότας αὐτῷ Ἰουδαίους — "the Jews who had believed him." The perfect participle pepisteukotas (those who have believed) distinguishes this group from the hostile interlocutors. ἐὰν ὑμεῖς μείνητε ἐν τῷ λόγῳ τῷ ἐμῷ — "if you abide in my word." Menō (to remain, abide, stay) is among the most theologically freighted verbs in the Johannine writings — it will dominate John 15 (the vine and branches). Here it is the condition of genuine discipleship: not a momentary assent but sustained dwelling in his word. The conditional framing (ἐάν + subjunctive) makes the outcome dependent on the continuing choice to abide.
8.32: γνώσεσθε τὴν ἀλήθειαν — "you will know the truth." The future gnōsesthe is experiential knowing (ginōskō), not merely intellectual assent. Alētheia in Johannine usage is not abstract epistemological truth but the reality that Jesus himself embodies and reveals — "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (14:6). To know the truth is to know him. ἡ ἀλήθεια ἐλευθερώσει ὑμᾶς — "the truth will set you free." Eleutherōsei (future of eleutheroō, G1659) — to liberate from moral, ceremonial, or mortal liability. The liberty is not philosophical but ontological: release from the condition described in v.34.
8.33: σπέρμα Ἀβραάμ ἐσμεν — "we are Abraham's seed/descendants." Sperma is the biological/genealogical term — physical descent. This is factually true (Jesus acknowledges it in v.37), but the Evangelist is already marking the distinction between physical descent and covenantal inheritance. οὐδενὶ δεδουλεύκαμεν πώποτε — "we have never been enslaved to anyone, ever." The perfect dedouleukamen is a remarkable historical claim: Israel had been enslaved in Egypt, Babylon, and was at that moment under Roman occupation. The claim operates at the level of covenantal identity rather than political history — they understand themselves as a free people by covenant even when politically subject.
8.34: πᾶς ὁ ποιῶν τὴν ἁμαρτίαν δοῦλός ἐστιν τῆς ἁμαρτίας — "everyone who practices sin is a slave of sin." The present participle poiōn marks habitual, ongoing practice — not a single act but the pattern of life. Doulos (G1401) is the Greek term for slave — not servant (that would be diakonos or hyperetēs), but one who is owned, whose will is subject to another's. Tēs hamartias is the genitive of ownership: sin is the master. The argument draws on Stoic philosophical commonplace (only the wise are truly free) but reframes it: the bondage is moral and spiritual, not social, and the slave is everyone who sins — including those who claim covenant freedom.
8.35: ὁ δὲ δοῦλος οὐ μένει ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "the slave does not remain in the household forever." The slave's position is contingent — he can be sold, freed, or expelled. ὁ υἱὸς μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "the son remains forever." Menō again — abiding is the characteristic of the son, not the slave. The argument employs the structure of household law: a slave's tenure is dependent on the master's will; a son's membership is by birth and permanent. Jesus is the Son in whom abiding is secured.
8.36: ἐὰν οὖν ὁ υἱὸς ὑμᾶς ἐλευθερώσῃ — "if therefore the Son sets you free." The conditional is not doubtful — it is the offer. ὄντως ἐλεύθεροι ἔσεσθε — "you will be truly free." Ontōs (truly, genuinely, really) is the adverb of genuine reality as opposed to appearance or claim. The freedom that Abraham's physical lineage cannot give (v.33), and that the law cannot give because the law requires obedience that enslaved people cannot render, the Son can give — because he is the permanent member of the household who can confer the status of adoption.
8.37: οἶδα ὅτι σπέρμα Ἀβραάμ ἐστε — "I know that you are Abraham's seed." Jesus does not contest the genealogy. The pivot is: ὁ λόγος ὁ ἐμὸς οὐ χωρεῖ ἐν ὑμῖν — "my word finds no place (makes no progress) in you." Chōreō (G5562) means to make room, to advance, to have space to move — it is used of water spreading or of a crowd making room. His word has no room to penetrate, no space to move within them. Physical descent from Abraham is not at issue; the failure is receptivity to the word.
8.38: ἃ ἐγὼ ἑώρακα παρὰ τῷ πατρί, λαλῶ — "what I have seen with the Father, I speak." The perfect heōraka (I have seen, and the seeing is complete and its result stands): Jesus's speech is entirely derived from prior vision of the Father. He is not speculating or inventing. καὶ ὑμεῖς οὖν ἃ ἠκούσατε παρὰ τοῦ πατρός, ποιεῖτε — "and you, therefore, do what you have heard from your father." The parallel construction — I have seen my Father / you have heard your father — sets up the disclosure of vv.44: the two "fathers" are not the same.
8.39: ὁ πατὴρ ἡμῶν Ἀβραάμ ἐστιν — "Abraham is our father." Their claim is genealogical — unchanged from v.33. Jesus's reply shifts the ground entirely: εἰ τέκνα τοῦ Ἀβραάμ ἐστε, τὰ ἔργα τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ ἐποιεῖτε — "if you were Abraham's children, you would do Abraham's works." The key distinction: sperma (seed, v.37) — biological connection; tekna (children) — relational and behavioral connection. Physical descent and spiritual kinship can diverge. The imperfect epoieite in the apodosis (contrary-to-fact conditional): they would have been doing Abraham's deeds — but they are not.
8.40: ἄνθρωπον ὃς τὴν ἀλήθειαν ὑμῖν λελάληκα — "a man who has spoken to you the truth." The perfect lelalēka marks the full extent of his disclosure to them — all the truth he has spoken stands on record. Abraham received heavenly visitors and showed hospitality; Abraham believed God and it was counted as righteousness (Gen 15:6). τοῦτο Ἀβραὰμ οὐκ ἐποίησεν — "Abraham did not do this" — seek to kill a messenger of God who has told him the truth from God. The contrast condemns: their conduct resembles not Abraham's but someone else's.
8.41: ὑμεῖς ποιεῖτε τὰ ἔργα τοῦ πατρὸς ὑμῶν — "you are doing the works of your father." The identity of this father has not yet been named, but the accusation is pointed. Their counter-claim: ἡμεῖς ἐκ πορνείας οὐ γεγεννήμεθα — "we were not born of sexual immorality." The word porneia (G4202) here is possibly a barb aimed at Jesus's own birth (a counteraccusation of illegitimacy), or it echoes the OT prophetic use of porneia for Israel's idolatrous unfaithfulness to God (Hos 1–2; Ezek 16). Either way they are claiming legitimate, exclusive descent: ἕνα πατέρα ἔχομεν τὸν θεόν — "we have one Father, God." The Shema (Deut 6:4) is behind the claim.
8.42: εἰ ὁ θεὸς πατὴρ ὑμῶν ἦν, ἠγαπᾶτε ἂν ἐμέ — "if God were your Father, you would love me." Second class conditional (εἰ + indicative imperfect; ἂν + indicative imperfect): contrary to fact. Agapaō (G25, dispute level 3) — the love that is covenantal orientation and commitment, not merely affection. The argument: God's children recognize and love the Son of God; failure to love him is evidence that God is not in fact their father in the sense they claim. ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθον — "I came forth from God." The verb exerchomai (came out, emerged from) describes origin: not just mission (sent) but derivation (came out of).
8.43: τὴν λαλιὰν τὴν ἐμήν / τὸν λόγον τὸν ἐμόν — the verse pairs two near-synonyms to distinguish them. Lalia (G2981) — manner of speech, mode of discourse, how he speaks. Logos (G3056, dispute level 3) — the word itself, the content and message. They cannot understand his manner of speaking because they are unable to hear his message. The incapacity to hear the deeper content (logos) produces incomprehension of the surface form (lalia). The failure of hearing is not perceptual but volitional and relational.
8.44: ὑμεῖς ἐκ τοῦ πατρὸς τοῦ διαβόλου ἐστέ — "you are of your father the devil." The Greek is slightly ambiguous — tou patros tou diabolou can be "of the father, the devil" (the devil is your father) or "of the father of the devil" (a phrase some MSS resolve by punctuation). The dominant reading, supported by context: the devil is their father. ἀνθρωποκτόνος ἦν ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς — "he was a murderer from the beginning." Anthrōpoktonos (G443, murder of humans) — a compound found nowhere else in the NT. "From the beginning" may refer to the fall of humanity through the serpent's deception (Gen 3) or to Cain's murder. οὐκ ἔστηκεν ἐν τῇ ἀληθείᾳ — "he does not stand in the truth." The perfect estēken: there is no point at which the devil took a stand in truth. ὅτι ψεύστης ἐστὶν καὶ ὁ πατὴρ αὐτοῦ — "because he is a liar and the father of it (lies)." Pseudos (the lie) is what the devil generates; it is his native produce.
8.45: ὅτι δὲ ἐγὼ τὴν ἀλήθειαν λέγω, οὐ πιστεύετέ μοι — "but because I speak the truth, you do not believe me." The conjunction de + causal hoti marks the paradox: it is precisely because he tells the truth that they disbelieve. If he were lying — as the father of lies produces — they would be more at home with it. Their unbelief is not a neutral failure but a diagnostic indicator of spiritual paternity. The present tense pisteúete marks the ongoing state of non-belief.
8.46: τίς ἐξ ὑμῶν ἐλέγχει με περὶ ἁμαρτίας — "which of you convicts me of sin?" Elegchō (G1651) is the legal term: to convict, to prove guilty by evidence. The challenge is real: bring evidence of sin. The silence that follows is itself the answer. εἰ ἀλήθειαν λέγω, διὰ τί ὑμεῖς οὐ πιστεύετέ μοι — "if I tell the truth, why do you not believe me?" The rhetorical structure is a legal argument: you cannot prove me guilty; I am telling the truth; therefore your unbelief is inexplicable on grounds of evidence — it must have a different cause, which v.47 will identify.
8.47: ὁ ὢν ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ τὰ ῥήματα τοῦ θεοῦ ἀκούει — "the one who is of God hears the words of God." The present participle ho ōn ("the one who is") — being of God is a present, ongoing condition. Rhēmata (individual utterances, words spoken) rather than logos (the comprehensive word) — yet the force is equivalent: those who belong to God are attuned to his speech. διὰ τοῦτο ὑμεῖς οὐκ ἀκούετε, ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ οὐκ ἐστέ — "for this reason you do not hear, because you are not of God." The diagnosis closes the argument of the chapter: the failure to hear is not intellectual but ontological — a question of origin and belonging.
8.48: Σαμαρίτης εἶ σύ — "you are a Samaritan." The accusation carries multiple insults: ethnic (mixed-blood, non-pure Israelite), religious (Samaritans rejected the Jerusalem temple and most of the prophetic writings), and social (Samaritans were despised by Judeans). καὶ δαιμόνιον ἔχεις — "and you have a demon." The word is daimonion (G1140, a lesser demonic being) rather than the stronger diabolos (devil). To attribute inspired speech to demonic influence is their only available counter to claims they cannot refute — it was the same accusation made in response to his exorcisms (Mark 3:22 // Matt 12:24).
8.49: ἐγὼ δαιμόνιον οὐκ ἔχω — "I do not have a demon." The flat denial. ἀλλὰ τιμῶ τὸν πατέρα μου — "but I honor my Father." Timaō (G5091) — to honor, to ascribe value to. Jesus frames his entire ministry as honor of the Father; to call him demon-possessed is to dishonor the Father whose work he does. καὶ ὑμεῖς ἀτιμάζετέ με — "and you dishonor me." The contrast: he honors; they dishonor. The verbal root timē (honor) is the standard language of shame-and-honor culture — they have publicly shamed him, which in their framework is the appropriate response to a false teacher.
8.50: ἐγὼ δὲ οὐ ζητῶ τὴν δόξαν μου — "but I do not seek my own glory." Zēteō (G2212, to seek, to pursue) — Jesus is not in a competition for honor. Doxa (G1391) — glory, honor, reputation. The point is not humility as virtue but a statement about his mission structure: his glory comes from the Father (v.54), not from human approval. ἔστιν ὁ ζητῶν καὶ κρίνων — "there is one who seeks and judges." The Father seeks the Son's glory and judges those who withhold it. The passive vindication of Jesus is left to God, not pursued by Jesus himself.
8.51: ἐάν τις τὸν ἐμὸν λόγον τηρήσῃ, θάνατον οὐ μὴ θεωρήσῃ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "if anyone keeps my word, he will certainly never see death forever." Tēreō (G5083) is "to keep, guard, observe" — the same verb used for keeping commandments; it implies sustained, careful obedience. Thanatos (G2288) — death. θεωρήσῃ (aorist subjunctive of theōreō) — to behold, to witness as a spectator. The strong double negative ou mē + subjunctive is the most emphatic negation Greek allows: absolutely will not. Eis ton aiōna — "into the age, forever." The claim is not that the believer will not die physically, but that the experience of death as ultimate terminus — as the last word — will not happen to them (cf. 11:26).
8.52: νῦν ἐγνώκαμεν ὅτι δαιμόνιον ἔχεις — "now we know that you have a demon." Their diagnosis has been confirmed to their satisfaction: his claim about death defies observable fact. Ἀβραὰμ ἀπέθανεν καὶ οἱ προφῆται — "Abraham died and the prophets [died]." The aorist apethanen is simple past: Abraham died. The prophets died. These are facts. If keeping Jesus's word prevents death, and Abraham died, then either Abraham did not keep the word, or Jesus is deluded. They choose the latter. γεύσηται θανάτου — "taste of death." The idiom "taste death" (cf. Heb 2:9) is a metaphor for the experience of dying; in v.51 Jesus used "see death." Both idioms mean the same: encounter death as ultimate reality.
8.53: μὴ σὺ μείζων εἶ τοῦ πατρὸς ἡμῶν Ἀβραάμ — "you are not greater than our father Abraham, are you?" The Greek particle mē expects a negative answer: surely not? Their question is sarcastic; they expect him to back down. The Evangelist is setting up the answer: yes, actually, he is greater — not merely because he makes large claims, but because he pre-exists Abraham (v.58). τίνα σεαυτὸν ποιεῖς — "who do you make yourself?" The verb poieō (to make, to do) implies fabrication — they accuse him of self-construction, of inventing a greatness that is not real. The same charge of self-promotion will be made at the cross (19:7, 12).
8.54: ἐὰν ἐγὼ δοξάζω ἐμαυτόν, ἡ δόξα μου οὐδέν ἐστιν — "if I glorify myself, my glory is nothing." The conditional (ἐάν + subjunctive) is logically possible but not actual: he does not self-glorify. Self-generated glory is empty — it has no external referent. ὃν ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι θεὸς ἡμῶν ἐστιν — "whom you say is your God." Jesus deliberately turns their own language back on them: this is the God they claim. ἐστιν ὁ δοξάζων με — "it is he who glorifies me." The Father's glorification of the Son is not future only — it is ongoing in the works Jesus does and will reach its climax in the cross-resurrection event (12:28; 17:5).
8.55: καὶ οὐκ ἐγνώκατε αὐτόν, ἐγὼ δὲ οἶδα αὐτόν — "and you have not known him, but I know him." The contrast of verbs is significant: egnōkate (perfect of ginōskō) — the relational, experiential knowing that grows over time — is what they lack. Oida (the knowing that comes from having seen) — is what Jesus has. His knowing of the Father is not derived from revelation given to him; it is immediate, direct seeing of the one from whom he came (cf. v.38). κἂν εἴπω ὅτι οὐκ οἶδα αὐτόν, ἔσομαι ὅμοιος ὑμῖν ψεύστης — "and if I said I did not know him, I would be a liar like you." He cannot deny what he knows without becoming what the devil is (v.44).
8.56: Ἀβραὰμ ὁ πατὴρ ὑμῶν ἠγαλλιάσατο ἵνα ἴδῃ τὴν ἡμέραν τὴν ἐμήν — "Abraham your father rejoiced that he would see my day." The aorist ēgalliasato marks a completed act of joy. Agalliaō (G21) is exultant, leaping gladness — stronger than ordinary happiness. The content: tēn hēmeran tēn emēn — "my day." Jewish tradition associated Abraham's joy with the birth of Isaac (Gen 21:6), with the vision of the future (Gen 15:17 — the smoking firepot passing between the covenant pieces), or with the Aqedah where Abraham "saw" God's provision. The Evangelist does not specify which moment; what matters is that Abraham looked forward to a day that was Jesus's own — and saw it, and was glad.
8.57: πεντήκοντα ἔτη οὔπω ἔχεις — "you are not yet fifty years old." The age of fifty was not chosen arbitrarily: it was the upper limit of Levitical service in the tabernacle (Num 4:3; 8:25). In rabbinic thought, fifty represented full maturity — the age at which wisdom was complete. The point is the absurdity of the claim: how could someone not yet a mature adult have had contact with a patriarch dead for two thousand years? Their objection assumes temporal proximity as the only mode of encounter — which is exactly the assumption Jesus dismantles in v.58.
8.58: πρὶν Ἀβραὰμ γενέσθαι, ἐγώ εἰμι — "before Abraham was born (came into being), I am." The theological climax of the chapter, and arguably of all of John 8. Two verbs in deliberate contrast: γενέσθαι (aorist infinitive of ginomai) — to come into being, to begin to exist, the verb of temporal origination used throughout the Prologue for what "became" in creation (1:3, 6, 10); and εἰμί (present indicative of eimi) — to be, in the absolute, timeless sense. Abraham "became"; Jesus simply "is." The grammar refuses to put Jesus inside the same temporal category as Abraham. The bare egō eimi — absolute, without predicate — is the divine name of Exod 3:14 (LXX: egō eimi ho ōn). The Jewish audience understands immediately: this is why they reach for stones.
8.59: ἦραν οὖν λίθους ἵνα βάλωσιν ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν — "therefore they lifted up stones in order to throw them at him." The inferential oun ("therefore"): the stoning attempt is the direct consequence of the ego eimi of v.58. Under Mosaic law, blasphemy — claiming to be God — was a capital offense requiring stoning (Lev 24:16). Their response is not irrational violence; it is the application of Torah to what they heard as the worst possible blasphemy. The Evangelist presents their action as legally coherent and spiritually catastrophic simultaneously. Ἰησοῦς δὲ ἐκρύβη καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τοῦ ἱεροῦ — "but Jesus hid himself and went out of the temple." The verb ekrybē (aorist passive of kryptō, to hide) is the only place in the Fourth Gospel where Jesus conceals himself from arrest in this way. His hour has not yet come (v.20); he withdraws until it does.

9.1: τυφλὸν ἐκ γενετῆς — "blind from birth." Genetē (G1079) is the noun for birth itself; ek genetēs ("out of birth") marks the condition as coextensive with the man's entire existence, not an accident that came later. The Evangelist's terse opening — "as he went along, he saw a man blind from birth" — drops the reader into the scene without prologue, the blindness stated as a fixed, absolute fact.
9.2: τίς ἥμαρτεν — "who sinned?" Hamartanō (G264, aorist). The disciples' question assumes a theology of strict retributive correspondence: disability is the visible result of specific sin. This view circulated widely in Second Temple Judaism (cf. the rabbinic discussion in b. Shabbat 55a: "there is no death without sin, no suffering without transgression"). The question acknowledges two logical candidates — the man himself or his parents — but cannot conceive of a third option. Jesus will supply it.
9.3: ἵνα φανερωθῇ τὰ ἔργα τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ — purpose clause: "so that the works of God might be manifested in him." Phaneroō (G5319, aorist passive subjunctive) is one of the Evangelist's signature verbs for revelation made visible (cf. 1:31; 2:11; 3:21; 17:6). The clause does not explain the cause of the man's blindness from birth but the purpose of this encounter: the condition is now the site of divine display. Ἐν αὐτῷ — "in him" — the man himself is the locus of the manifestation, not merely its occasion.
9.4: ἡμᾶς δεῖ ἐργάζεσθαι — "we must work." The manuscripts divide between hēmas ("we/us") and eme ("me/I"), with the plural having strong support. The dei (G1163) is the impersonal verb of divine necessity used repeatedly in John (3:7; 3:30; 4:4; 10:16; 20:9). ἔρχεται νύξ — "night is coming" — nyx here carries both literal and theological weight: the hours of Jesus's earthly mission are limited, and the darkness that is the condition of judgment (cf. 3:19–21) encroaches. The urgency of works follows from the finitude of the day.
9.5: φῶς εἰμι τοῦ κόσμου — "I am the light of the world." The ἐγώ εἰμι formula with the predicate phōs (G5457) frames the healing about to occur: Jesus is about to give literal sight to one who is literally blind, enacting in physical form what he is claiming theologically. The hotan clause ("when I am in the world") marks this as the period of his incarnate presence — the light is embodied and located. Compare 8:12, where the same I-AM formula appears in the treasury of the temple.
9.6: ἔπτυσεν χαμαί, ἐποίησεν πηλόν — "he spit on the ground, made mud." Chamai (G5476) means "on the ground" or "to the earth"; it appears only here and in 18:6 in the NT. The making of mud (pēlos, G4081) from saliva and earth carries a secondary register: in Gen 2:7, God forms (eplassen) man from the dust of the ground (choûn apo tēs gēs). The Evangelist does not invoke the parallel explicitly, but a healer who forms clay and restores sight to eyes is doing something that echoes the original act of creation. ἐπέχρισεν (G2025) — "he spread/anointed" — the verb shares its root with chriō (to anoint) and thus with Christos; the Anointed One anoints eyes that have never seen.
9.7: Σιλωάμ ὃ ἑρμηνεύεται Ἀπεσταλμένος — "Siloam, which is translated Sent." The pool name derives from the Hebrew/Aramaic Shiloaḥ (שִׁילֹחַ), related to the verb šālaḥ (to send). The Evangelist's gloss (hermēneuetai) activates the central "sending" theology of the Fourth Gospel: Jesus is repeatedly identified as "the one sent" (ho aposteillas me, G649); the pool named "Sent" is where the man goes at the word of the Sent One. καὶ ἦλθεν βλέπων — "and came back seeing." The aorist ēlthen and the present participle blepōn: the coming is narrated as completed, the seeing as a present ongoing state.
9.8: A brief narrative transition. οἱ γείτονες (hoi geitones) — "the neighbors." The community who had previously seen him begging (theōrountes, present participle — a habitual sight to them) now face an identification problem. The imperfect ἦν + present participle prosaitōn ("was begging") frames his begging as a settled, ongoing condition — what had seemed permanent is now changed.
9.9: ἐγώ εἰμι — "I am he." The man's self-identification uses the same formula with which Jesus discloses his divine identity throughout the Fourth Gospel (4:26; 6:20; 8:24, 28, 58; 18:5–6). Here the usage is entirely ordinary — the man is identifying himself, not making a theological claim. The juxtaposition is deliberate: the blind man who has just received sight from Jesus now speaks with the same words Jesus uses for himself. Both uses appear within a single chapter.
9.10: The neighbors' question is direct: πῶς [οὖν] ἠνεῴχθησάν σου οἱ ὀφθαλμοί; — "how then were your eyes opened?" Anoigō (G455, aorist passive) — "opened." The passive voice asks who acted on his eyes without naming the actor. The question drives the three-stage interrogation that structures the rest of the chapter: neighbors (vv.8–12), Pharisees first round (vv.13–17), Pharisees with parents (vv.18–23), Pharisees second round (vv.24–34).
9.11: ὁ ἄνθρωπος ὁ λεγόμενος Ἰησοῦς — "the man called Jesus." The healed man's first identification of Jesus is by name and title: "a man called Jesus." He does not yet know more. His account is precise and factual: mud on the eyes, the command to go to Siloam, washing, sight. ὑπῆγα (aorist, "I went") / ἐνιψάμην (aorist, "I washed") / ἀνέβλεψα (aorist, "I received sight") — three completed actions in sequence, the narration matching the simplicity of obedience.
9.12: ποῦ ἐστιν ἐκεῖνος; — "Where is that man?" Ekeinos (G1565) in the Evangelist's usage often marks a figure whose significance is greater than the speaker realizes ("that one" — with Johannine depth). Here it is simply a demonstrative pointing to an absent party. The healed man's answer — "I don't know" (ouk oida) — marks the limit of his present knowledge and sets the stage for Jesus to seek him out at the chapter's end (v35).
9.13: Narrative transition: the healed man is brought (agousin, G71, present — they lead/bring him) to the Pharisees. The same word is used of leading a defendant to authorities; the framing subtly positions the healed man as someone summoned for examination rather than celebrated. The Pharisees are the ones who will determine whether the healing is legally permissible.
9.14: ἦν δὲ σάββατον — "now it was a Sabbath." The Evangelist holds the Sabbath detail until after the healing is narrated, creating the same collision-effect as in 5:9: the miracle is complete, the controversy begins. The making of clay (pēlon poiein) was one of the thirty-nine categories of labor prohibited on the Sabbath (Mishnah Shabbat 7:2 lists kneading); the Pharisees will focus on this infraction rather than the restoration of sight.
9.15: The Pharisees' direct question to the man — πῶς ἀνέβλεψας; — receives the same account he gave the neighbors, shortened: mud on eyes, washing, sight. πηλόν ἐπέθηκέν μου ἐπὶ τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς — "he put mud upon my eyes." The verb epitithēmi (G2007, to place upon) is clinical and specific — the man does not embellish. His testimony is consistent. The short retelling through the chapter (vv.7, 11, 15, 25) is a Johannine technique: each repetition before a new audience drives home both the simplicity and the finality of what happened.
9.16: σχίσμα ἐν αὐτοῖς — "a division among them." Schisma (G4978) — "split/schism." This is the same word used in 7:43 and 10:19 for the divisions Jesus produces among listeners. The logic of the split is explicit: one party argues no one from God could break the Sabbath; the other asks how a sinner could perform such signs. ἁμαρτωλὸς (G268, "sinner") vs. ποιεῖν σημεῖα τοιαῦτα ("perform such signs") — the works testify against the sinner-verdict. The works-as-witness motif from chapter 5 is alive here.
9.17: τί σὺ λέγεις περὶ αὐτοῦ — "what do you say about him?" The Pharisees turn the question to the man himself — a notable move. The healed man's answer is a step forward in his christological confession: προφήτης ἐστίν — "he is a prophet." From "a man called Jesus" (v11) to "a prophet" (v17). The progress will continue: at v25 he testifies to the undeniable; at v38 he worships. The chapter structures the blind man's confession as a journey from ignorance to full recognition.
9.18: οὐκ ἐπίστευσαν — "did not believe." The aorist marks a decisive refusal, not ongoing uncertainty. ἕως ὅτου ἐφώνησαν τοὺς γονεῖς αὐτοῦ — "until they summoned his parents." The leaders cannot deny the healing; they shift to attacking the account by challenging the identity of the healed man. Phōneō (G5455, to call, summon) is used for authoritative summons — the parents are called as witnesses in a quasi-judicial process.
9.19: Three questions in sequence: Is this your son? Was he born blind? How does he now see? The tripartite structure is the formal structure of witness examination. ὃν ὑμεῖς λέγετε ὅτι τυφλὸς ἐγεννήθη — "whom you say was born blind." The leaders' framing is skeptical: they attribute the claim of congenital blindness to the parents' assertion, not yet accepting it as fact. The investigation is designed to find grounds for denial.
9.20: οἴδαμεν ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱὸς ἡμῶν — "we know that this is our son." The first affirmation: identity confirmed. καὶ ὅτι τυφλὸς ἐγεννήθη — the second: congenital blindness confirmed. Both uses of oida (G1492, "we know") are emphatic; the parents affirm exactly what the authorities had cast in doubt. Their knowledge runs out at the third question.
9.21: πῶς δὲ νῦν βλέπει οὐκ οἴδαμεν — "but how he now sees we do not know." The grammar is precise: they know the "what" (blindness, sight) but not the "how" (mechanism). αὐτὸς ἡλικίαν ἔχει — "he is of age" — hēlikia (G2244) means maturity, legal adulthood. The parents deflect to their son's legal competence to testify for himself — a calculated retreat that the Evangelist immediately explains in v22.
9.22: ἀποσυνάγωγος (G656) — "put out of the synagogue." This compound (apo-synagōgos) appears only in the Fourth Gospel (9:22; 12:42; 16:2). Expulsion from the synagogue was a formal act of excommunication that cut the person from community worship, social belonging, and in some cases commercial life. ἤδη γὰρ συνετέθειντο οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι — "for the Jewish leaders had already agreed" — the perfect syntetheninto marks a settled, prior decision, not improvised reaction. The threat preceded this incident; the healed man's case now triggers the machinery.
9.23: A brief explanatory note: the parents' evasion in v21 ("he is of age, ask him") is attributed entirely to this fear. Dia touto — "for this reason" — the evasion was not ignorance but calculated self-protection under threat of expulsion. The Evangelist does not condemn them; he explains their behavior as the understandable product of institutional pressure.
9.24: δὸς δόξαν τῷ θεῷ — "Give glory to God." This is a formulaic oath-preamble in Jewish judicial and social settings, equivalent to "swear before God to tell the truth" (cf. Josh 7:19, where Joshua uses it to require Achan's confession; 1 Sam 6:5). It is not a call to praise God for the healing but a demand for truthful testimony under divine obligation. What follows — ἡμεῖς οἴδαμεν ὅτι οὗτος ὁ ἄνθρωπος ἁμαρτωλός ἐστιν — is the Pharisees' predetermined conclusion, not an open question.
9.25: εἰ ἁμαρτωλός ἐστιν οὐκ οἶδα· ἓν οἶδα — "whether he is a sinner I do not know; one thing I know." The structure is chiastic: acknowledged uncertainty followed by declared certainty. τυφλὸς ὢν ἄρτι βλέπω — "though I was blind, now I see." The participle typhlos ōn concedes the prior state; the present blepō asserts the present reality. This is testimony reduced to its irreducible minimum: what I was, what I am now. The Pharisees cannot dislodge this because they cannot argue against the man's own experience of himself.
9.26: εἶπόν σοι ἤδη — "I told you already." The Evangelist records the escalating frustration on both sides: the Pharisees keep demanding the account; the healed man notes he has already given it. μήτι καὶ ὑμεῖς θέλετε αὐτοῦ μαθηταὶ γενέσθαι; (v27) — the counter-question, biting and ironic: "you don't also want to become his disciples, do you?" The mēti expects a negative answer but the framing exposes what is actually at stake in the investigation.
9.27: εἶπόν ὑμῖν ἤδη καὶ οὐκ ἠκούσατε — "I told you already and you did not listen." Ēkousate (aorist, G191) — "you listened/heard." The verb implies not just acoustic reception but receptive attention. That they did not "hear" despite being told is the same diagnosis Jesus made of the religious leaders in chapters 5–8: they have ears but do not hear. μήτι καὶ ὑμεῖς θέλετε αὐτοῦ μαθηταὶ γενέσθαι; — the man's rhetoric has sharpened from factual testimony to pointed challenge, marking his growing confidence.
9.28: σὺ μαθητὴς εἶ ἐκείνου, ἡμεῖς δὲ τοῦ Μωϋσέως ἐσμεν μαθηταί — "you are that man's disciple; we are disciples of Moses." The contrast is drawn in discipleship terms. Ekeinou ("of that one") is dismissive — they refuse to name Jesus. The self-identification with Moses is ironic in light of 5:45–47, where Jesus told these same leaders that Moses would be their accuser and that Moses wrote about him. The Mosaic credential they claim is the very thing that testifies against their rejection of Jesus.
9.29: ἡμεῖς οἴδαμεν ὅτι Μωϋσεῖ λελάληκεν ὁ θεός — "we know that God has spoken to Moses." The perfect lelālēken marks an abiding fact: God's speech to Moses is completed and stands. τοῦτον δὲ οὐκ οἴδαμεν πόθεν ἐστίν — "but this man — we do not know where he is from." The Johannine irony reaches its peak: the reader knows exactly where Jesus is from (from above, from the Father, from God — cf. 7:27–29; 8:14). The leaders' professed ignorance about his origin is the precise point at which they fail.
9.30: ἐν τούτῳ γὰρ τὸ θαυμαστόν ἐστιν — "in this is the remarkable thing." Thaumaston (G2298) — "remarkable, marvelous, astonishing." The healed man's irony is sharp: they claim not to know where this man is from, yet he opened the eyes of one born blind — which, as v32 will note, has no precedent in recorded history. The man's argument is empirical: results this unprecedented require a source this extraordinary.
9.31: οἴδαμεν ὅτι ἁμαρτωλῶν ὁ θεὸς οὐκ ἀκούει — "we know that God does not hear sinners." The man draws on common theological reasoning (cf. Ps 66:18; Prov 15:29): God's receptiveness to prayer is conditioned on the petitioner's moral standing. ἀλλ᾽ ἐάν τις θεοσεβὴς ᾖ καὶ τὸ θέλημα αὐτοῦ ποιῇ — "but if someone is devout toward God and does his will." The man's argument is a syllogism: God answers this man's acts; God does not hear sinners; therefore this man is not a sinner. The logic is the leaders' own theology turned against their conclusion.
9.32: ἐκ τοῦ αἰῶνος οὐκ ἠκούσθη — "from eternity it has not been heard." Ek tou aiōnos — "from the beginning of the age" — a hyperbolic claim of absolute uniqueness. That the eyes of a person born blind should be opened has no parallel in healing narratives the man can cite. The unprecedented nature of the sign is itself the argument for the unprecedented character of the one who performed it.
9.33: εἰ μὴ ἦν οὗτος παρὰ θεοῦ, οὐκ ἠδύνατο ποιεῖν οὐδέν — "if this man were not from God, he could do nothing." The conditional (ei mē ēn...ouk ēdynato) is second-class (contrary to fact in form): the healed man is not raising a hypothesis but asserting a logical necessity. Para theou ("from God") — the same phrase the leaders denied (v16, "not from God"); the healed man asserts it as the only adequate explanation. The chapter's irony is complete: the man born blind sees clearly; those who see are blind (v39–41).
9.34: ἐν ἁμαρτίαις σὺ ἐγεννήθης ὅλος — "in sins you were born wholly." The leaders deploy the retributive theology of v2 — which Jesus had explicitly rejected — as a weapon against the man who challenged them. Holos (G3650, "wholly/completely") is emphatic: the charge is total condemnation. ἐξέβαλον αὐτὸν ἔξω — "they cast him out." Ekballō (G1544, aorist) is the same verb used for casting out demons and for the expulsion Jesus warned about in v22. The man is formally expelled from the synagogue — the institutional consequence his parents feared has fallen on him instead.
9.35: ἤκουσεν Ἰησοῦς ὅτι ἐξέβαλον αὐτὸν ἔξω — "Jesus heard that they had cast him out." The Evangelist uses akouō (G191, heard) — Jesus is informed. εὑρὼν αὐτόν — "having found him." The word heuriskō (G2147) — to find — carries the weight of the divine search here: the one expelled from human community is sought out by Jesus. The connection to Ezekiel 34 (the shepherd seeking the lost sheep) is active in the background. υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — the full Danielic title (G5207 + G444), unlike the anarthrous form in 5:27.
9.36: τίς ἐστιν, κύριε, ἵνα πιστεύσω εἰς αὐτόν; — "Who is he, Lord, so that I may believe in him?" The man addresses Jesus with kyrie (G2962) — "Sir" or "Lord." The construction hina pisteusō (purpose clause, G4100, aorist subjunctive) indicates his readiness: he does not resist belief but lacks the object. The question mirrors the Samaritan woman's posture before Jesus's self-disclosure in 4:25–26.
9.37: καὶ ἑώρακας αὐτόν, καὶ ὁ λαλῶν μετὰ σοῦ ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν — "you have both seen him, and the one speaking with you is he." The perfect heōrakas ("you have seen and the seeing stands") followed by the present ho lalōn ("the one speaking") — sight and speech converge in the same moment. Jesus identifies himself not with the divine egō eimi of 4:26 but with a gentle, direct pointing: "that one — he is the one speaking with you."
9.38: κύριε, πιστεύω — "Lord, I believe." The transition from kyrie as polite address (v36) to kyrie as confession is complete in the act of προσεκύνησεν αὐτῷ — "he worshiped him." Proskyneō (G4352) is the verb used in John for worship of God (4:20–24; 12:20). The man born blind ends his day cast out of the synagogue and worshiping Jesus. The Evangelist does not need to comment: the progression from "a man called Jesus" (v11) → "a prophet" (v17) → "Lord" (v36) → worship (v38) speaks for itself.
9.39: εἰς κρίμα ἐγὼ εἰς τὸν κόσμον τοῦτον ἦλθον — "for judgment I have come into this world." Krima (G2917) here is the realized verdict — the actual outcome of the process — rather than the juridical proceeding (krisis). The purpose clause that follows — "that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind" — expresses the inversion John 9 has dramatized: the blind man sees; the Pharisees, who claim to see, are exposed as blind. The judgment is not imposed externally; it is the sorting that occurs in the encounter with the light (cf. 3:19–21).
9.40: Some Pharisees who were with Jesus hear his pronouncement on blindness and take his statement as applying to themselves: μὴ καὶ ἡμεῖς τυφλοί ἐσμεν; — "Surely we are not blind too, are we?" The particle mē expects a negative answer — they are confident they are not blind. Their question reveals they heard Jesus's words but did not grasp that "those who see" and "those who are blind" are categories determined by response to the light, not by physical sight.
9.41: εἰ τυφλοὶ ἦτε, οὐκ ἂν εἴχετε ἁμαρτίαν — "if you were blind, you would not have sin." The second-class condition (contrary-to-fact): their actual seeing is the problem, not blindness. Ignorance without opportunity for knowledge carries a different moral weight. νῦν δὲ λέγετε ὅτι βλέπομεν, ἡ ἁμαρτία ὑμῶν μένει — "but now you say 'we see,' your sin remains." Menei (G3306, present, "abides/remains") — the Johannine verb of permanent dwelling applied to sin. Their claim to sight, combined with their refusal to see who Jesus is, constitutes their guilt and makes it permanent.

10.1: ὁ μὴ εἰσερχόμενος διὰ τῆς θύρας εἰς τὴν αὐλήν — "the one not entering through the gate into the sheep pen." Aulē (G833) — a courtyard or enclosure, not the sheep themselves (that is poimnē). The doubled amen-formula (ἀμὴν ἀμὴν) introduces the figure with the weight it carries throughout John (cf. 1:51; 3:3, 5, 11; 5:19, etc.). The scene is set at night, in a culture where sheep were gathered into a walled enclosure guarded by a single gate. The scenario is immediately legible to a Palestinian audience.
10.2: ὁ δὲ εἰσερχόμενος διὰ τῆς θύρας ποιμήν ἐστιν τῶν προβάτων — "the one entering through the gate is the shepherd of the sheep." The contrast is absolute: the thief/robber climbs over; the shepherd enters by the gate. The legitimacy of the shepherd's access is visible in the method of entry. No comment is made yet about who the shepherd is; the figure is presented in terms of recognizable Palestinian shepherd practice before its allegorical key is given.
10.3: τούτῳ ὁ θυρωρὸς ἀνοίγει — "to this one the gatekeeper opens." The gatekeeper (thyrōros, G2377) is a detail in the figure — he recognizes the legitimate shepherd and gives access. τὰ ἴδια πρόβατα φωνεῖ κατ᾽ ὄνομα — "he calls his own sheep by name." Kat᾽ onoma ("by name") signals individual, personal knowledge: not a collective summons but individual recognition. ἐξάγει αὐτά — present, ongoing leading out. The Eastern shepherd leads from the front, not driving from behind — a cultural detail that gives v4 its point.
10.4: ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν πορεύεται — "he goes before them." The shepherd precedes the flock — he does not drive them. τὰ πρόβατα αὐτῷ ἀκολουθεῖ — "the sheep follow him." Akoloutheō (G190, to follow) is the technical Johannine word for discipleship. The reason for following is specified: ὅτι οἴδασιν τὴν φωνὴν αὐτοῦ — "because they know his voice." The basis of following is recognition, and the basis of recognition is knowing (perfect participle oida, G1492).
10.5: ἀλλοτρίῳ δὲ οὐ μὴ ἀκολουθήσουσιν — "a stranger they will absolutely not follow." The double negative ou mē with the future indicative is the Greek idiom for the strongest possible denial. Allotrios (G245) — "belonging to another, foreign, alien" — the stranger is not merely unknown but categorically other. φεύξονται ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "they will flee from him." The sheep recognize the stranger's voice as not their shepherd's and respond with flight. The pastoral realism is exact: ancient shepherds had personal voices that their flocks recognized.
10.6: ταύτην τὴν παροιμίαν — "this figure of speech." Paroiimia (G3942) — the Evangelist's term for figurative/proverbial speech — is distinct from the synoptic parabolē. While a parable typically presents a narrative scenario for comparison, paroiimia in John tends toward a riddle or enigmatic saying that conceals meaning from some and reveals it to others (cf. 16:25, 29). ἐκεῖνοι δὲ οὐκ ἔγνωσαν τίνα ἦν ἃ ἐλάλει αὐτοῖς — "but they did not know what it was he was saying to them." The failure to understand sets up the interpretive exposition that follows.
10.7: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ θύρα τῶν προβάτων — "I am the gate for the sheep." The I-AM formula with the predicate thura (G2374) — gate/door. In the figure, the gate was how legitimate shepherds entered; now Jesus identifies himself as the gate itself, through which both shepherds and sheep must pass. The claim shifts: Jesus is not only the shepherd (v11, 14) but also the sole point of legitimate access. The double amen-formula reintroduces the self-disclosure with the weight of a solemn declaration.
10.8: πάντες ὅσοι ἦλθον πρὸ ἐμοῦ κλέπται εἰσὶν καὶ λῃσταί — "all who came before me are thieves and robbers." The statement is absolute but contextually bounded by the immediate discourse: "all who came before me" in the role of gate (i.e., those who claimed to be the sole legitimate access to God's people) are thieves and robbers. It is not a blanket dismissal of the OT prophets but a polemic against those who positioned themselves as gatekeepers of Israel and exploited the flock. οὐκ ἤκουσαν αὐτῶν τὰ πρόβατα — "the sheep did not listen to them" — the true sheep discerned the false shepherd.
10.9: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ θύρα — repeated I-AM: "I am the gate." The entry clause that follows is comprehensive: εἰσελεύσεται καὶ ἐξελεύσεται καὶ νομὴν εὑρήσει — "he will come in and go out and find pasture." Three future indicatives: entry (security in the enclosure), exit (freedom to move), and finding pasture (provision). Nomē (G3542) — pasture, the essential provision — appears only here and in 2 Tim 2:17 in the NT. Access through Jesus is not confinement but the combination of security and freedom.
10.10: ὁ κλέπτης οὐκ ἔρχεται εἰ μὴ ἵνα κλέψῃ καὶ θύσῃ καὶ ἀπολέσῃ — "the thief comes only to steal and slaughter and destroy." Three aorist subjunctives in purpose clauses: the thief's entire agenda is defined by what he takes and destroys. ἐγὼ ἦλθον ἵνα ζωὴν ἔχωσιν καὶ περισσὸν ἔχωσιν — "I came that they may have life and have it abundantly." Perissos (G4053) — "excess, superabundance, overflowing measure." The contrast is total: the thief's purpose is pure subtraction; Jesus's is life beyond what sufficiency alone would provide.
10.11: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλός — "I am the good shepherd." Kalos (G2570) — not merely "good" in a moral sense but "excellent, beautiful, noble" — the word carries both ethical and aesthetic force. The Greek phrase uses two definite articles (the shepherd, the excellent one) — this is a specific identity claim, not a generic description. τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ τίθησιν ὑπὲρ τῶν προβάτων — "lays down his life for the sheep." Tithēmi (G5087, present indicative — the laying down is his characteristic action) is deliberate: to place, to set down. Dying is here an act of will, not loss or defeat.
10.12: ὁ μισθωτὸς (G3411) — "the hired hand." The contrast with the shepherd turns on ownership and stake: οὗ οὐκ ἔστιν τὰ πρόβατα ἴδια — "whose own the sheep are not." Idia (own, belonging to oneself) is the same word used in vv. 3–4 for the shepherd's own sheep. The hired hand has no personal investment in the flock. When the wolf comes (erchetai ho lykos, present — it comes as surely as it must), the hired hand abandons the sheep (aphiēsin, G863) and flees. The sheep are scattered (diaskorpizei, G4650 — "scattered/dispersed").
10.13: ὅτι μισθωτός ἐστιν καὶ οὐ μέλει αὐτῷ περὶ τῶν προβάτων — "because he is a hired hand and does not care for the sheep." Melei autō (G3199) — "it matters to him, he cares." The negative ou melei — the sheep simply do not concern him at the level that would override self-preservation. The hired hand's flight is not cowardice; it is the logical consequence of his non-ownership. The lesson for leadership is blunt: those who serve for wages rather than vocation cannot be trusted in crisis.
10.14: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλός, καὶ γινώσκω τὰ ἐμά — "I am the good shepherd, and I know my own." The I-AM formula is repeated (cf. v11) to anchor the mutual-knowing claim that follows. Ginōskō (G1097) here is not merely cognitive recognition but the relational, experiential knowing characteristic of covenant relationship — the same verb will be used in v15 for the Father-Son mutual knowledge.
10.15: καθὼς γινώσκει με ὁ πατὴρ κἀγὼ γινώσκω τὸν πατέρα — "as the Father knows me, and I know the Father." The shepherd-sheep knowledge in v14 is grounded in the Father-Son knowledge in v15 — the same quality of relational knowledge (ginōskō) flows from the divine relationship into the shepherd's relationship with his flock. τὴν ψυχήν μου τίθημι ὑπὲρ τῶν προβάτων — repeated from v11 — the laying-down of life is the expression of this knowing-love, precisely as the Son gives himself in union with the Father's will.
10.16: ἄλλα πρόβατα ἔχω ἃ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τῆς αὐλῆς ταύτης — "I have other sheep that are not of this fold." Aulē (G833, enclosure/fold) — not poimnē (flock). The "other sheep" are outside the current enclosure (the Jewish community) but still the shepherd's own. The scope is Gentile inclusion. μίαν ποίμνην, εἷς ποιμήν — "one flock, one shepherd." The final state: a single poimnē (flock), not separate pens. The structural unity runs through a single shepherd, not through a single enclosure — mission, not ethnicity, defines belonging.
10.17: διὰ τοῦτο ὁ πατήρ με ἀγαπᾷ ὅτι ἐγὼ τίθημι τὴν ψυχήν μου ἵνα πάλιν λάβω αὐτήν — "for this reason the Father loves me, because I lay down my life in order that I may take it up again." The Father's love (agapā, present — ongoing) is grounded in the Son's action of self-giving. Crucially, the purpose clause hina palin labō ("that I may take it up again") makes resurrection the built-in goal of the death itself — not an afterthought or reversal but the design of the laying-down.
10.18: οὐδεὶς αἴρει αὐτὴν ἀπ᾽ ἐμοῦ, ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ τίθημι αὐτὴν ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ — "no one takes it from me; I lay it down of my own accord." The agency is entirely the Son's: no one takes, he places. ἐξουσίαν ἔχω θεῖναι αὐτήν, καὶ ἐξουσίαν ἔχω πάλιν λαβεῖν αὐτήν — "I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it up again." Exousia (G1849) — delegated right, authorized power — appears twice: once for death, once for resurrection. Both are acts of authority, not compulsion. ταύτην τὴν ἐντολὴν ἔλαβον παρὰ τοῦ πατρός μου — the authority is itself received from the Father: the Son's autonomy over death is a commissioned autonomy.
10.19: σχίσμα πάλιν ἐγένετο ἐν τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις — "a division again arose among the Jews." Schisma palin — "division again" (G4978, G3825). The word palin links to 9:16, where the healing of the blind man produced the first schisma. The pattern is structural in John: Jesus's works and words sort listeners. διὰ τοὺς λόγους τούτους — specifically "because of these words" — the shepherd discourse is what divides them.
10.20: δαιμόνιον ἔχει καὶ μαίνεται — "he has a demon and is raving." Mainomai (G3105, to be mad/raving) — the charge is that Jesus is demon-possessed and insane, and therefore not worth listening to. Ti autou akouete — "why do you listen to him?" The dismissal is total. That serious people disagree about whether Jesus is insane or not is itself a Johannine sign of division, not merely a note about audience reaction.
10.21: ταῦτα τὰ ῥήματα οὐκ ἔστιν δαιμονιζομένου — "these words are not [the words] of a demon-possessed man." The counter-argument appeals to the quality of the teaching itself (rhēmata — spoken words/sayings). The second appeal reaches back to chapter 9: μὴ δαιμόνιον δύναται τυφλῶν ὀφθαλμοὺς ἀνοῖξαι; — "a demon cannot open the eyes of the blind, can it?" The healing stands as the test of the claim. The schism resolves into two fundamentally different readings of the evidence.
10.22: Ἐγκαίνια (G1456) — "the Festival of Dedication." Enkainia from kainos (new) + the dedicatory prefix — it is the feast of the rededication of the Temple after Antiochus IV Epiphanes desecrated it in 168 BC, celebrated by the Maccabees in 165 BC (1 Macc 4:52–59; 2 Macc 10:1–8). Today this is Hanukkah. The thematic resonance is deliberate: at the feast commemorating the Temple's rededication, Jesus will describe himself as the one the Father has sanctified (hēgiasen, v36 — same root as enkainia). χειμὼν ἦν — "it was winter" — a detail fixing the calendar (late November/December) and perhaps underscoring the cold reception Jesus is about to receive.
10.23: ἐν τῇ στοᾷ τοῦ Σολομῶνος — "in Solomon's Portico." This was the eastern colonnade of the Temple complex, known from Josephus (Ant. 20.9.7; B.J. 5.5.1–2) as a surviving structure from the Solomonic era or built to recall it. It provided shelter from wind in winter — which explains Jesus walking there in the cold month. The location will recur in Acts 3:11 and 5:12 as a gathering place for the early community.
10.24: ἕως πότε τὴν ψυχὴν ἡμῶν αἴρεις; — "How long will you hold our souls in suspense?" The idiom airō tēn psychēn (G142 + G5590) means to keep someone in a state of suspense or anticipation — to lift the soul up in expectation without resolution (cf. LXX Ps 24:1; 85:4). The demand that follows — εἰ σὺ εἶ ὁ Χριστός, εἰπὲ ἡμῖν παρρησίᾳ — "if you are the Messiah, tell us plainly" — uses parrēsia (G3954, boldness/open speech). The request for plain speech is ironic: Jesus has been speaking plainly; they have not received it.
10.25: εἶπον ὑμῖν καὶ οὐ πιστεύετε — "I told you and you do not believe." The verb tenses are precise: eipon (aorist, I told — a completed past act) vs. pisteuete (present, you do not believe — an ongoing state). The problem is not lack of information but lack of receptivity. τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ πατρός μου, ταῦτα μαρτυρεῖ περὶ ἐμοῦ — "the works I do in my Father's name, these testify about me." The works-as-witness motif from 5:36 reappears: Jesus appeals not to claims but to deeds, which are the Father's testimony.
10.26: ἀλλὰ ὑμεῖς οὐ πιστεύετε, ὅτι οὐκ ἐστὲ ἐκ τῶν προβάτων τῶν ἐμῶν — "but you do not believe, because you are not of my sheep." The causal relation here is striking: they do not believe because they are not his sheep — not: they are not his sheep because they do not believe. Their unbelief is diagnostic, not constitutive. The sheep-belonging precedes and explains the believing, in the same way that the Father's giving precedes the coming to Jesus in 6:37–44.
10.27: τὰ πρόβατά μου τῆς φωνῆς μου ἀκούουσιν — "my sheep hear my voice." All three verbs are present indicative: akouousin (hear), ginōskō (I know), akolouthiousin (they follow). The present tense marks the ongoing, characteristic relationship between shepherd and sheep — not a one-time event but the habitual pattern. The hearing-knowing-following triad is the complete description of what discipleship looks like from the shepherd's perspective.
10.28: κἀγὼ ζωὴν αἰώνιον δίδωμι αὐτοῖς, καὶ οὐ μὴ ἀπόλωνται εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "and I give them eternal life, and they will never perish forever." Ou mē with the aorist subjunctive — the strongest Greek negative: absolutely not at any point. Eis ton aiōna ("for the age/forever") reinforces the temporal absoluteness. καὶ οὐχ ἁρπάσει τις αὐτὰ ἐκ τῆς χειρός μου — "no one will snatch them from my hand." Harpazō (G726, to seize by force, snatch) — the word used for violent taking. The security is not the sheep's grip on the shepherd but the shepherd's grip on them.
10.29: ὁ πατήρ μου ὃ δέδωκέν μοι πάντων μεῖζόν ἐστιν — "my Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all." The perfect dedōken ("has given and the giving stands") marks the Father's gift of the sheep as a completed, permanent delegation. καὶ οὐδεὶς δύναται ἁρπάζειν ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς τοῦ πατρός — "and no one is able to snatch from the Father's hand." Two hands hold the sheep: the Son's (v28) and the Father's (v29). The double security is grounded in the Father's superior greatness — nothing surpasses the Father's power, therefore nothing can overcome his hold.
10.30: ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἕν ἐσμεν — "I and the Father are one." The word order places the two subjects before the verb. Hen is neuter (one thing, one unity) — not heis (masculine, one person). The grammar of this sentence preserves both the reality of unity (one) and the reality of distinction (I and the Father — two subjects, plural verb esmen). The statement is not modalism (the Father and Son are the same person) but the assertion of a unity of will, purpose, action, and nature that the leaders immediately interpret as a claim to divinity (v33: "you, being a man, make yourself God").
10.31: ἐβάστασαν πάλιν λίθους — "they picked up stones again." Palin — "again" — referring back to 8:59. The act of stone-picking is immediate and wordless; the leaders understand the claim of v30 without needing further clarification. The use of bastazō (G941, to carry/bear) rather than a simple "they took up" suggests the weight and deliberateness of the act: they equipped themselves for execution.
10.32: πολλὰ ἔργα καλὰ ἔδειξα ὑμῖν ἐκ τοῦ πατρός — "I have shown you many good works from the Father." Kalos again — the word from v11 applied to the shepherd. διὰ ποῖον αὐτῶν ἔργον ἐμὲ λιθάζετε; — "for which of them are you stoning me?" The question forces the leaders to name their actual objection. Their answer in v33 is precise: not for any work, but for the claim about himself.
10.33: περὶ βλασφημίας, καὶ ὅτι σύ, ἄνθρωπος ὤν, ποιεῖς σεαυτὸν θεόν — "for blasphemy, because you, being a man, make yourself God." The charge of blasphemy (G988) in Jewish law applied to those who usurped divine honor or misused the divine name. The leaders' framing is exact: they understand the claim of v30 as a man claiming to be God (theon, anarthrous — they may mean "a god" or "divine"), not merely a divine agent. Whether they heard v30 as an ontological identity claim or as an improper self-elevation, the charge is precise and the stoning response is legally consistent with Lev 24:16.
10.34: οὐκ ἔστιν γεγραμμένον ἐν τῷ νόμῳ ὑμῶν, Ἐγώ εἶπα θεοί ἐστε; — "Is it not written in your law: I have said you are gods?" Jesus quotes the LXX of Psalm 82:6 (egō eipa theoi este). ἐν τῷ νόμῳ ὑμῶν — "in your law" — "your" is not dismissive (Jesus uses "the law" in the same way elsewhere) but addresses his opponents using their own highest authority against them. The "law" here includes the Psalms, as was common Jewish usage (cf. 12:34; 15:25).
10.35: εἰ ἐκείνους εἶπεν θεούς, πρὸς οὓς ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐγένετο — "if he called those gods, to whom the word of God came." The argument is a qal wahomer (from the lesser to the greater): if Scripture can apply the term theos to human judges or rulers who received God's word, it cannot be blasphemy for the one who is the eternal Word to bear the title. καὶ οὐ δύναται λυθῆναι ἡ γραφή — "and Scripture cannot be broken." Lyō (G3089, to loosen, annul, dissolve) — Jesus's hermeneutical axiom: the Scripture holds. Its words, once written, carry binding force.
10.36: ὃν ὁ πατὴρ ἡγίασεν καὶ ἀπέστειλεν εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world." Hagiazō (G37, aorist — a completed act of setting apart) resonates with the festival context of Hanukkah (Enkainia, v22 — rededication/sanctification). At the feast of the Temple's rededication, Jesus identifies himself as the one the Father truly set apart. The two aorists run in sequence: the Father first sanctified (hēgiasen), then sent (apesteilen). The sending flows from the sanctification. ὅτι εἶπον, Υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ εἰμι — "because I said, I am the Son of God" — the precise claim at issue.
10.37: εἰ οὐ ποιῶ τὰ ἔργα τοῦ πατρός μου, μὴ πιστεύετέ μοι — "if I do not do the works of my Father, do not believe me." The conditional makes the works the test: the claim to Sonship must be validated by the works. The works are the Father's own works performed through the Son — the origin of the works is the criterion.
10.38: εἰ δὲ ποιῶ, κἂν ἐμοὶ μὴ πιστεύητε, τοῖς ἔργοις πιστεύετε — "but if I do, even if you do not believe me, believe the works." This is an appeal stripped to its minimal form: if personal trust in Jesus is beyond them, the works themselves stand as evidence. The purpose clause that follows: ἵνα γνῶτε καὶ γινώσκητε — "that you may know and keep knowing." The aorist gnōte (come to know, initial knowledge) + present subjunctive ginōskete (ongoing understanding) combine: the works are meant to trigger a moment of recognition that develops into sustained understanding.
10.39: ἐζήτουν οὖν αὐτὸν πάλιν πιάσαι — "again they tried to seize him." Piazō (G4084) — to seize, arrest. The imperfect ezētoun ("kept seeking") marks ongoing, unsuccessful attempts. καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς αὐτῶν — "and he escaped their grasp." Literally, "went out from their hand." The same word for hand (cheir) used in vv. 28–29 of the Father's and Son's protective hands holding the sheep: no one can snatch the sheep from those hands; the one whose hands those are passes through the opponents' hands unharmed.
10.40: ὅπου ἦν Ἰωάννης τὸ πρῶτον βαπτίζων — "where John had been baptizing at first." The withdrawal to Transjordan is a return to beginnings: the place where John's ministry started (1:28, Bethany beyond the Jordan). Jesus removes himself from the Temple precincts where the confrontation has reached impasse. ἔμεινεν ἐκεῖ — "he stayed there" — menō (G3306), the Evangelist's verb of purposeful dwelling. The stay is not flight but relocation to fruitful ground.
10.41: Ἰωάννης μὲν σημεῖον ἐποίησεν οὐδέν — "John indeed performed no sign." The contrast is explicit: John the Baptist was a truthful witness but not a miracle-worker. His prophetic function was testimony, not demonstration. πάντα δὲ ὅσα εἶπεν Ἰωάννης περὶ τούτου ἀληθῆ ἦν — "but all that John said about this man was true." Alēthē ēn — past tense, as if summarizing a completed record: John's entire testimony has been vindicated. Toutos ("this one") — the same demonstrative the Jewish leaders used dismissively (9:29: toutou de ouk oidamen); here it marks the one about whom the truthful witness spoke.
10.42: καὶ ἐπίστευσαν πολλοὶ ἐκεῖ εἰς αὐτόν — "and many believed in him there." Episteusan (aorist — a decisive moment of faith). The contrast with Jerusalem is complete: where the Temple establishment attempts to stone and arrest Jesus, this mixed crowd in Transjordan (beyond the Jordan, where Gentile territory begins) believes. The first Jewish community of disciples formed around John's baptizing site; now that same ground produces faith in the one John pointed to. The Evangelist closes the unit with the simplest possible notation: many believed there.

11.1: ἀσθενῶν (G770, present participle) — "sick/weakening." The participial form marks an ongoing condition rather than a sudden crisis. Λάζαρος ἀπὸ Βηθανίας — the name Lazarus is the Hellenized form of the Hebrew Elʿāzār (אֶלְעָזָר, "God has helped"). The naming at the chapter's opening is a Johannine technique: the figure who will be raised is named before the crisis is described, so the reader knows who is at stake. Bethany is identified as the village of Mary and Martha — a detail that presupposes the reader knows these women, suggesting the traditions about them were already widely circulated when the Evangelist wrote.
11.2: ἦν δὲ Μαριὰμ ἡ ἀλείψασα τὸν κύριον μύρῳ — "Now it was Mary who had anointed the Lord with ointment." The aorist aleipsasa (G218, anointing) refers proleptically to the event the Evangelist will narrate in chapter 12. The Evangelist inserts this identification here — before telling the story — presumably because Mary was already well-known in the community as "the one who anointed the Lord." ἐκμάξασα τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ ταῖς θριξὶν αὐτῆς — "and wiped his feet with her hair" — the detail specifies which Mary is meant; the anointing-with-hair is the identifying mark. Its placement here gives the subsequent conversation (especially Jesus's love for this household) a concrete, intimate context.
11.3: ἀπέστειλαν (aorist, G649) — the sisters "sent" the message, a deliberate act of reaching out rather than a casual report. The message itself is notable for what it does not ask: there is no request for Jesus to come, only the statement of the situation. κύριε, ἴδε ὃν φιλεῖς ἀσθενεῖ — "Lord, behold, he whom you love is sick." The verb phileis is G5368 (phileō), the love of personal affection and friendship — not G25 (agapaō). The sisters appeal to Jesus's personal bond with their brother. The Evangelist will use the weightier agapaō in his own narrative summary (v5), but the sisters' word choice reflects the intimacy of friendship rather than the covenantal dimension.
11.4: αὕτη ἡ ἀσθένεια οὐκ ἔστιν πρὸς θάνατον — "this sickness is not toward death." The Greek idiom pros thanaton means oriented toward death as its destination or end point — not that Lazarus won't die, but that death is not the final horizon of this sickness. The phrase deliberately echoes 1 John 5:16–17 ("sin not toward death"). ἀλλ᾽ ὑπὲρ τῆς δόξης τοῦ θεοῦ — "but for the glory of God." Hyper (G5228) with the genitive: "on behalf of" or "for the sake of." The goal clause that follows — ἵνα δοξασθῇ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ δι᾽ αὐτῆς — "so that the Son of God may be glorified through it" — uses the same verb (doxazō, G1392) the Evangelist uses throughout for Christ's glorification through crucifixion and resurrection (7:39; 12:16, 23; 13:31–32). The raising of Lazarus is already framed within the cross-and-resurrection trajectory.
11.5: ἠγάπα δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς τὴν Μάρθαν καὶ τὴν ἀδελφὴν αὐτῆς καὶ τὸν Λάζαρον — "now Jesus loved Martha and her sister and Lazarus." The verb here is agapaō (G25, imperfect — an ongoing, settled love), not the phileō of the sisters' message (v3). The Evangelist's deliberate shift to agapaō for his own summary broadens the love from friendship (phileō) to covenant-sustaining commitment. The imperfect ēgapa marks this as the enduring background condition against which the two-day delay of v6 will be read: the delay is not indifference.
11.6: ὡς οὖν ἤκουσεν ὅτι ἀσθενεῖ, τότε μὲν ἔμεινεν ἐν ᾧ ἦν τόπῳ δύο ἡμέρας — "when therefore he heard that he was sick, at that time he remained in the place where he was two days." The word oun (G3767, "therefore/then") immediately after "Jesus loved them" makes the delay a consequence of the love, not its contradiction. The deliberate two-day wait — while a friend lies dying — is the Johannine pattern of divine timing subordinating human urgency to a larger design (cf. 2:4; 7:6–8). Emeinen (G3306, "remained/stayed") is the Evangelist's verb of purposeful dwelling, not passive inaction.
11.7: μετὰ τοῦτο λέγει τοῖς μαθηταῖς — "after this he says to the disciples."
