The narrative temporal marker meta touto ("after this/these things") is one of the Evangelist's characteristic connectors between units (cf. 2:12; 3:22; 5:1; 6:1; 7:1). ἄγωμεν εἰς τὴν Ἰουδαίαν πάλιν — "let us go into Judea again." The hortatory subjunctive agōmen (G71) is Jesus issuing a summons, not proposing a discussion. The word palin ("again") is significant: the disciples heard "again" and immediately remembered the stones (v8). Return to Judea is return to danger.
11.8: νῦν ἐζήτουν σε λιθάσαι οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι — "the Jews were just now seeking to stone you." The imperfect ezētoun (G2212) frames the stoning as an ongoing, recent threat, not a past incident resolved. The adverb nyn ("now/just now") makes it immediate. πάλιν ὑπάγεις ἐκεῖ; — "you are going back there again?" The second palin in two verses: the disciples cannot understand the pattern of deliberate return into danger. Their objection is practically reasonable and theologically opaque — they do not yet see what Jesus sees.
11.9: οὐχὶ δώδεκα ὧραί εἰσιν τῆς ἡμέρας; — "Are there not twelve hours of the day?" The rhetorical question about the twelve-hour day draws on common knowledge of the Jewish and Greco-Roman day divided into twelve equal hours (dawn to dusk). ἐὰν τις περιπατῇ ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ, οὐ προσκόπτει — "if anyone walks in the day, he does not stumble." Proskoptō (G4350) — to strike against, stumble. The reason given: ὅτι τὸ φῶς τοῦ κόσμου τούτου βλέπει — "because he sees the light of this world." The walking-in-light/darkness metaphor from chapters 8–9 is reactivated: Jesus operates within the light of his appointed time (cf. 9:4–5). His movement into danger is calibrated walking in daylight, not reckless stumbling in darkness.
11.10: ἐὰν δέ τις περιπατῇ ἐν τῇ νυκτί, προσκόπτει — "but if anyone walks in the night, he stumbles." The contrast completes the antithetical parallel of v9. ὅτι τὸ φῶς οὐκ ἔστιν ἐν αὐτῷ — "because the light is not in him." The phrasing shifts from external light (v9: "the light of this world") to internal light: the one who has no light within stumbles regardless of external conditions. The Evangelist uses the same en autō construction as in 1:4 ("in him was life, and the life was the light of humanity"). The disciples' failure to understand Jesus's movement is itself an instance of stumbling for lack of the light within.
11.11: Λάζαρος ὁ φίλος ἡμῶν κεκοίμηται — "Lazarus our friend has fallen asleep." Kekoimētai (G2837, perfect passive) — "has fallen asleep and remains in that state." The perfect tense marks the sleep as a present condition with a completed onset. The sleep euphemism for death was widely used in both Jewish and Greco-Roman usage (cf. Ps 13:3 LXX; 1 Thess 4:13–15), but the Evangelist will immediately show that the disciples take the word at face value, forcing Jesus to speak plainly. ἵνα ἐξυπνίσω αὐτόν — "that I may wake him." Exypnizō (G1852) — to rouse from sleep, wake up. The purpose clause frames the journey as a mission of awakening, not a journey to mourn.
11.12: κύριε, εἰ κεκοίμηται σωθήσεται — "Lord, if he has fallen asleep, he will be saved/will get better." Sōthēsetai (G4982, future passive of sōzō) in the disciples' mouth means "he will recover/get well" — they take koimasthai as literal sleep and conclude that sleep indicates recovery rather than crisis. But sōzō in the Evangelist's register typically means far more: rescue, deliverance, salvation. The disciples' mundane use of the word anticipates what the raising will accomplish at a deeper level than they intend.
11.13: εἰρήκει δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς περὶ τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ — "but Jesus had spoken of his death." The pluperfect eirēkei (G2046) — "he had spoken" — positions the narrator's clarification as filling in what was latent in Jesus's words all along. ἐκεῖνοι δὲ ἔδοξαν ὅτι περὶ τῆς κοιμήσεως τοῦ ὕπνου λέγει — "but they thought that he was speaking of the repose of sleep." The noun koimēsis (G2838) — "taking of rest, sleep" — the disciples interpret the metaphor in the only register they can access. This misunderstanding is the Evangelist's technique of ironic miscommunication that illuminates what the crowd or disciples cannot yet see: Lazarus is not sleeping but dead, and Jesus is going to reverse what "dead" means.
11.14: τότε οὖν εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς παρρησίᾳ — "then therefore Jesus said to them plainly." Parrēsia (G3954, adverb) — "with openness, frankly, without figure." The same word Martha will use in v57 when people expect Jesus to speak plainly, and the same word the disciples request in 16:25, 29. Jesus sets aside the sleep metaphor entirely. Λάζαρος ἀπέθανεν — "Lazarus is dead." The aorist apethanen (G599) is blunt: completed action, unambiguous death. After two-day delay, after indirect speech about sleep, the plain word falls without elaboration.
11.15: χαίρω δι᾽ ὑμᾶς, ἵνα πιστεύσητε, ὅτι οὐκ ἤμην ἐκεῖ — "and I am glad for your sakes that I was not there, so that you may believe." Chairō (G5463) — joy, gladness. Jesus is glad he was absent — not because he is indifferent to the suffering, but because the death creates the condition for a sign that will produce deeper faith than a healing would have. The purpose clause hina pisteusēte (G4100, aorist subjunctive) frames the entire event as a faith-catalysis. The disciples already believe in some sense (1:49; 6:68); what they are about to believe will be at a different depth. ἀλλὰ ἄγωμεν πρὸς αὐτόν — "but let us go to him." The hortatory subjunctive again — Jesus leads into danger.
11.16: Θωμᾶς ὁ λεγόμενος Δίδυμος — "Thomas, called Didymus." The Evangelist provides the Greek equivalent: Didymos (G1324) = "twin." Thomas's name is always given with this translation note in the Fourth Gospel (11:16; 20:24; 21:2) — a feature unique to this Gospel. Ἄγωμεν καὶ ἡμεῖς ἵνα ἀποθάνωμεν μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "let us also go so that we may die with him." The word autou is ambiguous: "with him" could refer to Lazarus (dying alongside a dying friend) or Jesus (dying alongside the master who is heading to his death). The saying has the ring of courageous, if grim, loyalty. Thomas does not understand the resurrection that is coming, but he understands the danger and commits anyway — a complex portrait that prepares for his doubt in chapter 20.
11.17: εὗρεν αὐτὸν τέσσαρας ἤδη ἡμέρας ἔχοντα ἐν τῷ μνημείῳ — "he found him having been four days already in the tomb." The verb heuriskō (G2147, "to find") is used throughout John for the moment of significant discovery. The four-day detail (tetartaios, G5066, in v39) is not a casual chronological note. In Second Temple Jewish belief, the soul was thought to linger near the body for three days before permanently departing; on the fourth day, irreversible decay was presumed to have begun (the Leviticus Rabbah tradition; cf. also the concern Martha expresses in v39). Four days is not simply "more than three" — it is the socially established threshold of final, irreversible death. The Evangelist places Jesus's arrival precisely here.
11.18: ἦν δὲ ἡ Βηθανία ἐγγὺς τῶν Ἱεροσολύμων ὡς ἀπὸ σταδίων δεκαπέντε — "now Bethany was near Jerusalem, about fifteen stadia away." A stadion (G4712) is approximately 185–192 meters; fifteen stadia is roughly 1.8 miles (about 3 km). The geographical detail serves a narrative function: Jerusalem is close enough that the Jewish mourners have come out from the city (v19), and the events at Bethany will immediately reach the ears of the Pharisees (v46). The raising happens within easy report-range of the Sanhedrin.
11.19: πολλοὶ δὲ ἐκ τῶν Ἰουδαίων ἐληλύθεισαν — "many of the Jews had come." The pluperfect elelythisan (G2064) marks their arrival as prior to Jesus's — they are already there when he comes. παραμυθήσωνται αὐτὰς περὶ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ — "to console them concerning their brother." Paramytheomai (G3888, G3888) — to encourage, console, comfort; the word implies sustained presence alongside the grieving. Jewish mourning practices of the period included a seven-day formal mourning period (shiva) during which friends and community came to "sit with" the bereaved. The large crowd of mourners from Jerusalem is both a cultural reality and a narrative device: witnesses will be present for what happens next.
11.20: Μάρθα μὲν οὖν ὡς ἤκουσεν ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἔρχεται ὑπήντησεν αὐτῷ — "Martha therefore, when she heard that Jesus was coming, went to meet him." The verb hypantaō (G5221) — to go toward and meet someone on the way. Martha's immediate action to go out contrasts with Mary who "sat in the house" (ēsato en tō oikō). The contrast recapitulates the sisters' characteristic patterns seen in Luke 10:38–42 without the Evangelist needing to cite them: Martha acts, Mary waits.
11.21: κύριε, εἰ ἦς ὧδε οὐκ ἂν ἀπέθανεν ὁ ἀδελφός μου — "Lord, if you had been here my brother would not have died." The second-class conditional (ei ēs...ouk an apethanen) — "if you had been here [but you were not], he would not have died [but he did]." It is a statement of what did not happen, not a rebuke. Martha addresses it to Jesus's face as a fact, not an accusation. The conditional expresses faith: she believes Jesus's presence would have prevented the death. It does not yet express what she cannot conceive — that his absence was purposeful and that something greater than prevention is coming.
11.22: ἀλλὰ καὶ νῦν οἶδα ὅτι ὅσα ἂν αἰτήσῃ τὸν θεόν, δώσει σοι ὁ θεός — "but even now I know that whatever you ask God, God will give you." The word oida (G1492, "I know" — the perfect with present force) marks Martha's confidence as settled rather than tentative. The verb aitēsē (G154, aorist subjunctive) — "you should ask" — frames Jesus's relationship with God as petitionary, which is partially accurate but misses the identity claim Jesus is about to make. Martha believes Jesus has special access to God; she is not yet prepared for "I am the resurrection."
11.23: ἀναστήσεται ὁ ἀδελφός σου — "your brother will rise." The future indicative anastēsetai (G450) is deliberately unqualified. Jesus does not say "I will raise him" or "in a little while" — he says "he will rise," leaving the statement open enough that Martha reads it within her existing framework (resurrection at the last day, v24). The ambiguity is Johannine: the statement is true in the frame Martha hears it in, and far truer in the frame Jesus means it in. The gap between those two frames is the theological content of vv25–27.
11.24: οἶδα ὅτι ἀναστήσεται ἐν τῇ ἀναστάσει ἐν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ — "I know that he will rise in the resurrection at the last day." Martha's response places "the resurrection" (hē anastasis, G386) at the eschatological end point: tē eschatē hēmera — "the last day." This is the standard Pharisaic belief: resurrection is a corporate, future, end-of-age event (cf. Dan 12:2; 2 Macc 7:9). Martha's theology is orthodox and correct as far as it goes. But Jesus's I-AM claim in v25 does not contradict Pharisaic eschatology — it relocates resurrection from a future event to a present Person. Martha knows the doctrine; she is about to meet its ground of existence.
11.25: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἀνάστασις καὶ ἡ ζωή — "I am the resurrection and the life." The emphatic egō eimi (G1473 + G1510) formula with double predicate: both nouns carry the definite article, making each a specific, exclusive identification rather than a general description. Jesus does not say "I give resurrection" or "I bring life" but "I am the resurrection… I am the life." The resurrection Martha has placed at the last day is not merely something Jesus will do; it is what Jesus is. ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμέ, κἂν ἀποθάνῃ, ζήσεται — "the one who believes in me, even if he should die, will live." The present participle ho pisteuōn ("the one believing" — ongoing, characteristic faith) is paired with kan apothanē (G2579 + G599, aorist subjunctive — "even if he should die at some point"). Physical death is acknowledged as a real event, but it is no longer the final word for the one who believes.
11.26: καὶ πᾶς ὁ ζῶν καὶ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ οὐ μὴ ἀποθάνῃ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "and everyone living and believing in me will absolutely not die forever." The double negative ou mē (G3756 + G3364) with the aorist subjunctive is the strongest Greek idiom for absolute negation of a future event — not merely "probably not" but "under no circumstances." Eis ton aiōna ("for the age/into forever") reinforces the temporal scope. V25 spoke of those who have physically died; v26 speaks of those alive who believe. Together the two verses cover the entire spectrum: whether living or dead when Christ returns, the believer does not ultimately die. πιστεύεις τοῦτο; — "do you believe this?" The direct question moves the exchange from doctrine to personal confession.
11.27: ναί, κύριε, ἐγὼ πεπίστευκα ὅτι σύ εἶ ὁ Χριστὸς ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ὁ εἰς τὸν κόσμον ἐρχόμενος — "Yes, Lord, I have believed that you are the Messiah, the Son of God, the one coming into the world." The perfect pepistika (G4100) — "I have believed and the believing stands" — is the perfect of settled, established faith. Martha's confession is the most complete christological statement in the Gospel up to this point, comprising three identifications: ho Christos (Messiah, the anointed king), ho huios tou theou (Son of God, the unique relational title), and ho eis ton kosmon erchomenos ("the one coming into the world" — the expected figure of Mal 3:1; Ps 118:26). This threefold confession occurs here, before the raising, not after — Martha confesses without yet seeing. The Evangelist structures her faith as prior to the sign, not produced by it.
11.28: ἐφώνησεν Μαριὰμ τὴν ἀδελφὴν αὐτῆς λάθρᾳ — "she called her sister Mary secretly." Lathra (G2977) — secretly, quietly, away from others. Martha's deliberate summoning of Mary away from the crowd shows practical judgment: she does not want the mourners who might misunderstand or interfere. ὁ διδάσκαλος πάρεστιν καὶ φωνεῖ σε — "the Teacher is here and is calling for you." Martha addresses Mary with the title ho didaskalos (G1320) — "the Teacher" — the title by which Jesus was addressed by those outside the innermost circle (cf. Nicodemus in 3:2; the disciples in 13:13–14). The summoning by name is deliberate: Jesus is calling for Mary specifically.
11.29: ἐκείνη δὲ ὡς ἤκουσεν ἠγέρθη ταχὺ — "when she heard, she rose quickly." The adverb tachy (G5036) — swiftly, quickly — marks Mary's immediate responsiveness to the summons. The verb ēgerthē (G1453, aorist passive) — literally "she was raised up." The Evangelist's word choice for Mary's arising from her seated mourning position carries an echo of the vocabulary of resurrection, though the primary sense is simply rising to her feet.
11.30: οὔπω δὲ ἐληλύθει ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἰς τὴν κώμην — "now Jesus had not yet come into the village." The pluperfect elelythei (G2064) — had not yet arrived. He is still at the place outside the village where Martha met him. The detail is preparation for v31: the mourning crowd sees Mary leave and assumes she is going to the tomb. The Evangelist sets up the group's misinterpretation so that they follow her to Jesus accidentally, becoming witnesses to what follows.
11.31: νομίσαντες ὅτι ὑπάγει εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον ἵνα κλαύσῃ ἐκεῖ — "thinking that she was going to the tomb to weep there." Nomizō (G3543, aorist participle) — to suppose, assume, think. Their assumption is culturally plausible: Mary has risen quickly from mourning and is moving with purpose. The verb klausē (G2799, aorist subjunctive of klaiō) — to weep, wail. The mourning-cry is the same verb used in v33 for the weeping of Mary and the Jews that Jesus witnesses. Their misunderstanding is not foolish — they have no reason to think otherwise. But it means they follow her to the place of miracle as accidental witnesses, drawn by the expectation of continued mourning.
11.32: ἔπεσεν αὐτοῦ πρὸς τοὺς πόδας — "she fell at his feet." The verb piptō (G4098, aorist) — to fall, fall prostrate. The posture of falling at feet in the Greco-Roman and Jewish world signals deep submission, supplication, or worship (cf. 9:38 where the healed man "worshiped" Jesus). Mary's posture is simultaneously the posture of grief and the posture of a suppliant before one with power to act. κύριε, εἰ ἦς ὧδε οὐκ ἄν μου ἀπέθανεν ὁ ἀδελφός — Mary's words are identical to Martha's in v21, with only the pronoun order varied (mou placed before adelphos). The two sisters arrive at exactly the same statement of belief, expressed separately. Their shared faith formulation — Jesus's presence would have prevented death — is the unanimous testimony of the household.
11.33: ὡς εἶδεν αὐτὴν κλαίουσαν καὶ τοὺς συνελθόντας αὐτῇ Ἰουδαίους κλαίοντας — "when he saw her weeping and the Jews who had come with her weeping." The verb klaiō (G2799, G2799) — loud weeping, wailing — is used for the mourning of Mary and the crowd. ἐνεβριμήσατο τῷ πνεύματι καὶ ἐτάραξεν ἑαυτόν — "he was deeply moved in spirit and troubled himself." Embrimaomai (G1690) is not a gentle emotion word. Its primary sense in Greek is to snort with anger, to be indignant, to groan with strong feeling — used elsewhere in the NT for Jesus rebuking a leper (Mark 1:43) and for the stern admonition not to tell (Mark 14:5). The word carries force close to controlled outrage or deep indignation. Tarassō (G5015) — to stir up, disturb, trouble — used in John for the stirring of the Bethesda pool (5:4, 7) and for Jesus's troubled spirit before the cross (12:27; 13:21). Jesus is not merely sorrowful: he is shaken in a way the text deliberately intensifies.
11.34: ποῦ τεθείκατε αὐτόν; — "where have you laid him?" Tithēmi (G5087, perfect — "where have you placed him and he remains placed") — the same verb Jesus uses in chapter 10 for "laying down" his life. Here it is the bystanders who have "laid" Lazarus in the tomb. The question is not for information — as v41–42 will make clear, Jesus already knows what he is about to do — but it draws the crowd along as participants in the scene, witnesses who will lead him to the very spot.
11.35: ἐδάκρυσεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς — "Jesus wept." This is the shortest verse in the New Testament in Greek: two words. The verb dakryō (G1145) is distinct from klaiō (G2799), the word used for Mary's and the crowd's weeping in v33. Klaiō is loud mourning-wailing; dakryō is the shedding of tears, quieter and more restrained. The crowd's grief is open and vocal; Jesus's is inward and visible only in tears. The Evangelist reports it without commentary. The theological freight is considerable: the one who just declared himself "the resurrection and the life" stands before a tomb in tears. His knowledge of what he is about to do does not prevent the grief. Both are simultaneously true.
11.36: ἴδε πῶς ἐφίλει αὐτόν — "see how he loved him." The bystanders read the tears as proof of love. The verb is phileō (G5368, imperfect — "he used to love him/he loved him") — the same word the sisters used in v3 for the love they appealed to. The crowd names what they see: a man weeping for a friend he loved. Their reading is not wrong. But it is incomplete: the grief is the full, felt grief of one who loves; it does not cancel the power he is about to exercise.
11.37: οὐκ ἐδύνατο οὗτος ὁ ἀνοίξας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς τοῦ τυφλοῦ ποιῆσαι ἵνα καὶ οὗτος μὴ ἀποθάνῃ; — "Could not this man who opened the eyes of the blind have caused even this man not to die?" The rhetorical question links back to chapter 9 — the healing of the blind man is the bystanders' point of reference for Jesus's capacity to act. The implicit logic is: if he could do that, why not this? The question is theologically sharper than it appears: they are pointing toward the right conclusion (he had the power) while implicitly charging failure or absence. What they cannot conceive is that the failure to prevent the death was the design of the sign, not a gap in his power.
11.38: Ἰησοῦς οὖν πάλιν ἐμβριμώμενος ἐν ἑαυτῷ — "Jesus therefore again groaning/deeply moved within himself." The Evangelist repeats embrimaomai (G1690) from v33, now as a present participle — the intense emotion is ongoing as Jesus approaches the tomb. Palin ("again") marks the recurrence of the same state, intensified by proximity to the tomb. ἔρχεται εἰς τὸ μνημεῖον· ἦν δὲ σπήλαιον καὶ λίθος ἐπέκειτο ἐπ᾽ αὐτῷ — "he comes to the tomb; it was a cave and a stone was lying against it." The description matches the tomb of Jesus in 19:41–20:1: a cave, a stone. The parallel is not incidental — the Evangelist shapes the Lazarus narrative as a precursor to the resurrection narrative, using the same topographical and physical details.
11.39: αἴρατε τὸν λίθον — "take away the stone." The aorist imperative arate (G142) — a direct command. Jesus does not move the stone himself; he involves those present. Martha's objection follows immediately: κύριε, ἤδη ὄζει· τετραήμερος γάρ ἐστιν — "Lord, by now there is an odor, for he has been four days." Ozō (G3605, present) — "to smell, stink." The objection is not a failure of faith — Martha has just confessed Jesus as Messiah and Son of God — but an eruption of physical realism. The four-day detail (tetarteaios, G5066) re-emphasizes the burial datum of v17. Martha is saying: the body is past the point of recovery. The objection makes the miracle that follows more vivid, not less. There is no ambiguity about what state Lazarus is in.
11.40: οὐκ εἶπόν σοι ὅτι ἐὰν πιστεύσῃς ὄψῃ τὴν δόξαν τοῦ θεοῦ; — "Did I not tell you that if you believe, you will see the glory of God?" The question refers back not to a word spoken in this chapter directly, but to the message of v4 (the sickness is for God's glory) and the encounter of vv25–26 (faith and life). Pisteusēs (aorist subjunctive of pisteuō) — the believing is the condition; opsē (G3700, future middle — "you will see") is the consequence. The sequence is faith → sight, not sight → faith. Martha must take the stone away before she sees the glory. The Johannine reversal of the usual order is a consistent pattern (cf. 20:29: "blessed are those who have not seen and have believed").
11.41: ἦρεν οὖν τὸν λίθον — "so they took away the stone." The obedience is reported simply, without further comment on Martha's hesitation. Ἰησοῦς δὲ ἦρεν τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς ἄνω — "Jesus lifted his eyes upward." The gesture of lifting the eyes before prayer is a physical posture of prayer in Jewish practice (cf. Ps 121:1; John 17:1). πάτερ, εὐχαριστῶ σοι ὅτι ἤκουσάς μου — "Father, I thank you that you have heard me." The prayer is thanksgiving, not petition. The hearing is in the past tense (ēkousas, G191, aorist) — the Father has already granted the request. This is prayer reported as already-answered, which frames the raising as the execution of a divine decision previously made, not a request about to be made.
11.42: ἐγὼ δὲ ᾔδειν ὅτι πάντοτέ μου ἀκούεις — "and I knew that you always hear me." The pluperfect form ēdein (G1492, "I had known and knew") marks the knowledge as prior and ongoing. ἀλλὰ διὰ τὸν ὄχλον τὸν περιεστῶτα εἶπον — "but because of the crowd standing around I said this." The prayer was not for Jesus's own benefit — he already operates within the unity of will and hearing described in 10:30 and 5:19–20 — but for the crowd's benefit. ἵνα πιστεύσωσιν ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας — "so that they may believe that you sent me." The goal of the public prayer is the same goal stated for the event itself in v4 and v15: faith. The sending-language (apesteilan, G649) is the Evangelist's central christological shorthand — to believe in the one God sent is to rightly orient oneself toward God.
11.43: ταῦτα εἰπὼν φωνῇ μεγάλῃ ἐκραύγασεν — "having said these things, he cried out with a loud voice." Kraugazō (G2905, aorist) — to shout, cry out loudly. The same verb is used in 18:40 and 19:6, 15 for the crowd crying out for crucifixion. The contrast is stark: the same kind of voice that will be raised against Jesus is here raised to give life. Λάζαρε, δεῦρο ἔξω — "Lazarus, come out!" The address is personal and direct: a name, and a two-word command. Deuro (G1204) — "come here" — combined with exō (G1854) — "outside/out." The command is notable for what it is not: not a petition, not an invocation, not a formula. Jesus summons the dead man by name as a shepherd calls his own sheep (cf. 10:3: "he calls his own sheep by name").
11.44: ἐξῆλθεν ὁ τεθνηκὼς δεδεμένος τοὺς πόδας καὶ τὰς χεῖρας κειρίαις — "the dead man came out, bound hand and foot with linen strips." The participle ho tethnēkōs (G2348, perfect — "the one who has died and remains dead") identifies the figure even as he moves: he is the dead man walking out. Keiriai (G2750) — burial wrappings, linen strips. The detail of the wrapped condition is both realistic (he would have been bound for burial) and preparatory: what the burial wrappings signify (death) is now worn by a walking man. λύσατε αὐτὸν καὶ ἄφετε ὑπάγειν — "loose him and let him go." Two imperatives: lysate (G3089, to loosen, unbind) and aphete (G863, to release, let go). Jesus gives life; the community participates in setting the living man free. The burial clothes must be removed by human hands — the communal dimension of restoration from death is built into the command.
11.45: πολλοὶ οὖν ἐκ τῶν Ἰουδαίων οἱ ἐλθόντες πρὸς τὴν Μαριὰμ καὶ θεασάμενοι ἃ ἐποίησεν ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν — "many therefore of the Jews who had come to Mary and had seen what he did believed in him." Theaomai (G2300, aorist participle) — to see, behold, witness closely. The word is more deliberate than the common blepō: it implies looking with attention. Episteusan eis auton (G4100, aorist — "believed into him") — the Johannine formula for saving faith, directed toward a person rather than merely cognitive assent to a proposition. Those who witnessed the raising believed. The Evangelist records this as the immediate response, before noting the negative reaction in v46.
11.46: τινὲς δὲ ἐξ αὐτῶν ἀπῆλθον πρὸς τοὺς Φαρισαίους καὶ εἶπαν αὐτοῖς ἃ ἐποίησεν Ἰησοῦς — "but some of them went away to the Pharisees and told them what Jesus had done." The same verb aperchomai (G565, aorist) for departure — some go toward belief, some go away with a report. Both groups witnessed the same event (ha epoiēsen Iēsous, "what Jesus had done" — identical phrasing in both vv45 and 46). The sign divides: it produces faith in some and, through the report, precipitates the plot to kill Jesus. This is the Johannine pattern of krisis — the sorting that occurs when the light enters the world (3:19–21).
11.47: συνήγαγον οὖν οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι συνέδριον — "the chief priests and the Pharisees gathered a council/Sanhedrin." Sunedrion (G4892) — the Sanhedrin, the ruling council of 71 members comprising chief priests, elders, and scribes. This is the formal body that will adjudicate Jesus's fate. τί ποιοῦμεν, ὅτι οὗτος ὁ ἄνθρωπος πολλὰ σημεῖα ποιεῖ; — "what are we doing, for this man is performing many signs?" The question is practical, not theological — they do not contest the signs. Polla sēmeia poiei — "performs many signs" — the concession that the miracles are real is built into the formulation of the problem.
11.48: ἐὰν ἀφῶμεν αὐτὸν οὕτως, πάντες πιστεύσουσιν εἰς αὐτόν — "if we leave him alone in this way, everyone will believe in him." The conditional is third-class: a real possibility requiring action. καὶ ἐλεύσονται οἱ Ῥωμαῖοι καὶ ἀροῦσιν ἡμῶν καὶ τὸν τόπον καὶ τὸ ἔθνος — "and the Romans will come and take away both our place and our nation." The double concern — topos (G5117, place, often used for the Temple mount in Second Temple usage) and ethnos (G1484, nation) — is the Sanhedrin's institutional anxiety: Roman reprisal for a messianic movement. The calculation is entirely political. The irony is that precisely this outcome occurred in 70 CE, though triggered not by Jesus's movement but by armed revolt — and the Evangelist, writing after 70, almost certainly notes the reversal.
11.49: εἷς δέ τις ἐξ αὐτῶν Καϊάφας, ἀρχιερεὺς ὢν τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ ἐκείνου — "but a certain one of them, Caiaphas, being high priest of that year." The repeated emphasis on "that year" (tou eniautou ekeinou, G1763 + G1565) in vv49, 51 is a Johannine marker: the year is significant — the year of the Passover when Jesus died. ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε οὐδέν — "you know nothing at all." The double negative (ouk oidate ouden) is contemptuous dismissal: Caiaphas cuts off the deliberation with a rebuke of his colleagues' competence. His confidence is the confidence of political realism, not theological insight — and yet v51 will explain that he spoke more truly than he knew.
11.50: συμφέρει ὑμῖν ἵνα εἷς ἄνθρωπος ἀποθάνῃ ὑπὲρ τοῦ λαοῦ — "it is expedient for you that one man should die on behalf of the people." Sympherō (G4851) — to be useful, beneficial, expedient; to be in the common interest. The verb is the language of political calculation and utilitarian ethics. Hyper tou laou ("on behalf of/for the sake of the people") — the preposition hyper is precisely the preposition Paul and the Evangelist use for substitutionary atonement (cf. John 6:51; 10:11, 15; 1 John 2:2). Caiaphas uses the atonement-language of the gospel as a coldly political justification for judicial murder. The Evangelist's irony is exact: the high priest's political formula becomes, by divine providence, a theological statement he did not intend.
11.51: τοῦτο δὲ ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ οὐκ εἶπεν — "but he did not say this of his own accord." The Evangelist interrupts the narrative to explain what the reader must not miss: the words were not Caiaphas's own. ἀλλὰ ἀρχιερεὺς ὢν τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ ἐκείνου ἐπροφήτευσεν — "but being high priest of that year, he prophesied." Prophēteuō (G4395, aorist) — to prophesy, speak as a prophet. This is a theological claim of extraordinary force: Caiaphas, while speaking cynical political calculation, was functioning as a prophet of God. The Evangelist's view is that God overrode the intent of the speaker and made the office of high priest, in that particular year, the vehicle of genuine prophetic utterance. The content (hoti Iēsous emellen apothnēskein hyper tou ethnous — "that Jesus was about to die on behalf of the nation") is taken by the narrator as a true statement of what was about to happen.
11.52: καὶ οὐχ ὑπὲρ τοῦ ἔθνους μόνον, ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα καὶ τὰ τέκνα τοῦ θεοῦ τὰ διεσκορπισμένα συναγάγῃ εἰς ἕν — "and not only on behalf of the nation, but also that he might gather together the scattered children of God into one." The Evangelist expands Caiaphas's prophecy beyond its stated scope: the death is not merely for Israel but for ta tekna tou theou ta dieskorpismena — "the scattered children of God." Diaskorpizō (G1287, perfect passive participle) — "scattered/dispersed" — the same root as in 10:12 where the wolf scatters (diaskorpizei) the sheep. The good shepherd who lays down his life for the scattered sheep (10:15–16) gathers them into one flock. The dying and the gathering are the two sides of the same act. Eis hen — "into one" — a single unity, the same hen of 10:30 and 17:21–23.
11.53: ἀπ᾽ ἐκείνης οὖν τῆς ἡμέρας ἐβουλεύσαντο ἵνα ἀποκτείνωσιν αὐτόν — "from that day therefore they plotted that they might kill him." Bouleuō (G4823, aorist middle) — to deliberate, take counsel, plan. The aorist marks the beginning of a settled decision. Hina apokteinōsin (purpose clause, G615, aorist subjunctive) — the goal of their deliberation is his death. The sign that raised a dead man becomes the catalyst for the plot to kill him. The irony is structural: the chapter that climaxes with the reversal of death ends with the decision to bring death. The raising of Lazarus and the plotting against Jesus belong to the same moment.
11.54: Ἰησοῦς οὖν οὐκέτι παρρησίᾳ περιεπάτει ἐν τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις — "Jesus therefore no longer walked openly among the Jews." Parrēsia (G3954, boldness, openness) — the word Martha used of Lazarus's hope (v24), the word the disciples asked for (16:25). Public, open movement is over. ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὴν χώραν ἐγγὺς τῆς ἐρήμου, εἰς Ἐφραὶμ λεγομένην πόλιν — "he went away to the region near the wilderness, to a town called Ephraim." Erēmos (G2048) — the wilderness, a recurrent site of divine withdrawal and preparation in biblical narrative. κἀκεῖ ἔμενεν μετὰ τῶν μαθητῶν — "and he stayed there with the disciples." Meno (G3306) — the purposeful dwelling verb again. The time until Passover is spent in deliberate obscurity.
11.55: ἦν δὲ ἐγγὺς τὸ πάσχα τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "now the Passover of the Jews was near." The Evangelist marks the third Passover in the Gospel (cf. 2:13; 6:4) with this formula. ἀνέβησαν πολλοὶ εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα ἐκ τῆς χώρας πρὸ τοῦ πάσχα — "many went up to Jerusalem from the country before the Passover." The verb anabainō (G305, to go up) is the standard idiom for the pilgrimage journey to Jerusalem, which is elevated geography. The purpose given — ἵνα ἁγνίσωσιν ἑαυτούς — "to purify themselves" — specifies the pre-festival purification requirement. Pilgrims who had contracted ritual impurity during travel needed to undergo purification before participating in Passover. The Evangelist is setting the stage: pilgrims are gathered, purification is underway, the festival that will become the frame for Jesus's death is approaching.
11.56: ἐζήτουν οὖν τὸν Ἰησοῦν καὶ ἔλεγον μετ᾽ ἀλλήλων ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ ἑστηκότες — "they were seeking Jesus and saying to one another as they stood in the temple." The imperfect ezētoun and elegon — ongoing action, repeated conversation. The crowd standing in the temple courts are actively looking for him and debating among themselves. τί δοκεῖ ὑμῖν; ὅτι οὐ μὴ ἔλθῃ εἰς τὴν ἑορτήν; — "What do you think? That he will certainly not come to the festival?" The double negative ou mē eltēi (G3756 + G3361, aorist subjunctive) used as a question: "you don't think he's really going to show up, do you?" The crowd's uncertainty reflects their awareness that the authorities are looking for him — and yet they also want to see him, evidenced by their looking.
11.57: δεδώκεισαν δὲ οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι ἐντολάς — "now the chief priests and the Pharisees had given orders." The pluperfect dedōkeisan (G1325) — "had given and the orders stood." The commands were prior to the festival gathering. ἵνα ἐάν τις γνῷ ποῦ ἐστιν μηνύσῃ — "that if anyone knew where he was, he should report it." Mēnuō (G3377) — to disclose, inform, report; sometimes used for informing on someone to the authorities. The mechanics of surveillance are put in place: the crowd is obligated to act as informants. The chapter ends with a crowd looking for Jesus in the temple, under orders to report him, while he is deliberately absent. The stage for chapters 12–19 is fully set.

17.1: ταῦτα ἐλάλησεν — "these things he spoke" — aorist, closing the farewell discourse (chs. 14–16) before the prayer begins. ἐπάρας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν — "having lifted his eyes toward heaven" — a posture of direct address attested in synagogue prayer and the Psalms; Jesus speaks to the Father face-to-face, not through an intermediary formula. ἐλήλυθεν ἡ ὥρα — "the hour has come" — perfect tense, elēlythen: the arrival of the hour is a completed event whose results now stand. This is the hour repeatedly deferred in the Gospel (2:4; 7:30; 8:20) and now declared present. The two doxazō imperatives — "glorify your Son, that the Son may glorify you" — are aorist, marking the mutual glorification as a single completed act, not an ongoing disposition.
17.2: καθὼς ἔδωκας αὐτῷ ἐξουσίαν πάσης σαρκός — "just as you gave him authority over all flesh." Exousia (G1849) is delegated authority — a granted right, not mere capacity. Pasēs sarkos — "all flesh" — the Semitic idiom for all humanity without exception (cf. Gen 6:12; Isa 40:5). The scope is universal; the exercise is particular: "so that to all those you have given him, he might give eternal life." δέδωκας (perfect, "you have given") — the giving stands as a completed and present reality; Jesus prays over an already-constituted people.
17.3: αὕτη δέ ἐστιν ἡ αἰώνιος ζωή — "And this is eternal life." The Evangelist — or Jesus within the prayer — offers a formal definition, a rare move in the Gospel. αἰώνιος (G166, dispute_level 3): the adjective derives from aiōn (age/era), and carries both qualitative ("of the age to come," the divine life-order) and quantitative ("without end") senses. The MKT renders it "eternal" but the qualitative dimension is primary: this is the life of the new age, not merely endless duration. ἵνα γινώσκωσίν σε — "that they may know you" — present subjunctive, ongoing knowing, not a single act of recognition. Ginōskō in John carries relational freight: the same word used for the knowing between Father and Son (10:15), and between husband and wife in the LXX. τὸν μόνον ἀληθινὸν θεόν — "the only true God" — alēthinos (G228) means "real/genuine" as against counterfeit; not merely the only God numerically, but the God who is authentically what God is.
17.4: ἐγώ σε ἐδόξασα ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς — "I glorified you on the earth" — aorist, looking back over the entire ministry as a completed act. The initiative is Jesus's: he brought glory to the Father, not the reverse. τὸ ἔργον τελειώσας — "having completed/perfected the work" — aorist participle of teleioō (G5048), to bring to its destined end. The same root appears in the great cry from the cross, tetelestai (19:30): what is here prayed in anticipation ("having finished the work you gave me") is declared accomplished at death. The prayer is proleptic — Jesus speaks of the cross as already complete.
17.5: νῦν δόξασόν με σύ, πάτερ, παρὰ σεαυτῷ τῇ δόξῃ ᾗ εἶχον — "And now, Father, glorify me alongside yourself with the glory that I had." Para seautō — "alongside yourself / in your presence" — the preposition denotes proximity and personal relation, not merely location. πρὸ τοῦ τὸν κόσμον εἶναι παρὰ σοί — "before the world was, alongside you" — the pre-existence of the Son stated explicitly: the glory requested is a return to a state that antedated creation. The construction pro tou + infinitive marks temporal priority: the Son's shared glory precedes cosmogony. This is the most direct statement of the Son's pre-existence in the prayer and one of the clearest in the Gospel (cf. 1:1; 8:58).
17.6: ἐφανέρωσά σου τὸ ὄνομα τοῖς ἀνθρώποις — "I revealed your name to the people." Phaneroō (G5319, aorist) is the Evangelist's verb for making visible what is otherwise concealed (cf. 1:31; 2:11; 9:3). "Your name" in Jewish understanding is not a label but a disclosure of character and presence — the divine shem. Jesus claims to have made the Father's name-reality visible through his ministry. οὓς δέδωκάς μοι ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου — "those you gave me out of the world" — perfect, the Father's giving is a standing reality. τὸν λόγον σου τετήρηκαν — "they have kept your word" — perfect, their keeping also stands. Two perfects balance each other: the gift given and the reception completed.
17.7: νῦν ἔγνωκαν ὅτι πάντα ὅσα δέδωκάς μοι παρὰ σοῦ εἰσίν — "Now they have known that all things, whatever you have given me, are from you." Egnōkan — perfect of ginōskō: they have arrived at and now rest in this knowledge. The insight is singular: everything Jesus has — his words, works, authority, disciples — originates from the Father. The chain of origin (Father → Son → disciples) is the disciples's growing theological understanding that the prayer consolidates.
17.8: τὰ ῥήματα ἃ ἔδωκάς μοι δέδωκα αὐτοῖς — "the words you gave me I have given to them." The distinction between rhēma (individual spoken word/utterance) and logos (the Word as self-disclosure or teaching body) matters here: rhēmata are the specific verbal utterances transmitted down the chain. ἔλαβον (aorist, "they received"), ἔγνωσαν (aorist, "they knew"), ἐπίστευσαν (aorist, "they believed") — three aorists in sequence mark three completed acts that together constitute the disciples' reception: they received the words, recognized their origin, and committed to it by faith.
17.9: οὐ περὶ τοῦ κόσμου ἐρωτῶ — "I am not praying for the world." The present tense erōtō ("I am asking") marks this as the active scope of the prayer at this moment, not an absolute exclusion — the Evangelist has already shown Jesus speaking of his love for the world (3:16; 12:32). The restriction is strategic: the prayer focuses on those already given to him, the visible community who will carry the mission. περὶ ὧν δέδωκάς μοι — "for those you have given me" — perfect, the given ones are constituted. σοί εἰσιν — "they are yours" — predicate without emphasis; their belonging to the Father is the ground of the prayer.
17.10: καὶ τὰ ἐμὰ πάντα σά ἐστιν καὶ τὰ σὰ ἐμά — "and all mine is yours, and yours mine." The mutual possession is stated with remarkable compression — no verb in the second clause, the reciprocity made absolute. This mutual ownership of all things is the ontological ground of the Son's prayer: he prays as one who shares everything with the Father. δεδόξασμαι ἐν αὐτοῖς — "I have been glorified in them" — perfect passive: the disciples are already the locus where the Son's glory has landed and remains visible. The prayer is not for glory not yet given but for the protection of a glory already present in a fragile vessel.
17.11: Ἅγιε πάτερ — "Holy Father" — a unique address in the NT. Jesus elsewhere addresses God simply as Pater or Abba; the addition of Hagie (G40, "holy") creates a title that invokes the divine attribute that most fully belongs to God alone. Holiness here grounds the petition: the one who is holy is asked to guard those set apart for him. τήρησον αὐτοὺς ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί σου — "keep them in/by your name" — tēreō (G5083) is "guard/keep watch over" — protective keeping, not mere maintenance. ἵνα ὦσιν ἕν καθὼς ἡμεῖς — "that they may be one as we are." Hen — neuter, "one thing" — unity of kind, not numerical identity (which would be heis, masculine). The grammar preserves both unity and distinction in the divine relationship, and the disciples' unity is modeled on it, not identical to it.
17.12: ἐγὼ ἐτήρουν αὐτοὺς ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί σου — "I was keeping them in your name" — imperfect, continuous past action: the keeping was Jesus's ongoing activity throughout the ministry. οὐδεὶς ἐξ αὐτῶν ἀπώλετο εἰ μὴ ὁ υἱὸς τῆς ἀπωλείας — "none of them was lost except the son of destruction." Apōleia (G684) — destruction, perdition — gives Judas his epithet: he is defined by the outcome, the one who was heading toward destruction. The exception clause (ei mē) does not cancel the claim of keeping; it marks Judas as outside the group being described. ἵνα ἡ γραφὴ πληρωθῇ — "that the scripture might be fulfilled" — the fulfillment formula anchors the loss of Judas within providential necessity; the Evangelist probably has Ps 41:9 in view (cf. 13:18).
17.13: νῦν δὲ πρὸς σὲ ἔρχομαι — "But now I am coming to you" — present tense, proleptic: Jesus speaks of the cross-and-return as an imminent present movement. ἵνα ἔχωσιν τὴν χαρὰν τὴν ἐμὴν πεπληρωμένην ἐν ἑαυτοῖς — "that they may have my joy fulfilled in themselves." Peplerōmenēn — perfect passive participle, "having been made full/complete": the joy the disciples are to share is not an installment but the complete measure of Jesus's own joy. The farewell discourse in ch. 16 promised that joy-in-sorrow would be converted to a joy no one can take; this prayer confirms the goal.
17.14: ἐγὼ δέδωκα αὐτοῖς τὸν λόγον σου — "I have given them your word" — perfect, the giving stands. ὁ κόσμος ἐμίσησεν αὐτούς — "the world hated them" — aorist, the hatred is a historical response already incurred. ὅτι οὐκ εἰσὶν ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου καθὼς ἐγὼ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου — "because they are not out of the world just as I am not out of the world." The ek preposition marks source or origin: the disciples no longer derive their identity and orientation from the world-system, just as Jesus does not. This is the theological ground of the world's hostility.
17.15: οὐκ ἐρωτῶ ἵνα ἄρῃς αὐτοὺς ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου — "I am not asking that you take them out of the world." The prayer explicitly refuses withdrawal. ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα τηρήσῃς αὐτοὺς ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ — "but that you keep them from the evil one." Tou ponērou — genitive of ponēros (G4190): the masculine form is decisive — this is "the evil one" (a person), not "evil" (an abstract). The same construction appears in the Lord's Prayer (Matt 6:13). The petition is not for isolation but for guarded engagement: in the world but protected from the one who rules it (cf. 12:31; 14:30; 16:11).
17.16: ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ εἰσίν, καθὼς ἐγὼ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ εἰμί — verbatim repetition of v. 14b. In the Evangelist's style, repetition is rarely decorative; it marks the thesis being held constant while the surrounding argument develops. Here the repeated declaration of the disciples' non-world-derivation frames the petition of v. 15 on both sides, ensuring the reader understands the basis for the protection request.
17.17: ἁγίασον αὐτοὺς ἐν τῇ ἀληθείᾳ — "Sanctify them in/by the truth." Hagiazō (G37, aorist imperative): the verb's semantic range spans ceremonial consecration (setting apart for holy use), ritual purification, and moral transformation. All three are operative here: the disciples are being set apart from the world-system (v. 16), purified for service, and morally shaped by what is true. ὁ λόγος ὁ σὸς ἀλήθειά ἐστιν — "your word is truth." Not "true" (alēthēs) but "truth" (alētheia) without the article — the word does not merely correspond to truth; it is the substance of truth. The Evangelist has already identified Jesus as "the truth" (14:6); now the Father's word shares that characterization.
17.18: καθὼς ἐμὲ ἀπέστειλας εἰς τὸν κόσμον, κἀγὼ ἀπέστειλα αὐτοὺς εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "Just as you sent me into the world, I also sent them into the world." The sending of the disciples is explicitly modeled on the Father's sending of the Son: same verb (apostellō, G649), same direction (into the world), same syntax. This is the Fourth Gospel's theological grounding of mission: the disciples' apostolate participates in the structure of the divine sending, not merely in its content. The aorist "I also sent" treats the commissioning as already accomplished — cf. 20:21.
17.19: ὑπὲρ αὐτῶν ἐγὼ ἁγιάζω ἐμαυτόν — "for their sake I sanctify myself." The formula is priestly and sacrificial: the high priest consecrates himself before presenting an offering on behalf of others. Jesus is here both priest and offering — he sets himself apart for the purpose of his own sacrifice. Hagiazō emauton — the reflexive "sanctify myself" — occurs nowhere else in the NT; it is a unique claim. ἵνα ὦσιν καὶ αὐτοὶ ἡγιασμένοι ἐν ἀληθείᾳ — "that they too may be sanctified in truth" — perfect passive participle hēgiasmenoi: the result of his self-consecration is their standing consecration, an accomplished state, not merely an ongoing process. En alētheia here is probably instrumental: by means of truth (the word, cf. v. 17), and/or in genuine reality (as against merely ceremonial consecration).
17.20: Οὐ περὶ τούτων δὲ ἐρωτῶ μόνον — "I do not pray only for these." The scope of the prayer expands beyond the immediate disciples to encompass all future believers. ἀλλὰ καὶ περὶ τῶν πιστευόντων διὰ τοῦ λόγου αὐτῶν — "but also for those who believe through their word." Dia tou logou autōn — through their word/message: the chain of transmission is explicit — Father → Son → disciples → their word → future believers. The present participle pisteuontōn ("those believing") includes all who come to faith through the apostolic message, which by the Evangelist's own account (20:31) includes the Gospel itself.
17.21: ἵνα πάντες ἕν ὦσιν — "that all may be one." Hen — neuter, as in v. 11 — the same grammatical form maintains consistency: the unity prayed for is participatory, not numerical. καθὼς σύ, πάτερ, ἐν ἐμοί, κἀγὼ ἐν σοί — "just as you, Father, are in me and I in you" — the mutual indwelling (perikhōrēsis in later theological terminology) is the model, not just the analogy. ἵνα καὶ αὐτοὶ ἐν ἡμῖν ὦσιν — "that they also may be in us" — the goal is believers' participation in the Trinitarian fellowship, not mere social unity. ἵνα ὁ κόσμος πιστεύῃ — the unity of believers is missionary in purpose: it is to be visible enough to convince the world of the Son's divine sending.
17.22: κἀγὼ τὴν δόξαν ἣν δέδωκάς μοι δέδωκα αὐτοῖς — "And I have given them the glory that you have given me." Both verbs are perfect: the Father's giving of glory to the Son and the Son's giving of that glory to the disciples are both completed and standing. The glory given to the disciples is not merely honor or reputation but the self-revealing radiance of the divine presence — the same doxa that the Evangelist says "we beheld" (1:14). ἵνα ὦσιν ἕν καθὼς ἡμεῖς ἕν — "that they may be one as we are one" — neuter throughout; the unity given by glory is qualitative, not numerical fusion.
17.23: ἐγὼ ἐν αὐτοῖς καὶ σὺ ἐν ἐμοί, ἵνα ὦσιν τετελειωμένοι εἰς ἕν — "I in them and you in me, that they may be perfected into one." Teteleiōmenoi eis hen — perfect passive participle with eis of goal or result: "brought to completion into oneness." The verb teleioō (G5048) carries its characteristic Johannine sense of completing, bringing to intended purpose. The unity is not static but the telos toward which the community is being directed. ἵνα γινώσκῃ ὁ κόσμος ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας — "so that the world may know that you sent me" — the missionary claim again: the visible completion of the community is the apologetic argument for the Son's divine origin.
17.24: θέλω — "I will / I desire" — not the prayer-verb erōtaō used throughout (vv. 9, 15, 20) but the volitional thelō (G2309): "I will it, I want it." The shift marks this verse as something other than petition — it is a statement of the Son's determined will addressed to the Father. ἵνα ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγὼ κἀκεῖνοι ὦσιν μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ — "that where I am, they may also be with me" — the eschatological goal: the disciples' presence with Jesus where he is (cf. 14:3: "that where I am you may also be"). ἵνα θεωρῶσιν τὴν δόξαν τὴν ἐμήν — "that they may behold my glory" — theōreō (G2334), the seeing of prolonged direct observation, not a glance. The glory is the pre-existent glory of v. 5. The petition is for the disciples' eschatological vision of what they could not fully see during the ministry.
17.25: Πάτερ δίκαιε — "Righteous Father" — a second unique address in the prayer, balancing "Holy Father" in v. 11. Dikaios (G1342): just, righteous — the attribute that governs v. 25's claim: God's justice will vindicate the disciples' knowledge of Jesus over against the world's ignorance. ὁ κόσμος σε οὐκ ἔγνω — "the world did not know you" — aorist, a settled verdict on the world's history of not-knowing. ἐγὼ δέ σε ἔγνων — "but I knew you" — same aorist, a simple historical fact about the Son's consistent knowing of the Father. καὶ οὗτοι ἔγνωσαν ὅτι σύ με ἀπέστειλας — "and these knew that you sent me" — the disciples stand with the Son in knowing, differentiated from the world.
17.26: καὶ ἐγνώρισα αὐτοῖς τὸ ὄνομά σου καὶ γνωρίσω — "and I have made your name known to them, and I will make it known." The movement from aorist (egnōrisa) to future (gnōrisō) is significant: Jesus's disclosure of the Father's name is both a completed ministry achievement and a continuing work — the making-known will continue through the Spirit (cf. 16:13–15) and through the community's witness. ἵνα ἡ ἀγάπη ἣν ἠγάπησάς με ἐν αὐτοῖς ᾖ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτοῖς — "that the love with which you loved me may be in them, and I in them." The prayer closes with the ultimate goal: the Father's love for the Son is to take up residence in the disciples. Agapē (G26) here is not the disciples' love but the divine love itself — the inner-Trinitarian love — relocating into the believing community. The final phrase "and I in them" is the mechanism: Christ's indwelling is how the divine love enters and remains.

18.1: τοῦ χειμάρρου τῶν Κέδρων — "the Kidron torrent/valley." Cheimarros (G5493) denotes a wadi — a seasonal streambed that runs with water in winter rains and dries in summer. The name Kedrōn derives from the Hebrew Qidrōn (darkness/black), associated with the brook David crossed when fleeing Absalom (2 Sam 15:23) — a detail likely not accidental to the Evangelist. κῆπος — "garden" — the Synoptics name it Gethsemane; John uses the garden detail, which will recur at the burial (19:41) and resurrection (20:15). The garden as the site of the arrest and the tomb as a garden signals an Edenic counternarrative: the second Adam entering the garden where the first Adam fell.
18.2: ᾔδει δὲ καὶ Ἰούδας ὁ παραδιδοὺς αὐτόν τὸν τόπον — "Now Judas, the one betraying him, also knew the place." The participle ho paradidous — "the one delivering over/betraying" — is present, characterizing Judas by his ongoing function, not merely the act. Paradidōmi (G3860) carries the double register of handing over (to authorities) and betrayal (of trust); the Evangelist uses it without softening. The explanation — Jesus had met there often with his disciples — underscores that the garden was a place of intimacy now turned into a trap by insider knowledge.
18.3: τὴν σπεῖραν — "the cohort/detachment." Speira (G4686) is the Greek term for a Roman military unit, normally a cohort of 480–600 soldiers; in practice "a detachment" here may indicate a maniple or smaller unit. The presence of Roman soldiers (alongside temple police and officials) from the outset in John's account is theologically significant: the arrest is under the joint authority of Roman empire and Jewish leadership — the powers of this world act together. μετὰ φανῶν καὶ λαμπάδων καὶ ὅπλων — "with torches and lanterns and weapons" — the detail is ironic: they come with artificial light to arrest the Light of the world, with weapons to arrest the one who will lay down his life voluntarily.
18.4: Ἰησοῦς οὖν εἰδὼς πάντα τὰ ἐρχόμενα ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν — "Jesus, therefore, knowing all the things coming upon him." Eidōs — perfect participle of oida (G1492), the verb of intuitive or complete knowing, not the relational ginōskō. The Evangelist stresses Jesus's sovereign foreknowledge: the arrest does not happen to a surprised victim but to one who foreknew it and chose it. ἐξῆλθεν — "went out" — Jesus takes the initiative, coming to meet the arresting party rather than waiting to be seized.
18.5: Ἰησοῦν τὸν Ναζωραῖον — "Jesus the Nazarene" — the geographical designation used at the arrest, the trial placard (19:19), and the post-resurrection announcement (20:15; cf. Acts 2:22). ἐγώ εἰμι — the absolute I-AM without predicate. In Greek, "I am he" would be egō eimi autos or simply egō; the bare egō eimi carries the register of the divine self-disclosure formula of Exod 3:14 LXX and the Isaianic "I AM" declarations (Isa 43:10 LXX: egō eimi). The Evangelist has used it at 8:24, 8:28, 8:58; here it is the operative term that causes the arrest party to fall back. The parenthetical note about Judas standing with them maximizes the irony: the betrayer is present when the divine name drops them to the ground.
18.6: ἀπῆλθον εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω καὶ ἔπεσαν χαμαί — "they went back and fell to the ground." Chamai (G5476, "to the ground") appears only here and at 9:6 (where Jesus spits on the ground to make clay); the rare word links the healing of the blind man and the arrest — divine power operates at ground level. The falling backward of the armed detachment at the utterance of egō eimi is a theophany response: comparison with the prostrations at divine appearances in the OT (Dan 8:17; Ezek 1:28; Rev 1:17) makes clear the Evangelist's intent. The arresting party cannot arrest Jesus; he gives himself over.
18.7: Τίνα ζητεῖτε — the question repeated verbatim from v. 4. The Evangelist's repetition is deliberate: after the display of divine authority in the I-AM and the falling backward, Jesus asks again the same question. The soldiers and officials must answer again — "Jesus the Nazarene" — and receive the same reply. The repetition is a literary device of patient sovereignty: Jesus controls the arrest scene even while submitting to it.
18.8: εἶπον ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι — "I told you that I am he." The I-AM formula is now restated plainly. εἰ οὖν ἐμὲ ζητεῖτε, ἄφετε τούτους ὑπάγειν — "if then you seek me, let these go" — aphete (aorist imperative of aphiēmi, G863): release, let go. The Evangelist portrays Jesus as negotiating the terms of the arrest, securing the disciples' freedom as a condition — a detail unique to the Fourth Gospel. This connects to the "good shepherd" motif of ch. 10: the shepherd gives his life and the sheep go free.
18.9: ἵνα πληρωθῇ ὁ λόγος ὃν εἶπεν — "that the word he had spoken might be fulfilled." The Evangelist uses the standard fulfillment formula (hina plērōthē) but here applies it not to a scripture but to Jesus's own prior word (17:12: "I have not lost one of those you gave me"). Jesus's words have the same fulfillment-authority as Scripture — a significant claim that runs through the Johannine passion narrative.
18.10: Σίμων οὖν Πέτρος ἔχων μάχαιραν εἵλκυσεν αὐτήν — "Simon Peter therefore, having a sword, drew it." Machaira (G3162): a short sword or large knife, the standard personal sidearm. τοῦ ἀρχιερέως τὸν δοῦλον — "the high priest's servant." Only the Fourth Gospel names the servant: Μάλχος (G3124). The naming is a marker of concrete tradition — the Evangelist or his source knew who Malchus was. The specific ear named (tēn dexian, the right ear) adds further precision. ἀπέκοψεν αὐτοῦ τὸ ὠτάριον τὸ δεξιόν — "cut off his right ear" — ōtarion (G5621): diminutive of ous, perhaps "ear-lobe" rather than the whole ear.
18.11: Βάλε τὴν μάχαιραν εἰς τὴν θήκην — "Put your sword into the sheath." The command is a present-tense imperative (bale can also be aorist), but the gesture is unmistakable: the sword is sheathed. τὸ ποτήριον ὃ δέδωκέν μοι ὁ πατήρ, οὐ μὴ πίω αὐτό — "the cup that the Father has given me, shall I not drink it?" Potērion (G4221) — the cup is the Gethsemane language present in the Synoptics (Matt 26:39; Mark 14:36; Luke 22:42) but absent from John's Gethsemane-equivalent. The Evangelist introduces it here instead, as Jesus accepts the arrest: the cup metaphor for divinely-assigned suffering and death (cf. Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17; Jer 25:15). The double negative ou mē piō (strong negation with subjunctive) is a rhetorical question expecting the answer "of course I will drink it."
18.12: ἡ σπεῖρα καὶ ὁ χιλίαρχος καὶ οἱ ὑπηρέται τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "the cohort and the commander and the officers of the Jews" — three parties to the arrest: Roman soldiers, their commanding officer (chiliarchos, G5506, lit. "commander of a thousand," a tribune), and Jewish temple police. The binding of Jesus (ἔδησαν, aorist: they bound him) occurs immediately — the legal formality of custody asserted. Jesus goes bound through the rest of the trial narrative.
18.13: Ἄνναν πρῶτον — "to Annas first." The sequence — Annas before Caiaphas — is unique to John. Annas had been high priest from AD 6–15; Caiaphas (his son-in-law) held the office AD 18–36. Annas retained enormous political influence and is addressed or referred to as high priest by both Luke (Luke 3:2; Acts 4:6) and John. The appearance before Annas is a preliminary examination — the factual hearing before the formal proceeding at Caiaphas's house. ὁ πενθερὸς τοῦ Καϊάφα — "the father-in-law of Caiaphas" — a kinship detail explaining the first transfer.
18.14: ἦν δὲ Καϊάφας ὁ συμβουλεύσας τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ὅτι συμφέρει ἕνα ἄνθρωπον ἀπολέσθαι ὑπὲρ τοῦ λαοῦ — "It was Caiaphas who had advised the Jews that it was expedient for one man to die for the people." The Evangelist reaches back to 11:50, where Caiaphas's pragmatic counsel was identified as inadvertent prophecy. Sympherō (G4851): "to be expedient/profitable" — the political calculus of one death for national preservation. The Evangelist quotes this without comment, allowing the irony to work: the man who authorized the arrest is the one whose political prophecy the trial is now fulfilling in ways he did not intend.
18.15: ὁ ἄλλος μαθητής — "the other disciple" — the Beloved Disciple, unnamed throughout the Gospel (13:23; 19:26; 20:2; 21:7,20). γνωστὸς τῷ ἀρχιερεῖ — "known to the high priest" — gnōstos (G1110): one who is known, a recognized acquaintance. How the Beloved Disciple had this connection is unexplained; the tradition is taken as known to the original readers. This connection allows him to enter the aulē (courtyard/palace) of the high priest — the inner residential compound, not a public space.
18.16: εἱστήκει δὲ ὁ Πέτρος πρὸς τῇ θύρᾳ ἔξω — "Peter was standing at the door outside" — pluperfect heistēkei: he had taken up his standing position. The door between outside and inside becomes the spatial emblem of Peter's ambiguous position — he wants to follow but cannot enter, exactly as Jesus told him (13:36–38). ἡ παιδίσκη ἡ θυρωρός — "the girl who kept the door" — a female doorkeeper. The first challenge to Peter comes from the least threatening social figure imaginable: a servant girl. The irony of the arrest scene's powerful men falling at egō eimi while Peter caves at a doorkeeper's question is structurally intentional.
18.17: μὴ καὶ σὺ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν εἶ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τούτου — "You aren't also one of the disciples of this man, are you?" The construction with mē signals a question expecting a negative answer — the girl herself frames it as unlikely. οὐκ εἰμί — "I am not" — Peter's first denial is a stark reversal of the I-AM formula Jesus used in vv. 5–8. Where Jesus's egō eimi (I am) was the divine self-disclosure that felled soldiers, Peter's ouk eimi (I am not) is the self-protective denial. The Evangelist's juxtaposition is deliberate and devastating.
18.18: ἀνθρακιάν — "a charcoal fire." Anthrakia (G439) appears only twice in the NT: here (18:18) and at 21:9, where the risen Jesus has prepared a charcoal fire for the disciples on the shore. The rare word is a Johannine echo-marker: Peter warms himself at a charcoal fire during the denial; the risen Christ will meet Peter at a charcoal fire for the restoration. The verbal connection between the two scenes is not accidental. ἦν δὲ ψῦχος — "it was cold" — one of the Gospel's vivid physical details; the night cold explains the fire and sets the atmospheric register of the trial scenes.
18.19: ὁ οὖν ἀρχιερεὺς ἠρώτησεν τὸν Ἰησοῦν περὶ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ καὶ περὶ τῆς διδαχῆς αὐτοῦ — "The high priest then questioned Jesus about his disciples and about his teaching." Annas's two questions — about followers (potential insurgent movement) and teaching (possible blasphemy or sedition) — are the standard preliminary categories of a Jewish judicial inquiry. Didachē (G1322): teaching, doctrine. The Evangelist does not identify this as a formal Sanhedrin proceeding — the formal judgment was Caiaphas's (11:50) and the legal death-verdict will be in 19:6–7. This is an interrogation.
18.20: ἐγὼ παρρησίᾳ λελάληκα τῷ κόσμῳ — "I have spoken openly to the world." Parrēsia (G3954) — literally "all-speech," frank and public disclosure — is the legal claim Jesus makes: nothing was said in private that is not on public record. Lelalēka — perfect, "I have spoken and this speaking stands." ἐν συναγωγῇ καὶ ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ — "in synagogue and in the temple" — the two primary public institutions of Jewish religious life; Jesus's teaching venues were the most scrutinized and crowded spaces available. ἐν κρυπτῷ ἐλάλησα οὐδέν — "in secret I spoke nothing" — the privacy charge implicit in Annas's questioning is denied outright. Jesus is not a clandestine teacher of a secret sect.
18.21: τί με ἐρωτᾷς; ἐρώτησον τοὺς ἀκηκοότας — "Why do you question me? Ask those who have heard me." Jesus's reply invokes the standard Jewish legal principle: testimony must come from witnesses (Deut 19:15), not from self-incrimination. Tous akēkootas — perfect participle: "those who have heard and still have that hearing." The witnesses exist; the court should call them. This is a legally astute response, not mere deflection: Jesus is pointing to the correct procedure under Torah.
18.22: ῥάπισμα ἔδωκεν — "gave a slap/blow." Rapisma (G4475): a blow with an open hand or rod — in rabbinic legal contexts, striking a party under judicial examination was a contempt of procedure if the examination had not yet concluded. Οὕτως ἀποκρίνῃ τῷ ἀρχιερεῖ — "Is this how you answer the high priest?" — the official's intervention treats Jesus's appeal to witnesses as impudence rather than legal procedure. The blow is an assertion of institutional power in the face of a challenge to its process.
18.23: εἰ κακῶς ἐλάλησα, μαρτύρησον περὶ τοῦ κακοῦ — "If I spoke wrongly, testify about the wrong." Kakōs (G2560): wrongly, badly — moral or factual wrongness. The conditional is first class (ei + aorist indicative), treating the condition as factually stated without assuming its truth: "if I spoke wrongly (which I did not) — prove it." εἰ δὲ καλῶς, τί με δέρεις — "but if rightly, why do you strike me?" Kalōs (G2573): rightly, well. Jesus' response to the blow is a demand for procedural justice: violence is not argumentation. The two conditionals — kakōs / kalōs — are clean legal logic rather than a refusal to cooperate.
18.24: ἀπέστειλεν οὖν αὐτὸν ὁ Ἄννας δεδεμένον πρὸς Καϊάφαν τὸν ἀρχιερέα — "Annas therefore sent him bound to Caiaphas the high priest." Dedemenon — perfect passive participle, "bound/having been bound" — the binding from v. 12 is still in effect. Annas transfers jurisdiction to the official high priest for the formal proceedings. The brevity of the Evangelist's account of both preliminary hearings (the Synoptics give more detail of the Caiaphas proceedings) reflects the Fourth Gospel's focus: the theological weight of the trial falls on the Pilate exchange, not on the Jewish court.
18.25: Μὴ καὶ σὺ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ εἶ — "You aren't also one of his disciples, are you?" The question form is identical to v. 17 — same mē construction anticipating denial — now from the servants and officials standing by the charcoal fire. Peter is asked to declare his allegiance in the very social group where belonging to Jesus would be most costly. ἠρνήσατο ἐκεῖνος καὶ εἶπεν· Οὐκ εἰμί — "he denied and said, I am not" — the same ouk eimi as v. 17. This is the second denial.
18.26: εἷς ἐκ τῶν δούλων τοῦ ἀρχιερέως, συγγενὴς ὢν οὗ ἀπέκοψεν Πέτρος τὸ ὠτίον — "one of the high priest's servants, being a relative of the one whose ear Peter cut off." The third challenge is the most specific: a kinsman of Malchus, who presumably recognized Peter from the garden. The question — "Didn't I see you in the garden with him?" — is virtually an accusation; it has the force of an eyewitness identification. Ouk egō se eidon — "Did I not see you?" — double negative with egō for emphasis. Peter has no credible denial left; the kinship connection makes a lie nearly impossible to sustain.
18.27: πάλιν οὖν ἠρνήσατο Πέτρος — "Peter denied again" — the third denial, narrated with minimal drama; the Evangelist's restraint is its own commentary. καὶ εὐθέως ἀλέκτωρ ἐφώνησεν — "and immediately a cock crowed." Eutheōs (G2112): immediately — the temporal marker confirms the fulfillment of Jesus's prediction (13:38: "before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times"). The cock crow is the structural endpoint of the Peter narrative in the passion; the Evangelist returns to the courtroom. The Johannine account of Peter's breakdown contains no weeping (contrast Matt 26:75; Luke 22:62) — Peter's restoration will come separately (21:15–19).
18.28: ἄγουσιν τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἀπὸ τοῦ Καϊάφα εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον — "they lead Jesus from Caiaphas to the praetorium." Praitōrion (G4232): the Roman governor's official residence and judgment hall in Jerusalem — either the Antonia Fortress or Herod's Palace (scholars dispute). The Greek term transliterates the Latin praetorium. αὐτοὶ δὲ οὐκ εἰσῆλθον εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον, ἵνα μὴ μιανθῶσιν — "they themselves did not enter the praetorium, so that they might not be defiled." The defilement concern is Passover purity: contact with a Gentile dwelling (understood as containing leaven and perhaps corpse-impurity) would render them unable to eat the Passover meal. The irony the Evangelist deploys is sharp — they guard their ritual purity while handing over an innocent man to death. Miainō (G3392): to stain, defile.
18.29: ἐξῆλθεν οὖν ὁ Πιλᾶτος ἔξω πρὸς αὐτούς — "Pilate therefore went outside to them." The spatial choreography of the trial — inside/outside the praetorium — is the Evangelist's structural device. Pilate must repeatedly cross the threshold because the accusers will not enter; the back-and-forth movements (vv. 29, 33, 38; 19:1, 4, 9, 13) dramatize the governor caught between Roman jurisdiction inside and Jewish pressure outside. Τίνα κατηγορίαν φέρετε κατὰ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τούτου — "What accusation do you bring against this man?" — the standard Roman judicial opening: the accuser must state the charge.
18.30: εἰ μὴ ἦν οὗτος κακοποιός, οὐκ ἄν σοι παρεδώκαμεν αὐτόν — "If this man were not a criminal, we would not have handed him over to you." The construction is second-class conditional (past contrary to fact): "if he were not [which he is]." The Jewish leaders do not actually state a charge — they assert the verdict and ask Pilate to ratify it. Kakopoios (G2555): evil-doer, criminal — a vague accusation that avoids the specifics they know Pilate would need to adjudicate.
18.31: Λάβετε αὐτὸν ὑμεῖς καὶ κατὰ τὸν νόμον ὑμῶν κρίνατε αὐτόν — "Take him yourselves and judge him by your own law." Pilate's retort pushes the matter back: if the charge falls under Jewish law, Jewish courts should handle it. οὐκ ἔξεστιν ἡμῖν ἀποκτεῖναι οὐδένα — "it is not permitted for us to put anyone to death." Exestin (G1832): it is lawful, permitted — a legal claim about Roman provincial authority. Crucifixion (as opposed to stoning) was a Roman death-penalty; Rome had reserved capital punishment for itself in Judea (a debated but likely historical reality under the procuratorship). The Jewish leaders want crucifixion specifically, which only Pilate can authorize.
18.32: ἵνα ὁ λόγος τοῦ Ἰησοῦ πληρωθῇ ὃν εἶπεν σημαίνων ποίῳ θανάτῳ ἤμελλεν ἀποθνῄσκειν — "that the word of Jesus might be fulfilled, which he spoke, signifying by what death he was going to die." The fulfillment notice applies to Jesus's own word — specifically the hypsōthēnai (being lifted up) sayings (3:14; 8:28; 12:32–33). Sēmainō (G4591): to signal, indicate — the same verb used in Rev 1:1 for symbolic revelation. Crucifixion is the "lifted up" death; stoning (the Jewish alternative) is not. The Evangelist's fulfillment formula here confirms that divine necessity governs the specific mode of execution.
18.33: εἰσῆλθεν πάλιν εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον ὁ Πιλᾶτος καὶ ἐφώνησεν τὸν Ἰησοῦν — Pilate goes back inside and summons Jesus privately — the spatial movement marks the beginning of the interior hearing that will be the theological center of the trial. Σὺ εἶ ὁ βασιλεὺς τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "Are you the king of the Jews?" The question carries the political charge: a king of the Jews is a rival to Caesar and a threat to Roman order. Basileus (G935): king, ruler — in Pilate's mouth a political category; in Jesus's answer it will be redefined.
18.34: Ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ σὺ τοῦτο λέγεις ἢ ἄλλοι εἶπόν σοι περὶ ἐμοῦ — "Do you say this from yourself, or did others tell you about me?" Jesus's counter-question is not evasion; it is a juridical clarification. The distinction between Roman and Jewish categories of "king" is decisive: if Pilate is asking about a political insurrectionist (his own concern), the answer is no; if he is repeating the Jewish leaders' theological charge, the question needs reframing. Jesus forces Pilate to locate the question before answering it.
18.35: Μήτι ἐγὼ Ἰουδαῖός εἰμι — "I am not a Jew, am I?" — mēti with egō emphatic: a scornful rhetorical question. Pilate disavows any stake in Jewish messianic categories. τὸ ἔθνος τὸ σὸν καὶ οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς παρέδωκάν σε ἐμοί — "Your own nation and the chief priests handed you over to me" — Pilate names the accusers and distances himself from their charge while acknowledging his own position as the decision-maker they require. τί ἐποίησας — "What have you done?" — Pilate's actual question: what is the concrete act being charged?
18.36: ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου — "My kingdom is not from this world." The critical preposition: ek tou kosmou toutou — "out of / from this world" — marks origin and source, not location. Jesus is not claiming his kingdom does not operate within the world but that it does not derive from the world's power structures. εἰ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἦν ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμή, οἱ ὑπηρέται οἱ ἐμοὶ ἠγωνίζοντο ἄν — "if my kingdom were of this world, my servants would be fighting" — second-class conditional: contrary to fact. The test is armed resistance: world-kingdoms defend themselves by force. His servants are not fighting. ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐντεῦθεν — "my kingdom is not from here" — enteuthen (from here) closes the definition: the kingdom's source is elsewhere, its operation is therefore by different means.
18.37: Οὐκοῦν βασιλεὺς εἶ σύ — "So you are a king!" — oukoun is an inferential particle: Pilate draws the conclusion from Jesus's own words. Jesus accepts the designation with a qualification: σὺ λέγεις ὅτι βασιλεύς εἰμι — "you say that I am a king" — neither full affirmation nor denial, but an acknowledgment that the word is Pilate's category and the reality it points to is Jesus's. ἐγὼ εἰς τοῦτο γεγέννημαι καὶ εἰς τοῦτο ἐλήλυθα εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "for this I have been born and for this I have come into the world" — perfect tenses: the birth and the coming are standing facts. The double purpose is truth-bearing: ἵνα μαρτυρήσω τῇ ἀληθείᾳ — "that I might witness to the truth." Martyreō (G3140): to witness, testify — the language of the law court now applied to Jesus's entire mission. πᾶς ὁ ὢν ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας ἀκούει μου τῆς φωνῆς — "everyone who is of the truth hears my voice" — ek tēs alētheias: out of / deriving from truth — the same source-preposition as the kingdom in v. 36. Belonging to truth is an ontological status, not a moral achievement.
18.38: Τί ἐστιν ἀλήθεια — "What is truth?" Pilate's famous question has been read as philosophical skepticism, rhetorical dismissal, weary pragmatism, or genuine inquiry. The Evangelist gives no answer — Pilate exits without waiting for one. The question lands in the reader's ear as the supreme irony of the trial: the one who is "the truth" (14:6) stands before the man who asks "what is truth?" and receives no answer because none is sought. οὐδεμίαν εὑρίσκω ἐν αὐτῷ αἰτίαν — "I find no charge/fault in him." Aitia (G156): cause, accusation, legal ground — Pilate's formal verdict of innocence is delivered three times in John (18:38; 19:4; 19:6). The repetition is juridically and theologically deliberate.
18.39: ἔστιν δὲ συνήθεια ὑμῖν ἵνα ἕνα ἀπολύσω ὑμῖν ἐν τῷ πάσχα — "Now there is a custom for me to release one prisoner to you at the Passover." The Passover amnesty — the custom of releasing one prisoner at the feast — is attested in the Gospels (all four) but not in extra-biblical Jewish or Roman sources; scholars debate its historicity. The Evangelist's account uses it narratively to force the crowd into an explicit choice. βούλεσθε οὖν ἀπολύσω ὑμῖν τὸν βασιλέα τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "do you want me to release to you the king of the Jews?" — Pilate uses the royal title ironically and mockingly, but in doing so he names Jesus with the title that will go on the cross (19:19).
18.40: ἐκραύγασαν οὖν πάλιν λέγοντες· Μὴ τοῦτον ἀλλὰ τὸν Βαραββᾶν — "They shouted again saying, Not this one but Barabbas." Kraugazō (G2905): to cry out loudly — a crowd shout, not deliberation. ἦν δὲ ὁ Βαραββᾶς λῃστής — "Now Barabbas was a robber/bandit." Lēstēs (G3027) is the same word used in 10:1 for the one who enters the sheepfold as a thief rather than through the gate. The Evangelist's word choice is not accidental: the crowd releases a lēstēs and crucifies the gate-giver, the shepherd. The name Barabbas — bar (son of) + abba (father) — adds another layer: they choose the son of a human father over the Son of the Father.

12.1:
ἓξ ἡμέραι πρὸ τοῦ πάσχα — "six days before the Passover." The Evangelist's precise temporal marker ties Jesus' arrival at Bethany to the Passover calendar. Under Levitical practice the Passover lamb was selected on Nisan 10 — exactly six days before Nisan 14, the day of slaughter. The timing is unlikely to be accidental.

ὃν ἤγειρεν ἐκ νεκρῶν — "whom he raised from the dead," aorist of egeirō (G1453). The clause is explanatory — the Evangelist re-anchors Lazarus as the embodied evidence that draws the crowd (v.9), making Bethany the staging point for the final confrontation.
12.2:
ἐδιακόνει — imperfect of diakoneō (G1247): "was serving." The imperfect marks the service as ongoing throughout the meal, not a single act. Martha's characteristic activity (Luke 10:40 uses the same verb) appears here without contrast or rebuke — the dinner context is hospitable, not polemical.

ἀνακειμένων — present participle of anakeimai (G345), "reclining at table." Formal Passover preparation and Greco-Roman banquet practice both called for reclining, distinguishing festive meals from ordinary eating. Lazarus reclining alongside Jesus underlines the physical reality of the resurrection.
12.3:
λίτραν μύρου νάρδου πιστικῆς — litra (G3046) is the Roman pound (~327g, a large quantity). Nardos (spikenard, from the Himalayan plant Nardostachys jatamansi) was imported from India and priced accordingly. Pistikēs (G4101, related to pistis) most probably means "genuine" or "unadulterated" — a quality marker, not a place-name. The construction means "a pound of genuine, costly spikenard."

ἤλειψεν τοὺς πόδας — "anointed the feet," aleiphō (G218), the more common term for anointing with oil or perfume (as opposed to the sacred-appointment sense of chriō). Anointing feet rather than the head is unusual and underlines the extravagance and humility simultaneously.

ἐπλήρωθη ἡ οἰκία — "the house was filled" (plēroō, G4137, aorist passive). The filling of the house with fragrance is the Evangelist's detail that no other account shares — functioning as a sensory witness to the scene's excess.
12.4: ὁ μέλλων αὐτὸν παραδιδόναι — "the one about to betray him," present participle of mellō + infinitive of paradidōmi (G3860). The participle is progressive: the betrayal plan is already underway, not a future contingency. The Evangelist introduces Judas here, before his objection, to prime the reader to hear the "concern for the poor" as performance rather than conviction.
12.5: τριακοσίων δηναρίων — "three hundred denarii." A denarius was the standard daily wage for a laborer (Matt 20:2), making 300 denarii roughly a full year's earnings. Judas's calculation correctly identifies the extravagance, which is the point — the anointing is deliberately disproportionate.
12.6:
κλέπτης ἦν — "he was a thief," kleptēs (G2812), imperfect of eimi. The imperfect marks this as an established characteristic, not a new development. The Evangelist's retrospective comment here is the only place in the Gospels where Judas is called a thief outright.

τὸ γλωσσόκομον εἶχεν — "he had the money bag." Glōssokomon (G1101) originally referred to a case for mouthpieces of reed instruments, then repurposed for any small box or bag. The imperfect eichen again marks it as a continuing arrangement — Judas managed the group's finances habitually.

ἐβάσταζεν — "was stealing/carrying," bastazō (G941). The verb means both "to carry" (his legitimate role) and in Hellenistic usage "to pilfer" — the Evangelist deploys the ambiguity deliberately. Both senses are likely intended: he carried the bag and carried off its contents.
12.7:
