ἄφες αὐτήν — "leave her alone," aorist imperative of aphiēmi (G863), abrupt and final. The command overrides Judas entirely and defends Mary without explanation of her motives.

εἰς τὴν ἡμέραν τοῦ ἐνταφιασμοῦ τετήρηκεν αὐτό — "she has kept it for the day of my burial." Entaphiasmos (G1780, preparation for burial) occurs in NT only here and Matt 26:12. Tetērēken is perfect active of tēreō (G5083) — "she has kept/reserved it." Whether Mary consciously acted for the burial or Jesus reinterprets her act proleptically is intentionally ambiguous; the syntax allows both readings. What the Evangelist underlines is that the anointing is oriented toward death, not celebration.
12.8:
τοὺς πτωχοὺς πάντοτε ἔχετε μεθ' ἑαυτῶν — "the poor you always have with you," echoing Deut 15:11 ("for there will never cease to be poor in the land"). The allusion does not demean the poor or charitable care for them; it situates the present moment as exceptional — this particular act, at this particular moment, is unrepeatable.

ἐμὲ δὲ οὐ πάντοτε ἔχετε — "but me you do not always have." The contrast is temporal: the anointing is possible only now, before the death Jesus is moving toward.
12.9: ἔγνω ὄχλος πολύς — "a large crowd learned" (aorist of ginōskō, G1097). The crowd's motivation is split: not only Jesus but Lazarus — the Evangelist signals that the resurrection of Lazarus has become an independent attraction, complicating the authorities' attempts to manage public response.
12.10: ἵνα καὶ τὸν Λάζαρον ἀποκτείνωσιν — "in order that they might kill Lazarus also," purpose clause with aorist subjunctive of apokteinō (G615). The logic of silencing a living sign by killing it is grimly ironic — killing the one Jesus raised would only re-demonstrate the claim. The chief priests' decision is self-defeating, but fear overrides reason.
12.11:
ὅτι πολλοὶ δι᾽ αὐτὸν ὑπῆγον — "because many on account of him were going away," imperfect of hypagō (G5217). The imperfect marks a gradual, ongoing defection from the Jerusalem leadership's sphere of influence. Hypagō can mean simply "to go" but in Johannine usage often implies movement from one sphere to another (cf. 8:21).

ἐπίστευον εἰς τὸν Ἰησοῦν — "were believing in Jesus," imperfect again — the belief is spreading incrementally, not as a single event.
12.12:
τῇ ἐπαύριον — "the next day," marking the scene shift from the intimate dinner at Bethany to the public approach to Jerusalem. The movement from private to public is a Johannine structural feature across the passion week.

ὁ ὄχλος πολύς ὁ ἐλθὼν εἰς τὴν ἑορτήν — "the large crowd that had come to the festival." The antecedent crowd is the Passover pilgrimage crowd, distinct from the Bethany observers. Both groups converge for the triumphal entry.
12.13:
βαΐα τῶν φοινίκων — "branches of palm trees," baia (G902) appears only here in NT. Palms were carried at the Feast of Tabernacles (Lev 23:40) and were a symbol of Jewish national and military victory (1 Macc 13:51; 2 Macc 10:7). Waving palms at Passover time, when Tabernacles palms were not customary, is a politically charged gesture.

ὡσαννά — hōsanna (G5614), a transliteration of Hebrew הוֹשִׁיעָה נָּא, "save, pray!" (Ps 118:25). By the first century it had become a liturgical acclamation rather than a petition, roughly "hail!" or "praise God!" — though its original intercessory meaning likely lingered.

εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου — Ps 118:26 LXX, the Hallel psalm sung at Passover. The crowd adds ὁ βασιλεὺς τοῦ Ἰσραήλ (king of Israel), an interpretive expansion not in the psalm text, making explicit the messianic reading.
12.14:
εὑρὼν δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὀνάριον — "having found a young donkey," aorist participle; onarion (G3678) is a diminutive — "little donkey" — found only here in NT. The act of finding and riding is understated; the Evangelist is pointing the reader to the prophetic text rather than the logistics.

ἐκάθισεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτό — "sat on it," aorist of kathizō (G2523). The action fulfills the prophetic pattern; the brief narrative keeps attention on the significance, not the mechanics.
12.15:
Μὴ φοβοῦ, θύγατερ Σιών — "Do not fear, daughter Zion." The MT of Zech 9:9 reads "Rejoice greatly" (גִּילִי), but the LXX reads "rejoice" differently, and John's version "do not fear" may follow a variant LXX tradition or paraphrase. The address "daughter Zion" (a personification of Jerusalem) is retained.

ἐπὶ πῶλον ὄνου — "on a donkey's colt," pōlos (G4454, colt), the same term as the LXX of Zech 9:9. John uses onarion in v.14 but quotes the LXX's pōlos in the fulfillment citation — a common citation practice: narrative freedom, lexical precision in the quotation.
12.16:
οὐκ ἔγνωσαν αὐτοῦ οἱ μαθηταί — "his disciples did not understand these things at first," aorist of ginōskō. The disciples' incomprehension at significant moments is a recurring Johannine motif (cf. 2:22; 20:9).

ὅτε ἐδοξάσθη Ἰησοῦς — "when Jesus was glorified," aorist passive of doxazō (G1392). In John "glorification" encompasses crucifixion, resurrection, and ascension as a single movement. The post-resurrection retrospect enables the disciples to connect the entry with Zechariah's text.

γεγραμμένα — "written things," perfect passive participle of graphō: "the things standing written." The perfect tense is the Evangelist's standard way of marking scripture as permanently authoritative.
12.17: ἐμαρτύρει οὖν ὁ ὄχλος — "the crowd was therefore testifying," imperfect of martyreō (G3140). The witnesses to the Lazarus event spread their testimony, generating the crowd of v.18. Martyreō is a forensic term — the crowd is functioning as legal witnesses to what they saw.
12.18: διὰ τοῦτο καὶ ὑπήντησεν αὐτῷ ὁ ὄχλος — "for this reason the crowd met him," aorist of hypantaō (G5221). The Evangelist distinguishes the two crowds: those who went out because of what they heard Lazarus had been raised (v.18), and those who witnessed the event (v.17). Both groups testify, but their testimony has different epistemic weights.
12.19: ὁ κόσμος ὀπίσω αὐτοῦ ἀπῆλθεν — "the whole world has gone after him," aorist of aperchomai (G565). The Pharisees' frustrated hyperbole ("the whole world") is immediately followed by the arrival of Greeks (v.20), turning their exaggeration into unwitting prophecy. Kosmos in John's usage (cf. 3:16) carries precisely the universal scope the Pharisees dismiss as an idiom.
12.20: Ἕλληνές τινες — "certain Greeks," most likely Gentile God-fearers or proselytes who came up to worship at the festival rather than diaspora Greek-speaking Jews. The arrival of Gentiles at this precise moment functions as the external trigger for Jesus' declaration in v.23 that "the hour has come."
12.21:
Φιλίππῳ τῷ ἀπὸ Βηθσαϊδά — Philip from Bethsaida; both Philip and Andrew (v.22) bear Greek names, and Bethsaida was a border region with significant Gentile contact, making them natural intermediaries. The Evangelist's specificity here is not incidental — these disciples become the channel through which the Gentile approach reaches Jesus.

ἠρώτων αὐτόν — "they were asking him," imperfect — repeated or persistent asking. ἰδεῖν τὸν Ἰησοῦν — "to see Jesus." Theloō + horaō infinitive; in Johannine idiom "to see" often carries the weight of genuine encounter with Jesus' identity (cf. 1:39, 6:36).
12.22: ἔρχεται Φίλιππος καὶ λέγει τῷ Ἀνδρέᾳ — historical presents (erchomai, legō) vivify the relay. Philip does not go directly to Jesus but consults Andrew first — possibly reflecting uncertainty about how Jesus would receive Gentiles, or a chain-of-command dynamic within the Twelve.
12.23:
ἐλήλυθεν ἡ ὥρα — "the hour has come," perfect of erchomai (G2064): "it has come and is now here." Throughout John 2–11, "the hour" of Jesus was either not yet come (2:4; 7:30; 8:20) or approaching (4:21, 23; 5:25, 28). The Gentiles' request marks the transition: the universal mission Jesus is about to complete through death is now imminent.

ἵνα δοξασθῇ ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — purpose clause: "that the Son of Man might be glorified," aorist passive subjunctive of doxazō. The response to the Greeks' desire to "see" Jesus is not an audience but an announcement of cross-death as the very means of glorification.
12.24:
ὁ κόκκος τοῦ σίτου — "the kernel of wheat," kokkos sitou (G2848, G4621). The grain-of-wheat image is Jesus' own interpretation of his death — not merely loss but necessary precondition for fruitfulness. The logic is agricultural: buried seed is not wasted but productive.

ἐὰν μὴ πέσῃ εἰς τὴν γῆν καὶ ἀποθάνῃ — "unless it falls into the earth and dies," two aorist subjunctives in a conditional. Apothnēskō (G599) is used for both literal death and the grain's ending — the metaphor turns on the word.

μόνος μένει — "it remains alone," monos (G3441) + present of menō (G3306, to abide/remain). The contrast with polun karpon pherei (bears much fruit) is absolute: isolation vs. abundance, death vs. multiplication.
12.25:
ὁ φιλῶν τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ ἀπολλύει αὐτήν — "the one loving his life loses it." Psychē (G5590) appears in both clauses, creating the paradox that hinges on one word. Phileō (G5368, love with attachment) and miseō (G3404, hate) form the antithesis; but miseo here means "value less by comparison," a Semitic idiom of relative priority (cf. Luke 14:26).

εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον φυλάξει αὐτήν — "will keep it for eternal life," future of phylassō (G5442). The keeping in view of zōē aiōnios (G2222 + G166) is the governing purpose — the apparent self-destruction is actually preservation at the highest level.
12.26:
ἐμοί τις διακονῇ, ἐμοὶ ἀκολουθείτω — "if anyone serves me, let him follow me," present subjunctive + present imperative. Diakoneō (G1247) and akoloutheō (G190) are paired: service to Jesus is inseparable from the pattern of following him — including through death.

ὅπου εἰμὶ ἐγώ, ἐκεῖ καὶ ὁ διάκονος ὁ ἐμὸς ἔσται — "where I am, there also will my servant be." The ἐγώ is emphatic; the promise of co-location with Jesus is the reward of servant-following.

τιμήσει αὐτόν — "will honor him," future of timaō (G5091). The Father's honoring of Christ's servant is the direct counterpart of the shame/dishonor that public discipleship brought in an honor-shame culture.
12.27:
ἡ ψυχή μου τετάρακται — "my soul has been troubled," perfect of tarassō (G5015). The perfect marks a present state resulting from a past disturbance — Jesus is not merely observing a coming trouble but is already in it. The same verb appears at 11:33 (the tomb of Lazarus) and 13:21 (announcing the betrayal) — tarachthē marks the moments of deepest human cost in the narrative.

τί εἴπω; πάτερ, σῶσόν με ἐκ τῆς ὥρας ταύτης — the prayer-question is interrogative, not an actual petition: "What should I say? 'Father, save me from this hour'?" The rhetorical structure immediately overturns the possibility: ἀλλὰ διὰ τοῦτο ἦλθον εἰς τὴν ὥρα ταύτην — "but for this very reason I came to this hour." This is the Johannine equivalent of the Synoptic Gethsemane — the struggle is present but the submission is immediate.
12.28:
δόξασόν σου τὸ ὄνομα — "glorify your name," aorist imperative of doxazō. The request is not for deliverance but for the Father's glorification — a reorientation from v.27's implicit plea.

ἐδόξασα καὶ πάλιν δοξάσω — "I have glorified and will glorify again." The double aorist-future response from heaven (cf. John 12:30) encompasses past (the entire ministry) and future (the passion and exaltation). Edoxasa is aorist — a completed glorification; doxasō is future — the completing act is imminent.
12.29: βροντὴν γεγονέναι… ἄγγελος αὐτῷ λελάληκεν — "that it had thundered… that an angel had spoken to him." The perfect infinitives (gegonēnai from ginomai, lelalēken from laleō) report completed events. The crowd's divided response — thunder vs. angel — illustrates the Johannine motif of differential hearing: the voice was audible but only partially intelligible.
12.30: δι᾽ ὑμᾶς αὕτη ἡ φωνὴ γέγονεν, οὐ δι᾽ ἐμέ — "this voice has occurred for your sake, not for mine," perfect of ginomai. Jesus does not need the audible confirmation — it is for the witnesses. The statement implies that Jesus' own confidence in the Father's glorification does not depend on public divine speech.
12.31:
νῦν κρίσις ἐστὶν τοῦ κόσμου τούτου — "now is the judgment of this world," krisis (G2920) in its judicial sense. The cross is the moment of cosmic adjudication — not the final judgment of all things but the definitive crisis in which the world's allegiance is exposed and the ruler's power broken.

ὁ ἄρχων τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἐκβληθήσεται ἔξω — "the ruler of this world will be cast out," future passive of ekballō (G1544). Archōn (G758, ruler) is used for the devil/Satan in John (14:30; 16:11) — a supernatural power over the world system, whose authority is broken at the cross, not merely threatened.
12.32:
κἀγὼ ἐὰν ὑψωθῶ ἐκ τῆς γῆς — "and I, if I am lifted up from the earth," aorist passive subjunctive of hypsoō (G5312). Hypsoō in John carries double freight: literal elevation on a cross (3:14; 8:28) and figurative exaltation to glory. The cross is simultaneously degradation and enthronement in Johannine theology.

πάντας ἑλκύσω πρὸς ἐμαυτόν — "I will draw all people to myself," future of helkyō (G1670, to drag/draw). Pantas (all people, accusative plural) reaches beyond Israel — triggered precisely by the Greeks' arrival (vv.20–21). The drawing is not automatic universalism but a statement of intended scope; cf. 6:44 where the Father draws those who will come.
12.33: σημαίνων ποίῳ θανάτῳ ἤμελλεν ἀποθνῄσκειν — "signifying by what kind of death he was about to die," sēmainō (G4591, to indicate by sign or signal). The Evangelist's editorial comment connects "lifted up" (v.32) to crucifixion explicitly — completing the pattern established at 3:14 and 8:28.
12.34:
ἡμεῖς ἠκούσαμεν ἐκ τοῦ νόμου ὅτι ὁ Χριστὸς μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "we have heard from the Law that the Christ remains forever." The crowd's appeal to "the Law" invokes texts like Ps 89:36–37 and Isa 9:7 (the eternal Davidic throne). They cannot reconcile "lifted up" with eternal messianic reign.

τίς ἐστιν οὗτος ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — "who is this Son of Man?" The question reveals that the crowd understands "Son of Man" as a messianic title but cannot integrate the death-trajectory with it. Their nomos hermeneutic is correct in its data but wrong in its interpretive frame.
12.35:
ἔτι μικρὸν χρόνον τὸ φῶς ἐν ὑμῖν ἐστιν — "still a little time the light is among you," mikros (G3398) + chronos (G5550). Jesus does not answer the crowd's question directly but shifts to urgent appeal. The temporal pressure ("a little while") is a characteristic Johannine device (7:33; 13:33; 16:16).

περιπατεῖτε ὡς τὸ φῶς ἔχετε, ἵνα μὴ σκοτία ὑμᾶς καταλάβῃ — "walk while you have the light, so that darkness may not overtake you." Katalambanō (G2638, to seize/overtake) gives darkness an active, pursuing quality. The imperative peripateite (G4043, present — ongoing walking) frames discipleship as lived movement.
12.36:
πιστεύετε εἰς τὸ φῶς, ἵνα υἱοὶ φωτὸς γένησθε — "believe in the light, so that you may become sons of light," aorist subjunctive of ginomai. Hyioi phōtos (sons of light) is a Semitic idiom denoting people characterized by and belonging to the light — not just illuminated but constituted by it (cf. 1 Thess 5:5; Eph 5:8).

ἀπελθὼν ἐκρύβη ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν — "having gone away, he hid himself from them," aorist of kryptō (G2928). The withdrawal marks the close of Jesus' public teaching; chapters 13–17 are private. The hiddenness also fulfills 12:35's warning about the light's departure.
12.37: τοσαῦτα δὲ αὐτοῦ σημεῖα πεποιηκότος ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν οὐκ ἐπίστευον εἰς αὐτόν — "although he had done so many signs in their presence, they were not believing in him," imperfect of pisteuō. The perfect participle pepoiēkotos underlines the completed, abundant evidence; the imperfect episteuon marks the unbelief as an ongoing state in the face of it. Volume of evidence does not produce faith.
12.38: ἵνα ὁ λόγος Ἠσαΐου τοῦ προφήτου πληρωθῇ — "that the word/oracle of Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled." Logos (G3056, disp=3) here means "oracle" or "prophetic word" — not the metaphysical Logos of 1:1 but the spoken word of Isaiah. Isa 53:1 LXX is quoted essentially verbatim: κύριε, τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν — "Lord, who believed our report?" Akoē (G189) means both "hearing" and "report/message heard" — the LXX renders the Hebrew שְׁמֻעָתֵנוּ accurately.
12.39: διὰ τοῦτο οὐκ ἠδύναντο πιστεύειν — "for this reason they were not able to believe." Dynamai (G1410) + infinitive of pisteuō — a statement of incapacity, not merely unwillingness. The Evangelist is not eliminating human responsibility but is tracing the theological logic of Isaiah's text: the hardening is divinely permitted and scripturally anticipated.
12.40:
τετύφλωκεν αὐτῶν τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς — "he has blinded their eyes," perfect of typhloō (G5186). The divine subject of both verbs (blinds, hardens) in the Isa 6:10 citation is theologically difficult. Isa 6 depicts Isaiah's commissioning with a message that will produce judicial hardening — not that God wills unbelief as such but that the word of God exposes and confirms the orientation of the heart.

ἐπώρωσεν αὐτῶν τὴν καρδίαν — "has hardened their hearts," perfect of pōroō (G4456), from pōros (hard stone, callus). The perfect tense in both verbs signals a state now established, not an ongoing process. John's version of the Isa 6:10 quotation differs from the LXX and from Matt 13:14-15 — it may follow a different textual tradition or be a paraphrase.
12.41:
ταῦτα εἶπεν Ἠσαΐας ὅτι εἶδεν τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ — "Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory," aorist of horaō (G3708). The pronoun autou (his) refers to Jesus — the Evangelist's astonishing exegetical claim is that the vision of Yahweh enthroned in Isa 6:1-3 was a vision of Christ's pre-incarnate glory. This is one of the highest Christological statements in the Gospel, made almost in passing.

καὶ ἐλάλησεν περὶ αὐτοῦ — "and he spoke about him," aorist of laleō. The entire Isaiah 6 commission — including the hardening oracle — is understood as speech about Christ.
12.42:
ὅμως μέντοι καὶ ἐκ τῶν ἀρχόντων πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτόν — "nevertheless even many of the rulers believed in him." Homōs mentoi — a rare particle combination in NT (only here), meaning "nevertheless/nonetheless even so." The believing rulers are a significant qualification to the universal unbelief of vv.37–40.

διὰ τοὺς Φαρισαίους οὐχ ὡμολόγουν — "because of the Pharisees they were not confessing," imperfect of homologeō (G3670, to confess/declare openly). The fear is institutional: the Pharisees have authority to exclude (cf. 9:22).
12.43: ἠγάπησαν γὰρ τὴν δόξαν τῶν ἀνθρώπων μᾶλλον ἤπερ τὴν δόξαν τοῦ θεοῦ — "for they loved the glory of men more than the glory of God," aorist of agapaō (G25). The comparison mallon ēper (more than) reveals not absence of belief but divided loyalty. Doxa (G1391) appears twice — the same word, differently sourced: human approval vs. divine honor. In a shame/honor culture this is the ultimate diagnosis: miscalibrated honor attachment.
12.44:
Ἰησοῦς δὲ ἔκραξεν καὶ εἶπεν — "Jesus cried out and said," aorist of krazō (G2896, to cry out loud, often of urgent proclamation). The verb marks a public declaration rather than private teaching — a formal announcement after his withdrawal (v.36).

ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ οὐ πιστεύει εἰς ἐμὲ ἀλλὰ εἰς τὸν πέμψαντά με — "the one believing in me does not believe in me only, but in the one who sent me." The construction is not paradoxical — it is relational: faith in Jesus is not isolated to his person but reaches through him to the Father. Pempsanta (aorist participle of pempō, G3992, to send) specifies the Father's active commissioning.
12.45: ὁ θεωρῶν ἐμὲ θεωρεῖ τὸν πέμψαντά με — "the one seeing me sees the one who sent me," present participles. Theōreō (G2334) is the more intensive verb for seeing — to observe, behold, contemplate. The statement extends the faith-transparency of v.44 to vision: to look at Jesus is to look at the Father. Compare 14:9.
12.46: ἐγὼ φῶς εἰς τὸν κόσμον ἐλήλυθα — "I have come as light into the world," perfect of erchomai — "I have come and remain present as light." The light/world declaration echoes the prologue (1:4-9) and the self-declarations at 8:12 and 9:5, now given as the culminating public statement of Jesus' ministry.
12.47:
καὶ ἐάν τίς μου ἀκούσῃ τῶν ῥημάτων καὶ μὴ φυλάξῃ — "if anyone hears my words and does not keep them," aorist subjunctives (akousē, phylaxē). Both hearing and keeping are conditional; neither alone defines the relationship to Jesus' words.

οὐ κρίνω αὐτόν — "I do not judge him." The first-person negation is emphatic. The mission of the first coming is soteriological, not judicial — the contrast between 3:17 and the final judgment is repeated here.
12.48:
ὁ ἀθετῶν ἐμέ — "the one rejecting me," present participle of atheteō (G114, to set aside, refuse, reject — often of rejecting what has authority). The one who rejects is contrasted with the one who merely fails to keep (v.47); atheteō is active refusal rather than passive neglect.

ὁ λόγος ὃν ἐλάλησα, ἐκεῖνος κρινεῖ αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ ἐσχάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ — "the word I have spoken, that word will judge him on the last day." The word (logos) is the instrument of judgment — not an external law imposed but the standard Jesus himself established in his ministry. The word stands as witness against those who rejected it.
12.49:
ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐξ ἐμαυτοῦ οὐκ ἐλάλησα — "because I have not spoken from myself," ek emautou (from myself/on my own authority). The divine origin of Jesus' words is the ground for their judicial authority — they are not his private opinion but the Father's commission.

ὁ πέμψας με πατήρ αὐτός μοι ἐντολὴν δέδωκεν — "the Father who sent me himself has given me commandment," perfect of didōmi — "has given and the commandment stands." Entolē (G1785, commandment) frames the Father's direction of Jesus' speech as a standing directive.
12.50:
ἡ ἐντολὴ αὐτοῦ ζωὴ αἰώνιός ἐστιν — "his commandment is eternal life," zōē aiōnios (G2222 + G166). The equation is remarkable: the Father's instruction is not a path to eternal life but constitutes it. Obedience to the Father and eternal life are identified, not merely connected.

καθὼς εἴρηκέν μοι ὁ πατήρ, οὕτως λαλῶ — "as the Father has spoken to me, so I speak," perfect of legō. The perfect eirēken marks the Father's word as the permanent source from which Jesus' present speaking flows.

13.1:
πρὸ δὲ τῆς ἑορτῆς τοῦ πάσχα — "before the festival of the Passover." The temporal marker sets this chapter earlier than the Synoptics' Last Supper timeline; in John the crucifixion occurs on Nisan 14 (the day of Passover preparation) rather than Nisan 15, making Jesus the Passover lamb killed when the lambs are slaughtered.

ἀγαπήσας τοὺς ἰδίους τοὺς ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ, εἰς τέλος ἠγάπησεν αὐτούς — "having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end." Agapaō (G25) appears as a participle (prior love) then aorist (the definitive act). Eis telos (G5056) carries both temporal sense ("to the end, until the last moment") and qualitative sense ("completely, utterly") — the ambiguity is intentional; both are true of what follows in these chapters and at the cross.
13.2:
δείπνου γινομένου — "during supper being held/underway," present participle of ginomai — the meal is in progress, not concluded (contrast earlier translations that read "when supper was ended").

τοῦ διαβόλου ἤδη βεβληκότος εἰς τὴν καρδίαν — "the devil having already cast into the heart," perfect active participle of ballō (G906, to throw/cast). The perfect marks the devil's prompting as a completed instigation: Judas's heart is already set before the meal's action begins. Diabolos (G1228, the accuser/slanderer) is used here rather than Satanas (13:27), noting both terms converge on the same figure.
13.3:
εἰδὼς ὅτι πάντα ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ ὁ πατήρ εἰς τὰς χεῖρας — "knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands," perfect of didōmi. The cosmic frame is essential: Jesus washes feet not in submission to the disciples but from a position of total authority. The humility is voluntary, not imposed.

ἀπὸ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθεν καὶ πρὸς τὸν θεὸν ὑπάγει — "from God he came out and to God he is going," aorist of exerchomai + present of hypagō. The framing of Jesus' existence between two divine termini — departure from God and return to God — is the theological ground for the shocking act that follows. He who spans eternity stoops to wash feet.
13.4:
τίθησιν τὰ ἱμάτια — "lays down his garments," present of tithēmi (G5087). The same verb is used for laying down one's life (10:11, 15, 17, 18; 13:37–38). The Evangelist chooses tithēmi for the garment-laying precisely to anticipate the cross-language.

λέντιον διέζωσεν ἑαυτόν — "wrapped a linen cloth around himself," aorist of diazōnnymi (G1241). Lention (G3012) is a Latin loanword (linteum) — a linen towel, the garb of a household slave. The detail makes the status-inversion explicit.
13.5:
εἶτα βάλλει ὕδωρ εἰς τὸν νιπτῆρα — "then he pours water into the basin," present tense continuing the vivid narration. Niptēr (G3537, a washbasin) appears only here in NT.

ἤρξατο νίπτειν τοὺς πόδας τῶν μαθητῶν — "he began to wash the feet of the disciples," niptō (G3538, to wash a body part). The distinction from louō (G3068, full bathing) matters for vv.9–10. Foot-washing was a task for the lowest-ranked household slave, not for a rabbi — this is the crux of Peter's objection.
13.6: κύριε, σύ μου νίπτεις τοὺς πόδας — "Lord, are you washing my feet?" The word order in Greek puts su (you) immediately after the address, making the contrast emphatic: You — of all people — wash my feet? Peter's objection registers the role-reversal as an impropriety, not merely a surprise.
13.7:
ὃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ σὺ οὐκ οἶδας ἄρτι — "what I am doing you do not understand now," present of oida (G1492). Arti (G737, now/at this moment) — current incomprehension is normal; understanding will come. The word oida in John often denotes deeper comprehension, not surface knowledge; Peter's deficiency is theological, not informational.

γνώσῃ δὲ μετὰ ταῦτα — "but you will know after these things," future of ginōskō. The post-resurrection understanding promised to the disciples (cf. 12:16) is anticipated here.
13.8:
οὐ μὴ νίψῃς μου τοὺς πόδας εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "you shall by no means wash my feet forever," strong negation (ou mē + aorist subjunctive). Eis ton aiōna ("into the age") is the standard emphatic expression for "never" in a permanent sense. Peter's refusal is categorical.

οὐκ ἔχεις μέρος μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ — "you have no part with me," present of echō. Meros (G3313, part/share) carries the sense of participation and belonging — an inheritance or allotment term (cf. Josh 14:4 LXX; Rev 20:6). Exclusion from meros is exclusion from the shared life Jesus offers.
13.9: μὴ μόνον τοὺς πόδας ἀλλὰ καὶ τὰς χεῖρας καὶ τὴν κεφαλήν — "not only my feet but also my hands and head." Peter's characteristic overcorrection swings from categorical refusal to total submission. The three body parts (feet, hands, head) may reflect a complete-cleansing idiom, or simply Petrine impulsiveness.
13.10:
ὁ λελουμένος οὐ χρείαν ἔχει εἰ μὴ τοὺς πόδας νίψασθαι — "the one who has bathed does not need anything except to wash his feet," perfect passive participle of louō (G3068, full immersion bathing). The distinction between louō (whole-body bath, once) and niptō (partial washing, ongoing) is deliberate. A person who has bathed needs only foot-cleaning after walking — the metaphor suggests that the disciples have already received comprehensive cleansing (discipleship with Jesus) and need only the ongoing foot-washing (continued service and humility).

καὶ ὑμεῖς καθαροί ἐστε, ἀλλ᾽ οὐχὶ πάντες — "and you are clean, but not all of you," katharos (G2513, clean/pure). The qualification "not all" prepares for v.11's identification of Judas, and anticipates the moral dimension of the cleansing metaphor.
13.11: ᾔδει γὰρ τὸν παραδιδόντα αὐτόν — "for he knew the one who was betraying him," pluperfect of oida — "had known." The parenthetical narrator comment reveals that Jesus' "not all" in v.10 was deliberate and knowing, not uncertain.
13.12:
ἔλαβεν τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ καὶ ἀνέπεσεν πάλιν — "he took his garments and reclined again," aorists marking the resumption of normal table position. The deliberateness of putting garments back on — completing the re-dressing before asking the interpretive question — underlines that this is a structured teaching act, not spontaneous.

γινώσκετε τί πεποίηκα ὑμῖν — "do you understand what I have done for you?" present of ginōskō + perfect of poieō. The completed act now awaits interpretation — the question is pedagogical, inviting reflection before explanation.
13.13:
ὁ διδάσκαλος καὶ ὁ κύριος — "the Teacher and the Lord," both with the definite article. These titles represent the two poles of Jesus' acknowledged authority: didaskalos (G1320, rabbi/teacher) is the common address used by disciples; kyrios (G2962, Lord/master) acknowledges sovereign authority. Jesus affirms both simultaneously.

καλῶς λέγετε, εἰμὶ γάρ — "you speak rightly, for I am," present of eimi. The affirmation is unqualified — Jesus does not diminish the titles but draws on their combined authority for the foot-washing argument.
13.14:
εἰ οὖν ἐγὼ ἔνιψα ὑμῶν τοὺς πόδας ὁ κύριος καὶ ὁ διδάσκαλος — "if then I, the Lord and the Teacher, washed your feet," aorist of niptō. The conditional is real (first class implied): he did wash them. The argument from the greater to the lesser (a fortiori) is explicit — if the one of higher rank performed the act, those of lower rank are obligated.

ὀφείλετε καὶ ὑμεῖς ἀλλήλων νίπτειν τοὺς πόδας — "you also ought to wash one another's feet," present infinitive of niptō indicating an ongoing obligation. Opheilō (G3784, to owe/ought) is not mere suggestion but moral obligation derived from the enacted example.
13.15:
ὑπόδειγμα γὰρ δέδωκα ὑμῖν — "for I have given you an example," perfect of didōmi. Hypodeigma (G5261, example/pattern/model) appears here for the first time in John — this is the interpretive key Jesus provides. Not a one-time rite but a repeatable pattern of servant-action among equals.

ἵνα καθὼς ἐγὼ ἐποίησα ὑμῖν, καὶ ὑμεῖς ποιῆτε — "so that as I did for you, you also might do," aorist subjunctive of poieō. The kathōs (just as) establishes correspondence of kind, not merely of external form — the quality of self-giving is the model, not the literal act only.
13.16:
οὐκ ἔστιν δοῦλος μείζων τοῦ κυρίου αὐτοῦ — "a servant is not greater than his master," present of eimi. The principle appears also at 15:20; the axiom is well-attested in Jewish wisdom literature. Applied here: if the master washed feet, the servant has no grounds to refuse to do the same.

οὐδὲ ἀπόστολος μείζων τοῦ πέμψαντος αὐτόν — "nor is a messenger/apostle greater than the one who sent him." Apostolos (G652) appears only here in John — the only occurrence in the entire Gospel. The word means "sent one" (from apostellō, G649) — the parallel with pempō (sent) is intentional: Jesus is himself the one sent by the Father (passim), and the disciples are sent by him.
13.17: εἰ ταῦτα οἴδατε, μακάριοί ἐστε ἐὰν ποιῆτε αὐτά — "if you know these things, you are blessed if you do them." Two conditionals bracket the promise: knowing (oida) and doing (poieō). Makarios (G3107, blessed/happy) is a beatitude-form — the only such form in John's Gospel. Knowing without doing produces no blessing; action in conformity with knowledge does.
13.18:
οὐ περὶ πάντων ὑμῶν λέγω — "I am not speaking about all of you," present of legō. The assurance of v.17 is explicitly not addressed to Judas — the exception is stated before the citation so readers understand Jesus' blessing has not missed the betrayal.

ὁ τρώγων μου τὸν ἄρτον ἐπῆρεν ἐπ᾽ ἐμὲ τὴν πτέρναν αὐτοῦ — Ps 41:9 (LXX 40:10) citation. Trōgō (G5176, to gnaw or crunch) is a more visceral eating verb than esthiō (G2068) — the LXX uses esthiō but John's version upgrades to trōgō, the same verb Jesus used for eating his flesh at 6:54–58. The betrayal occurs in the context of shared bread, which was the ultimate expression of covenant solidarity in the ancient world.
13.19: ἵνα πιστεύσητε ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι — "so that you may believe that I am," aorist subjunctive of pisteuō. ἐγώ εἰμι without predicate is the Johannine absolute divine self-identification (cf. 8:24, 28, 58), carrying the register of the divine name from Exod 3:14 (LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν) and Isa 43:10 (LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι). Foreknowledge of the betrayal, announced before it happens, becomes evidence for the disciples that Jesus operates from divine knowledge.
13.20: ὁ λαμβάνων ἄν τινα πέμψω ἐμὲ λαμβάνει — "whoever receives anyone I send receives me," present participle of lambanō. The chain of reception (agent → sender → Father) mirrors the chain of revelation in John's theology: receiving the apostle is receiving Jesus; receiving Jesus is receiving the Father. The commissioning authority flows unbroken from the Father through Jesus to the sent ones.
13.21:
ἐταράχθη τῷ πνεύματι — "was troubled in spirit," aorist passive of tarassō (G5015) + dative pneumati. This is the same disturbance as at 11:33 (the tomb) and echoes 12:27 (the hour of death). The localization "in spirit" (pneumati) distinguishes this from the purely emotional; it is disturbance at the deepest level of Jesus' being.

ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι εἷς ἐξ ὑμῶν παραδώσει με — "truly truly I tell you, one of you will betray me," future of paradidōmi. The doubled-amen formula marks solemn revelation; the betrayal, already anticipated from 6:71 and 12:4, is now formally announced at table.
13.22:
ἔβλεπον εἰς ἀλλήλους οἱ μαθηταί — "the disciples were looking at one another," imperfect of blepō — mutual, repeated glancing. The imperfect captures the prolonged confused exchange as each searches the others' faces.

ἀπορούμενοι περὶ τίνος λέγει — "at a loss about whom he was speaking," present participle of aporeō (G639, to be at a loss, have no way out). The disciples' uncertainty is genuine — Judas's hidden intention has not been visible to them.
13.23:
εἷς ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ἐν τῷ κόλπῳ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ — "one of his disciples in the bosom of Jesus," kolpos (G2859, bosom/chest/lap) — the position of intimate reclining at a banquet, next to the host. The same word is used for Jesus' relationship with the Father at 1:18 ("the one in the bosom of the Father"). The beloved disciple's position mirrors Jesus' own position with the Father.

ὃν ἠγάπα ὁ Ἰησοῦς — "whom Jesus loved," imperfect of agapaō. The imperfect marks sustained, ongoing love, not a single act. This is the first explicit designation of "the disciple whom Jesus loved" — the anonymous witness whose testimony underlies the Gospel (21:24).
13.24: νεύει οὖν τούτῳ Σίμων Πέτρος — "Simon Peter therefore nods to this one," present of neuō (G3506, to nod or beckon). A silent gesture across the reclining group — Peter cannot ask directly without disrupting the meal, so he signals the beloved disciple who has better access to Jesus.
13.25: ἐπιπεσὼν δὲ ἐκεῖνος οὕτως ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος τοῦ Ἰησοῦ — "leaning back in that way upon the breast of Jesus," aorist participle of epipiptō (G1968, to fall upon/lean against). The reclining posture makes the question physically intimate; the beloved disciple simply leans back to whisper.
13.26:
ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν ᾧ ἐγὼ βάψω τὸ ψωμίον — "that one is the one to whom I will dip the piece of bread," future of baptō (G911, to dip). Psōmion (G5596, a morsel or small piece) is a piece of bread dipped in sauce or the dish. Offering a dipped morsel was an act of honor at a Middle Eastern meal — the host singles out a guest for distinction.

δοὺς τῷ Ἰούδᾳ Σίμωνος Ἰσκαριώτου — "having given it to Judas son of Simon Iscariot," aorist participle. The identification by name and patronymic at the moment of the morsel is the Evangelist's literary climax of the scene.
13.27:
εἰσῆλθεν εἰς ἐκεῖνον ὁ σατανᾶς — "Satan entered into that one," aorist of eiserchomai. Satanas (G4567) here compared to diabolos in v.2 — both terms are used for the adversary; the shift may indicate an escalation from prompting (v.2) to full possession/control. The entrance is the counterpart to the morsel: an act of honor becomes the vehicle of darkness.

ὃ ποιεῖς ποίησον τάχιον — "what you are doing, do more quickly," present + aorist imperative of poieō. Tachion (G5032, comparative of tachy, quickly) — Jesus is not hastening the betrayal for pragmatic reasons but releasing Judas to his chosen course. The authority of the command underlines that even this falls within Jesus' sovereignty.
13.28: τοῦτο δὲ οὐδεὶς ἔγνω τῶν ἀνακειμένων πρὸς τί εἶπεν αὐτῷ — "but no one of those reclining knew for what reason he said this to him," aorist of ginōskō. The narrator's aside emphasizes the opacity — the exchange between Jesus and Judas was perceived only by the beloved disciple (v.26) and perhaps Peter; the rest remain uninformed.
13.29: τινὲς γὰρ ἐδόκουν ἐπεὶ τὸ γλωσσόκομον εἶχεν Ἰούδας — "for some thought, since Judas had the money bag," imperfect of dokeō (G1380, to think/suppose). The narrator records the plausible misreading — Judas' role as treasurer made the most natural interpretation one of errand-running, not betrayal. The irony is that the disciples' charity toward Judas was misplaced.
13.30:
εὐθέως οὖν ἐξῆλθεν — "immediately therefore he went out," aorist of exerchomai. The adverb eutheōs (G2112, immediately) marks the speed of response — the decision is irrevocable, the course set.

ἦν δὲ νύξ — "and it was night," nyx (G3571). Two words in Greek, but among the most theologically dense in the Gospel. Nyx is not only a temporal marker but the Johannine symbol for spiritual darkness, opposition to the light (1:5; 3:19; 11:10). Judas goes out into the night in every sense.
13.31: νῦν ἐδοξάσθη ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — "now the Son of Man was glorified," aorist passive of doxazō. The aorist is proleptic — the glorification has not yet occurred, but with Judas' departure the cross is now inevitable and imminent; the aorist treats the outcome as already secured. Nun (now) ties the glorification to this very moment of the betrayal's activation.
13.32:
εἰ ὁ θεὸς ἐδοξάσθη ἐν αὐτῷ, καὶ ὁ θεὸς δοξάσει αὐτὸν ἐν αὐτῷ — "if God was glorified in him, God will also glorify him in himself." The conditional is not uncertain but logical: because the Father has been glorified in the Son's mission, the Father will reciprocally glorify the Son. The mutual glorification is the structure of John's theology of the cross.

καὶ εὐθὺς δοξάσει αὐτόν — "and will glorify him at once," eutheōs (G2112). The immediacy of the coming glorification matches the immediacy of Judas' exit — the timetable is now compressed.
13.33:
τεκνία — "little children," diminutive of teknon (G5040). This is the only occurrence of teknia in John's Gospel (though common in 1 John). The term of endearment marks the shift in tone from instruction to farewell.

ζητήσετέ με, καὶ καθὼς εἶπον τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ὅτι ὅπου ὑπάγω ἐγὼ ὑμεῖς οὐ δύνασθε ἐλθεῖν — "you will seek me, and as I told the Jews that where I am going you cannot come." The language of departure echoes 7:33–34 and 8:21, but with different pathos — what was said as warning to opponents is now said as sorrowful farewell to the beloved disciples.
13.34:
ἐντολὴν καινὴν δίδωμι ὑμῖν — "a new commandment I give you," present of didōmi. Kainos (G2537, new in kind/quality, as opposed to neos, new in time) signals that the commandment is not merely added to existing law but is of a different order. The love commandment is not unprecedented (Lev 19:18) — what makes it "new" is the standard given in v.34b.

ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους, καθὼς ἠγάπησα ὑμᾶς — "love one another, as I have loved you," present imperative + aorist of agapaō. The kathōs (just as) makes Christ's own love the measure and model — not the natural impulse to love those who love you, but a love shaped by the self-giving pattern of 13:1–17 and the cross. The aorist ēgapēsa encapsulates the completed act now being described as the standard.
13.35:
ἐν τούτῳ γνώσονται πάντες ὅτι ἐμοὶ μαθηταί ἐστε — "by this all will know that you are my disciples," future of ginōskō. The criterion of recognizable discipleship is not adherence to teaching, observance of ritual, or organizational affiliation — it is mutual love.

ἐὰν ἀγάπην ἔχητε ἐν ἀλλήλοις — "if you have love among one another," agapē (G26, disp=3). The disputed semantic range of agapē — from "warm affection" to "selfless covenantal love" — is resolved by context: the standard in v.34 (kathōs ēgapēsa hymas) demands a love shaped by Christ's own self-giving, which is not adequately described as mere affection. The observable social reality of this love becomes the community's public witness.
13.36:
κύριε, ποῦ ὑπάγεις — "Lord, where are you going?" Peter's question interrupts the farewell; hypagō (G5217, to go/depart) is the departure verb John uses for Jesus' movement toward death and to the Father (cf. 7:33; 8:14, 21–22; 13:33).

οὐ δύνασαί μοι νῦν ἀκολουθῆσαι, ἀκολουθήσεις δὲ ὕστερον — "you are not able to follow me now, but you will follow later," present of dynamai + future of akoloutheō. The hysteron (later) gestures toward Peter's martyrdom (21:18–19), where he will indeed follow Jesus in the way of the cross.
13.37:
κύριε, διὰ τί οὐ δύναμαί σοι ἀκολουθεῖν ἄρτι — "Lord, why am I not able to follow you now?" Peter's arti (now) echoes Jesus' arti in v.7 (you do not understand now) — Peter still does not grasp the nature of what following Jesus will require.

τὴν ψυχήν μου ὑπὲρ σοῦ θήσω — "I will lay down my life for you," future of tithēmi, the same verb used for Jesus' own life-laying in 10:11, 15. Peter claims to be ready for what is in fact Jesus' unique role — the confidence is sincere but premature.
13.38:
τὴν ψυχήν σου ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ θήσεις — "you will lay down your life for me?" The echo of Peter's own words (v.37) turned into a question — Jesus neither affirms the claim nor rejects it as impossible (Peter will eventually die for Christ), but punctures the immediate confidence.

ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω σοι, οὐ μὴ ἀλέκτωρ φωνήσῃ ἕως οὗ ἀρνήσῃ με τρίς — "truly truly I tell you, the rooster will not crow until you have denied me three times," double aorist subjunctives with the strong negation ou mē. Alektōr (G220, rooster) appears only in denial-prophecy passages in all four Gospels. The tripling of the denial (tris) will be fulfilled in 18:15–27 with precision.

14.1: μὴ ταρασσέσθω ὑμῶν ἡ καρδία — "Let not your heart be troubled." The verb tarasséō (G5015) is present passive imperative — "stop being troubled," or "do not continue in the state of being shaken." The same verb appears in 5:7 of the stirred pool, and in 13:21 where Jesus himself is described as "troubled in spirit." The command is not to suppress emotion but to redirect trust: the next clause sets up the ground — "believe in God; also believe in me." The conjunction kai ("also") links belief in God and belief in Jesus as parallel acts, not sequential ones.
14.2:
ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ τοῦ πατρός μου μοναὶ πολλαί εἰσιν — "In my Father's house there are many dwelling-places." Monē (G3438) — from menō (to remain) — is a place of stable, enduring residence, not a temporary lodging. The cognate connection matters: the "dwelling-place" Jesus promises is the same root as the "remaining" (μένω) that structures the vine discourse in chapter 15.

εἰ δὲ μή — the conditional clause is difficult. The Greek reads: "if it were not so, I would have told you." The Evangelist presents Jesus as affirming the Father's house as a known reality, not a speculative hope. The purpose clause ἑτοιμάσαι τόπον ὑμῖν ("to prepare a place for you") uses aorist infinitive — a decisive, completed preparation, not an ongoing process.
14.3: καὶ ἐὰν πορευθῶ καὶ ἑτοιμάσω τόπον ὑμῖν, πάλιν ἔρχομαι — the two aorist subjunctives (poreutho, hetoimasō) express the contingent departure-and-preparation; then the present indicative érchomai ("I am coming") shifts the return to a certain, already-in-motion reality. The present is used as a definite future: the return is so certain it is stated as a present fact. παραλήμψομαι ὑμᾶς πρὸς ἐμαυτόν — "I will take you to myself" — the verb paralambánō carries the sense of active reception, like taking someone by the hand to bring alongside.
14.4: καὶ ὅπου ἐγὼ ὑπάγω οἴδατε τὴν ὁδόν — "And where I am going, you know the way." The emphatic egō ("I") separates the subject from the verb. Oídate (G1492, perfect of eidon) carries the force of "you have come to know and it stands known." Thomas's immediate objection in v5 exposes that their knowledge is more implicit than they realize — the declaration is anticipatory, not descriptive of their current self-understanding.
14.5: οὐκ οἴδαμεν ποῦ ὑπάγεις — Thomas's reply uses the same verb oidamen (perfect "we have known"), but negated: "we have not come to know." The exchange turns on the same word — Jesus asserts they know the way; Thomas counters they do not even know the destination. The mismatch sets up the I-AM saying of v6: the way is not a route to a known place but a person.
14.6:
ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ὁδὸς καὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια καὶ ἡ ζωή — the sixth predicated I-AM saying of the Fourth Gospel. Three nouns with articles, all in the nominative, linked by kai … kai: "the way, and the truth, and the life." The article before each noun ("the" way, "the" truth, "the" life) is definitive — not one path among many, not a truth among truths. Hodós (G3598) is way, road, path — the means of movement; alētheia (G225) is truth as unconcealedness, reality as it actually is; zōē (G2222) is life in the Johannine sense — the life of the age to come, already present in the Son (1:4).

οὐδεὶς ἔρχεται πρὸς τὸν πατέρα εἰ μὴ δι᾽ ἐμοῦ — "no one comes to the Father except through me." The double-negative pattern of the LXX exclusives: oudeis … ei mē — absolute. The preposition dia with the genitive ("through me") marks agency and mediation, not merely association.
14.7: εἰ ἐγνώκειτέ με, καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου ἂν ᾔδειτε — a mixed past counterfactual. Egnōkeite is pluperfect of ginōskō (relational, experiential knowing); ēdeite is pluperfect of oida (complete, settled knowledge). The sentence distinguishes two kinds of knowing: if you had truly known me (relational knowing), you would already possess settled knowledge of the Father. From v7b, the counterfactual flips to an affirmation: "from now on, you do know him and have seen him" — present tense, applying the completed work of revelation.
14.8: κύριε, δεῖξον ἡμῖν τὸν πατέρα — Philip's request uses the aorist imperative deixon — "show us" as a single act. The word deíknymi (G1166) means to make visible, to display. Philip imagines a theophany separable from Jesus — a visible display of the Father as a distinct event. The irony is that the display has been ongoing throughout the ministry. καὶ ἀρκεῖ ἡμῖν — "and it will be enough for us." Arkeō (G714) — to suffice, to be content — implies that what they have seen in Jesus is insufficient; the reply in v9 overturns this assumption.
14.9:
τοσούτῳ χρόνῳ μεθ᾽ ὑμῶν εἰμι — "such a long time I am with you." The dative tosoutō chronō marks duration — Jesus has been present as a fact of ongoing experience. The perfect-present combination charges Philip's failure to recognize: "so long a time" of seeing (θεωρέω) without perceiving.

ὁ ἑωρακὼς ἐμὲ ἑώρακεν τὸν πατέρα — "the one having seen me has seen the Father." Both verbs are perfect tense: heōrakōs (having seen, with the result standing) and heōraken (has seen and it stands seen). The perfect asserts that seeing Jesus is not preparation for seeing the Father later — it is the seeing of the Father, already accomplished.
14.10: τὰ ῥήματα ἃ ἐγὼ λέγω ὑμῖν ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ οὐ λαλῶ — the distinction between two speaking verbs: légō (G3004, to say — the content) and laléō (G2980, to speak — the act of speaking). Jesus says: the things I say I do not speak from myself. The content of his words and the utterance of his speech both originate in the Father. ὁ δὲ πατὴρ ὁ ἐν ἐμοὶ μένων ποιεῖ τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ — "the Father who remains in me does his works." Menōn is present participle of menō — the Father's indwelling is ongoing, not sporadic. The works (erga) are the Father's own, performed through the Son.
14.11: πιστεύετέ μοι … εἰ δὲ μή, διὰ τὰ ἔργα αὐτὰ πιστεύετε — a two-tier appeal to belief, both using present imperative pisteuete. The first tier asks belief grounded in the mutual indwelling claim (v10); the second — "if not, believe for the sake of the works themselves" — appeals to the observable evidence of the works as a lower but valid ground. The pattern recurs from 10:38: the works testify when the words are not received.
14.12: ὁ πιστεύων εἰς ἐμὲ τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ κἀκεῖνος ποιήσει — "the one believing in me will do the works I do." Present participle pisteuōn — the one who is (continuously) believing. The future poiēsei is a straight promise. μείζονα τούτων ποιήσει — "greater than these will he do." Meizōn (G3187, comparative of megas) — greater in scope and extent, not in kind. The ground for this claim: "because I am going to the Father" — the departure enables the Spirit's coming (16:7), and the Spirit's coming enables the mission's worldwide reach.
14.13: καὶ ὅ τι ἂν αἰτήσητε ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου τοῦτο ποιήσω — "whatever you ask in my name, this I will do." The phrase en tō onomati mou ("in my name") does not mean invoking Jesus as a formula; in Semitic thought, "name" encompasses identity, authority, and character. To ask "in the name of" Jesus is to ask as his representative, in alignment with his character and mission. ἵνα δοξασθῇ ὁ πατὴρ ἐν τῷ υἱῷ — the purpose clause: "so that the Father may be glorified in the Son." The aorist passive subjunctive doxasthē ties answered prayer to the Father's glorification — not a blank check but a shaped promise.
14.14: ἐάν τι αἰτήσητέ με ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου ἐγὼ ποιήσω — v14 repeats v13 with one addition: the pronoun me ("you ask me") in some manuscripts, making Jesus himself the object of the petition, not merely the name invoked in a petition to the Father. This variant is well-attested (P66, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and shifts the addressee of prayer: in these texts, Jesus explicitly receives petitions made "in his name." The emphasis on repetition in vv13–14 underscores the certainty of the promise.
14.15: ἐὰν ἀγαπᾶτέ με, τὰς ἐντολάς τὰς ἐμὰς τηρήσετε — the conditional is present subjunctive: "if you love me [as an ongoing disposition], you will keep my commandments." The future indicative tērēsete is not an imperative but a prediction: love issues in obedience naturally. Tēréō (G5083) — to guard, to keep watch over — is stronger than mere compliance; it implies vigilant preservation of what has been entrusted. The possessive tas emas ("my commandments") emphasizes that these are not the Mosaic commands but the commands of Jesus as covenant mediator.
14.16:
κἀγὼ ἐρωτήσω τὸν πατέρα καὶ ἄλλον παράκλητον δώσει ὑμῖν — the first appearance of παράκλητος (Paráklētos, G3875) in the Gospel. The word is a legal and relational term: one called alongside (para + kaleō) to help, support, advocate, or defend. In Hellenistic legal usage, a paraklētos is a defense advocate in court. Philo uses it of an intercessor with God. The KJV "Comforter" (from Latin comfortare) captures one dimension but misses the legal freight. "Advocate" recovers the forensic register; "Helper" is also defensible.

ἄλλον — "another" of the same kind (G243, allos, not G2087, heteros — another of a different kind). The implication is that Jesus himself has been functioning as Paráklētos for the disciples — 1 John 2:1 makes this explicit. The Spirit is a second Advocate of the same order. εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "forever" — eis ton aiōna, into the age, with perpetuity: the Spirit's presence is permanent in a way that Jesus's physical presence was not.
14.17:
τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας — "the Spirit of truth." The genitive is appositional: the Spirit who is truth, or the Spirit characterized by truth. Alētheia (G225) in John always carries ontological weight — truth as unconcealedness, the way things actually are before God. The Spirit does not merely teach accurate facts; he mediates genuine reality as opposed to the world's distorted perception.

ὃ ὁ κόσμος οὐ δύναται λαβεῖν — "whom the world cannot receive." Dúnamai (G1410, present indicative) — not merely "does not" but "cannot" — the incapacity is structural, not circumstantial. ὅτι οὐ θεωρεῖ αὐτὸ οὐδὲ γινώσκει — "because it neither sees him nor knows him." Two verbs: theōréō (G2334, perceptual seeing) and ginōskō (relational knowing) — the world lacks both. ὑμεῖς δὲ γινώσκετε αὐτό — "but you know him" — emphatic hymeis ("you" in contrast to "the world"). The present tense suggests the disciples already have this knowing, even if not yet conscious of its full extent.
14.18: οὐκ ἀφήσω ὑμᾶς ὀρφανούς — "I will not leave you orphans." Orphanós (G3737) — literally, a child bereft of a father; metaphorically, one without a patron or protector in the Greco-Roman world. Orphans were among the most legally and socially vulnerable in antiquity. The word activates both the familial and the social-protection register. ἔρχομαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς — "I am coming to you" — again the present as certain future (cf. 14:3). The question of whether this refers to the resurrection appearances, the Spirit's coming, or the parousia is debated; the Evangelist may intend the ambiguity to encompass all three.
14.19: ἔτι μικρὸν καὶ ὁ κόσμος με οὐκέτι θεωρεῖ — "yet a little while and the world sees me no longer." Mikrón (G3397, a little while) — the same phrase frames 16:16–19. Theōréō (G2334) — extended perceptual seeing. The world's sight of Jesus ends with the crucifixion. ὑμεῖς δὲ θεωρεῖτε με — "but you see me" — same verb, present tense: the disciples' seeing continues past the world's horizon. The basis: "because I live, you also will live." Zō (G2198, present indicative) — "I live" — the resurrection life of Jesus is stated as an ongoing present fact that grounds the disciples' future life.
14.20: ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ γνώσεσθε — "on that day you will know." The future gnōsesthe (G1097) — the knowing is coming but is not yet complete. "That day" is the day of recognition that comes with the Spirit's arrival and the resurrection. Three mutual indwellings are stated: "I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you" — a nested mutual indwelling that mirrors the mutual knowing of 10:14–15 (Father-Son-sheep). The grammar is paratactic: three ens (in) without subordination, all equally real.
14.21: ὁ ἔχων τὰς ἐντολάς μου καὶ τηρῶν αὐτάς — two participles: "the one having my commands" (echōn) and "keeping them" (tērōn). Possession without keeping is insufficient; keeping without possession is impossible. κἀγὼ ἀγαπήσω αὐτὸν καὶ ἐμφανίσω αὐτῷ ἐμαυτόν — "I will love him and reveal myself to him." Emphanízō (G1718) — to make visible, to disclose, to cause to appear. The verb appears in the Exodus context for God showing his glory (Exod 33:13 LXX). The self-disclosure of Jesus is conditioned on the love-obedience relationship.
14.22: τί γέγονεν ὅτι ἡμῖν μέλλεις ἐμφανίζειν σεαυτὸν καὶ οὐχὶ τῷ κόσμῳ — Judas (not Iscariot) raises a reasonable question: if Jesus is going to reveal himself, why not universally? The verb emphanízō from v21 is repeated — the disciples have registered the word. The question implies they expected a public, triumphant Messiah. Jesus's answer redefines revelation: it is relational (love + obedience), not spectacular.
14.23: ἐάν τις ἀγαπᾷ με, τὸν λόγον μου τηρήσει — "if anyone loves me, he will keep my word." Note the shift from entolás (commandments, plural, vv15, 21) to ton logon mou ("my word," singular): the totality of Jesus's teaching as an integrated whole, not individual commands. καὶ πρὸς αὐτὸν ἐλευσόμεθα καὶ μονὴν παρ᾽ αὐτῷ ποιησόμεθα — "and we will come to him and make our dwelling with him." The plural "we" — Father and Son — is striking; the inhabitation is Trinitarian. Monēn (G3438, dwelling-place) returns from v2: the "many dwelling-places" in the Father's house and the dwelling made in the obedient believer are cognate realities.
14.24: ὁ μὴ ἀγαπῶν με τοὺς λόγους μου οὐ τηρεῖ — the negative form of v23: "the one not loving me does not keep my words." Present tense throughout — ongoing states. καὶ ὁ λόγος ὃν ἀκούετε οὐκ ἔστιν ἐμὸς ἀλλὰ τοῦ πέμψαντός με πατρός — "the word you hear is not mine but the Father's who sent me." The aorist participle tou pempsantos me — "of the one who sent me" — is one of the Evangelist's characteristic phrases for the Father's commissioning authority (cf. 5:37; 7:28; 8:26). The refusal to receive Jesus's word is thereby a refusal of the Father's word.
14.25: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν παρ᾽ ὑμῖν μένων — "these things I have spoken to you while remaining with you." The perfect lelalēka — "I have spoken and the speaking stands" — a summary of the entire Farewell Discourse before it is technically over. Par᾽ hymīn menōn — "while remaining alongside you" — menō again: the physical remaining of Jesus with the disciples is about to end; the Spirit's remaining (v17: menει παρ᾽ ὑμῖν) will replace it.
14.26:
ὁ δὲ παράκλητος, τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον — the second Paraclete saying. Here the Paraclete is explicitly identified as "the Holy Spirit," and the sending agent shifts: "whom the Father will send in my name." In 15:26, Jesus sends the Spirit from the Father; here the Father sends the Spirit in the name of Jesus — both are true, the persons of the Trinity acting in coordinated mission.

ἐκεῖνος ὑμᾶς διδάξει πάντα — "that one will teach you all things." Ekéinos is an emphatic demonstrative — "that one" — used to distinguish the Spirit from Jesus as a second distinct person with agency. Didáxei (G1321, future of didáskō) — will teach: the Spirit continues and completes what Jesus began. καὶ ὑπομνήσει ὑμᾶς πάντα ἃ εἶπον ὑμῖν ἐγώ — "and will remind you of all that I said to you." Hypomnēsei (G5279, to remind, to call to memory) — the Spirit's work grounds the apostolic recollection of Jesus's teaching; John's Gospel itself is implicitly presented as the product of this anamnesis.
14.27:
εἰρήνην ἀφίημι ὑμῖν, εἰρήνην τὴν ἐμὴν δίδωμι ὑμῖν — "Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you." Two verbs: aphíēmi (to release, to send away — the farewell bestowal) and dídōmi (to give — the active gift). Eirēnē (G1515) translates shalom — wholeness, well-being, right relationship, not merely absence of conflict. The possessive tēn emēn — "my peace" — distinguishes this from a generic benediction: it is the specific peace that characterizes Jesus himself, grounded in his relationship with the Father.

οὐ καθὼς ὁ κόσμος δίδωσιν ἐγὼ δίδωμι ὑμῖν — "not as the world gives, I give to you." The world's giving is conditional, transactional, temporary. Jesus's giving is permanent (v16: "forever") and unconditional. μὴ ταρασσέσθω ὑμῶν ἡ καρδία μηδὲ δειλιάτω — the chapter that opened with "do not let your heart be troubled" closes with the same prohibition plus deiliaō (G1167, to be cowardly, to shrink in fear). Two present imperatives: stop being troubled, stop being afraid.
14.28: ὁ πατὴρ μείζων μού ἐστιν — "the Father is greater than I." This verse is among the most contested in Johannine Christology. Meízōn (G3187, comparative of megas) — greater. The statement follows "I am going to the Father" — the context is missional and relational: the Son going back to the one who sent him, returning to the Father who is the source of the mission. The text does not say "greater in being" but "greater" without specification; interpreters who read this ontologically (Arius) press the text further than its context requires. Within John's own framework (1:1; 10:30; 20:28), the subordination operative here is functional and economic, not ontological. The note of rebuke — "if you loved me, you would have rejoiced" — grounds Jesus's departure not in loss but in the Father's greatness as the goal of the Son's return.
14.29: καὶ νῦν εἴρηκα ὑμῖν πρὶν γενέσθαι — "and now I have told you before it happens." The perfect eirēka — "I have spoken and it stands spoken" — is the Evangelist's recurring formula for completed disclosure. The aorist infinitive genésthai — "before happening" — marks the events as temporally future from the moment of speech. ἵνα ὅταν γένηται πιστεύσητε — "so that when it happens, you may believe." The purpose clause shapes the prophetic function of Jesus's words: not merely to inform but to produce belief when the events confirm the prediction.
14.30: ὁ τοῦ κόσμου ἄρχων — "the ruler of this world." Árchōn (G758) — ruler, chief, magistrate — is used three times in the Gospel for the devil (12:31; 14:30; 16:11). The title acknowledges real dominion over the present age, while the next clause strips away its power: ἐν ἐμοὶ οὐκ ἔχει οὐδέν — "in me he has nothing." The phrase ouden echei means "has no claim," "has no foothold," "finds nothing to seize." The coming of the ruler is a confrontation, but Jesus meets it without any crack or leverage that evil can exploit.
14.31: ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα γνῷ ὁ κόσμος ὅτι ἀγαπῶ τὸν πατέρα — "but that the world may know that I love the Father." The purpose of the passion is revelatory: the cross is the demonstration that Jesus loves the Father, enacted in total obedience. καθὼς ἐνετείλατό μοι ὁ πατήρ, οὕτως ποιῶ — "as the Father commanded me, so I do." Eneteilato (G1781, aorist — a definitive commissioning); poiō (G4160, present — ongoing execution of it). The aorist command of the Father and the present obedience of the Son in simultaneous relation. ἐγείρεσθε, ἄγωμεν ἐντεῦθεν — "Rise, let us go from here." Present imperative + hortatory subjunctive. The discourse ends in motion: the theology of the cross is immediately enacted.

15.1: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος ἡ ἀληθινή — the seventh and final predicated I-AM saying of the Gospel. Ampelos (G288) — vine. Alēthinós (G228) — genuine, real, as opposed to merely alēthēs (accurate, truthful). The distinction matters: Israel was the vine (Ps 80:8; Isa 5:1–7; Jer 2:21), but a vine that produced sour grapes, withered and failed. Jesus is the alēthinē vine — the genuine article, the fulfillment of what Israel's vine was always meant to be, not merely a true-rather-than-false vine. ὁ πατήρ μου ὁ γεωργός ἐστιν — "my Father is the gardener." Geōrgós (G1092) — farmer, vine-dresser, one who works the earth. The Father is the active cultivator, tending the vine and its branches with purposeful skill.
15.2:
πᾶν κλῆμα ἐν ἐμοὶ μὴ φέρον καρπόν, αἴρει αὐτό — "every branch in me not bearing fruit, he takes away." Aírō (G142) — to lift up and take away. The same verb is used for taking away sin (1:29) and lifting the blind man's mat (5:8). Here it is removal from the vine.

καὶ πᾶν τὸ καρπὸν φέρον, καθαίρει αὐτό — "and every branch bearing fruit, he prunes it." Kathaírō (G2508) — to cleanse, to prune. The wordplay in Greek is audible: aírō (take away) and kathaírō (prune/cleanse) share the root airō. English loses this entirely. The action of the Father is not neutral: he removes the fruitless and purifies the fruitful. Both are active interventions in the vine.
15.3: ἤδη ὑμεῖς καθαροί ἐστε διὰ τὸν λόγον ὃν λελάληκα ὑμῖν — "already you are clean because of the word I have spoken to you." The verb kathairomai from v2 (prune/cleanse) generates its adjective katharoi here — clean. The disciples have already been pruned by the word. The perfect lelalēka — "I have spoken and it stands spoken" — grounds their cleanness in a completed speech-act of Jesus. This echoes 13:10 ("you are clean, but not every one of you") — a cleanness that is real but not universal among the Twelve.
15.4: μείνατε ἐν ἐμοί, κἀγὼ ἐν ὑμῖν — "remain in me, and I in you." The aorist imperative meínate — "begin and continue to remain" — issues the central command of the vine discourse. Menō (G3306) occurs 11 times in 15:1–16, making it the structural verb of the passage. καθὼς τὸ κλῆμα οὐ δύναται καρπὸν φέρειν ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ — "just as the branch cannot bear fruit of itself." Ou dúnatai — cannot, not merely does not. The incapacity is organic: separated from the vine, the branch has no sap, no life, no possibility of fruit. The analogy holds for the disciples: spiritual fruitfulness is not a human achievement but a product of union.
15.5: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἄμπελος, ὑμεῖς τὰ κλήματα — the I-AM saying is repeated in its shorter form (without alēthinē), now paired with explicit identification of the disciples as branches. The juxtaposition is stark: "I am the vine; you are the branches." The grammar keeps the distinction: Jesus is the vine-stock itself, the source; the disciples are the extensions. ὁ μένων ἐν ἐμοὶ κἀγὼ ἐν αὐτῷ οὗτος φέρει καρπὸν πολύν — "the one remaining in me and I in him — this one bears much fruit." The mutual indwelling is inseparable from fruitfulness. χωρὶς ἐμοῦ οὐ δύνασθε ποιεῖν οὐδέν — "apart from me you can do nothing." Chōrís (G5565) — apart from, separate from. Ouden — nothing, absolutely nothing. No qualification; the totality of fruitful work depends on union with Christ.
15.6: ἐὰν μή τις μένῃ ἐν ἐμοί, ἐβλήθη ἔξω ὡς τὸ κλῆμα — "if anyone does not remain in me, he is thrown outside like the branch." The aorist passive eblēthē — "was thrown" — is gnomic aorist, expressing a typical or certain result as if it has already occurred. καὶ ἐξηράνθη — "and withered." The drying out follows inevitably from separation; it is not imposed but natural. καὶ συνάγουσιν αὐτὰ καὶ εἰς τὸ πῦρ βάλλουσιν — "and they gather them and throw them into the fire." The third-person plural "they" is indefinite — "people gather them" — focusing on the process, not an agent. The image of fire echoes the Baptist's warning (Matt 3:10; Luke 3:9) about trees without fruit being cut and burned.
15.7: ἐὰν μείνητε ἐν ἐμοὶ καὶ τὰ ῥήματά μου ἐν ὑμῖν μείνῃ — "if you remain in me and my words remain in you." Two conditions, both present subjunctive: the mutual remaining of disciples in Jesus and of Jesus's words (rhēmata) in the disciples. Rhēmata (plural of rhēma, G4487) — the specific sayings, the individual words — as opposed to logos (the unified word or message). ὃ ἐὰν θέλητε αἰτήσασθε — "ask whatever you wish." The aorist imperative aitēsasthe — the command to petition. The condition on asking is not arbitrary desire but the double remaining: one who remains in Jesus and has Jesus's words remaining in them will, by that very formation, want what the Father wills.
15.8: ἐν τούτῳ ἐδοξάσθη ὁ πατήρ μου, ἵνα καρπὸν πολὺν φέρητε — "in this is my Father glorified — that you bear much fruit." The aorist passive edoxasthē — "was glorified" — is again gnomic: the bearing of fruit constitutes the Father's glorification as a realized reality. καὶ γένησθε ἐμοὶ μαθηταί — "and you become my disciples." Génēsthe (G1096, aorist subjunctive of ginomai) — become, come to be. Discipleship is not a static status but an ongoing becoming that fruit-bearing both expresses and constitutes.
15.9: καθὼς ἠγάπησέν με ὁ πατήρ, κἀγὼ ὑμᾶς ἠγάπησα — "as the Father loved me, I also loved you." Both verbs are aorist — completed historical acts of love. The Father's love for the Son and the Son's love for the disciples are structurally parallel: the same kind of love, the same verb, the same tense. This is not analogy but derivation — Jesus's love for the disciples flows from and replicates the Father's love for him. μείνατε ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ τῇ ἐμῇ — "remain in my love." The aorist imperative meínate here modifies the key Johannine command: abiding in Jesus (vv4–7) is now specified as abiding in his love. Love is not an addition to union with Christ — it is what union with Christ feels like from the inside.
15.10: ἐὰν τὰς ἐντολάς μου τηρήσητε, μενεῖτε ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ μου — "if you keep my commandments, you will remain in my love." The future indicative meneite — this is not a conditional threat but a description of how remaining-in-love works: obedience is the relational channel. καθὼς ἐγὼ τὰς ἐντολὰς τοῦ πατρός μου τετήρηκα καὶ μένω ἐν τῇ ἀγάπῃ αὐτοῦ — "as I have kept my Father's commandments and remain in his love." Two tenses: perfect tetērēka ("I have kept and it stands kept") + present menō ("I remain"). Jesus's own Sonship is the template for the disciples' discipleship: his keeping of the Father's commands is both the model and the ground of theirs.
15.11: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα ἡ χαρὰ ἡ ἐμὴ ἐν ὑμῖν ᾖ — "these things I have spoken to you so that my joy may be in you." The perfect lelalēka again — completed disclosure. The purpose clause specifies the goal: not obedience for its own sake but the transmission of Christ's own joy. Chará (G5479, joy) — the noun from chaírō (to rejoice). καὶ ἡ χαρὰ ὑμῶν πληρωθῇ — "and your joy may be fulfilled/completed." Plēroō (G4137, aorist passive subjunctive) — to fill to fullness. The same verb used for Scripture's fulfillment; joy is "fulfilled" as a promise or vessel is filled to completion. Joy is the goal and product of abiding, not a byproduct.
15.12: αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ ἐντολὴ ἡ ἐμή, ἵνα ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους — "this is my commandment: that you love one another." The singular entolē — "commandment" — here, after the plural "commandments" in vv10, 21, 14:15. The plural commands are gathered into one: mutual love. καθὼς ἠγάπησα ὑμᾶς — "as I loved you." The aorist again — Jesus's completed act of love is the standard and measure. "Love one another" without this qualifier would be ambiguous; "as I loved you" sets the definition by what will happen at the cross.
15.13: μείζονα ταύτης ἀγάπην οὐδεὶς ἔχει — "greater than this love no one has." The superlative of agapē is defined by its limit: giving life. Meizōna (G3187) — greater, highest — comparative used for the superlative. ἵνα τις τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ θῇ ὑπὲρ τῶν φίλων αὐτοῦ — "that one lays down his life for his friends." Psuchēn (G5590) — soul, life, the whole self. Títhēmi (G5087) — to lay down, to put — the verb Jesus consistently uses for his own death (10:11, 15, 17, 18). The present subjunctive thē presents the action as a particular case. The "friends" (philoi) here are the disciples — not the universal humanitarian but the covenanted community. This verse defines the standard that v12 ("love as I loved you") enacts.
15.14: ὑμεῖς φίλοι μού ἐστε ἐὰν ποιῆτε ἃ ἐγὼ ἐντέλλομαι ὑμῖν — "you are my friends if you do what I command you." The conditional is present subjunctive — ongoing doing. Phílos (G5384) — friend, one who is dear, a beloved companion. In the patronage world, philos carried significant social weight: a friend of the patron was one who had privileged access and trust. The condition — doing what Jesus commands — is not a transactional requirement but an organic description: friendship with Jesus is constituted by alignment with his will, as obedience to the Father constitutes the Son's relationship with him.
15.15: οὐκέτι λέγω ὑμᾶς δούλους — "no longer do I call you servants/slaves." Doúlos (G1401) — slave, bond-servant. In the honor-shame world, the slave does not know the master's plans — only instructions. ὑμᾶς δὲ εἴρηκα φίλους — "but I have called you friends." Perfect eirēka — a declaration that stands. ὅτι πάντα ἃ ἤκουσα παρὰ τοῦ πατρός μου ἐγνώρισα ὑμῖν — "because all that I heard from my Father I have made known to you." Two aorists: ēkousa (I heard) and egnōrisa (I made known). The disclosure is complete and total — panta (all things). The disciples are philoi because they have been admitted into the counsel of God that Jesus received from the Father.
15.16: οὐχ ὑμεῖς με ἐξελέξασθε, ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ ἐξελεξάμην ὑμᾶς — "not you chose me, but I chose you." Both verbs are aorist: the election is a completed historical act. Eklégomai (G1586) — to choose, to select out from a larger group. The emphatic pronoun contrast — ouch hymeis vs. all᾽ egō — makes the priority of the divine initiative absolute. καὶ ἔθηκα ὑμᾶς ἵνα ὑμεῖς ὑπάγητε καὶ καρπὸν φέρητε — "and I appointed you so that you might go and bear fruit." Títhēmi (G5087) — to place, to appoint — used for laying down the life (10:11), now for commissioning. The election is purposive: chosen for mission, not merely for status. καρπὸς ὑμῶν μένῃ — "your fruit might remain." Menō again: the fruit produced by those remaining in the vine is itself characterized by remaining — enduring, permanent results.
15.17: ταῦτα ἐντέλλομαι ὑμῖν, ἵνα ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους — the command of v12 is repeated exactly: the vine discourse returns to its ethical center. The repetition brackets the intermediate material (vv13–16) as the ground and explication of mutual love. The Evangelist frames the commandment twice: it is the command, and it requires a reason, and the reason is given between the two statements of the command.
15.18: εἰ ὁ κόσμος ὑμᾶς μισεῖ, γινώσκετε ὅτι ἐμὲ πρῶτον ὑμῶν μεμίσηκεν — "if the world hates you, know that it has hated me before you." The perfect memísēken — "has hated and continues to hate." Jesus's being-hated is a completed, standing fact that precedes and explains the disciples' experience. Ginōskete can be indicative ("you know") or imperative ("know!") — either reading makes sense: the disciples either already know this or need to know it as a ground for resilience.
15.19: εἰ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου ἦτε, ὁ κόσμος ἂν τὸ ἴδιον ἐφίλει — "if you were of the world, the world would love its own." The conditional is past contrary-to-fact: ēte (imperfect, you were) + an ephílei (would love). To ídion — "its own" — the world loves what belongs to it, what shares its nature. ὅτι δὲ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου οὐκ ἐστέ, ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ ἐξελεξάμην ὑμᾶς ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου — "but because you are not of the world, but I chose you out of the world." The election in v16 now has a negative implication: to be chosen out of the world is to be set against it. Ek tou kosmou — "out of the world" — spatial metaphor of extraction from a domain.
15.20: μνημονεύετε τοῦ λόγου οὗ ἐγὼ εἶπον ὑμῖν — "remember the word that I said to you." Mnēmoneuete (G3421, present imperative) — keep on remembering. The word is the servant-master proverb: "a servant is not greater than his master." This appeared in the footwashing scene (13:16); here it is applied to persecution: the treatment of the master predicts and defines the treatment of the servants. εἰ ἐμὲ ἐδίωξαν, καὶ ὑμᾶς διώξουσιν — "if they persecuted me, they will also persecute you." The aorist ediōxan and the future diōxousin frame the disciples' future in the pattern of Jesus's past.
15.21: ἀλλὰ ταῦτα πάντα ποιήσουσιν ὑμῖν διὰ τὸ ὄνομά μου — "but all these things they will do to you on account of my name." The phrase dia to onoma mou — "on account of my name" — makes the persecution christological: the disciples are targeted not for their own sake but because of their association with Jesus. ὅτι οὐκ οἴδασιν τὸν πέμψαντά με — "because they do not know the one who sent me." The perfect oidasin — "they have not come to know and it stands unknown." The world's hatred of Jesus and his followers stems from its fundamental not-knowing of the Father.
15.22: εἰ μὴ ἦλθον καὶ ἐλάλησα αὐτοῖς, ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ εἴχοσαν — "if I had not come and spoken to them, they would not have sin." The condition is past contrary-to-fact: ēlthon and elalēsa (aorists of real events) are cast counterfactually. The point is not that Jesus's coming creates sin but that it removes the excuse for not-knowing. νῦν δὲ πρόφασιν οὐκ ἔχουσιν περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας αὐτῶν — "but now they have no excuse for their sin." Próphasis (G4392) — a pretext, a plausible reason for denial. The coming of the light makes darkness inexcusable.
15.23: ὁ ἐμὲ μισῶν καὶ τὸν πατέρα μου μισεῖ — "the one hating me also hates my Father." The logic of Johannine identity: to hate the Son is to hate the one who sent him, because to see the Son is to see the Father (14:9). There is no access to the Father that bypasses the Son. The two loves and two hates are structurally identical.
15.24: εἰ τὰ ἔργα μὴ ἐποίησα ἐν αὐτοῖς ἃ οὐδεὶς ἄλλος ἐποίησεν — "if I had not done among them the works that no one else did." Oudeis allos — no one else. The works of Jesus are categorically unique — not merely impressive but without precedent. ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ εἴχοσαν — "they would not have sin" — the same construction as v22: the works, like the words, remove the excuse of ignorance. Rejection after seeing the unique works is culpable rejection.
15.25: ἀλλ᾽ ἵνα πληρωθῇ ὁ λόγος ὁ ἐν τῷ νόμῳ αὐτῶν γεγραμμένος — "but that the word written in their law might be fulfilled." Plēroō (G4137, aorist passive subjunctive) — the fulfillment formula. The phrase "their law" is not dismissive but rhetorically pointed: Jesus cites their own scriptures against them. ὅτι ἐμίσησάν με δωρεάν — "that they hated me without cause." Dōreán (G1432) — gratis, freely, without payment or reason. The citation draws from Ps 35:19 or 69:4. The hatred is gratuitous: there is no provocation in Jesus that grounds it; the antipathy is self-originating in the world's alienation from God.
15.26: ὅταν ἔλθῃ ὁ παράκλητος ὃν ἐγὼ πέμψω ὑμῖν παρὰ τοῦ πατρός — the third Paraclete saying, and here the sending agent shifts again to Jesus: "whom I will send to you from the Father." In 14:16, the Father sends in response to the Son's prayer; in 14:26, the Father sends in the Son's name; here, the Son sends from the Father. The two missions (Father-sends, Son-sends) are held in tension without contradiction, reflecting the coinherence of the persons. τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας ὃ παρὰ τοῦ πατρὸς ἐκπορεύεται — "the Spirit of truth who proceeds from the Father." Ekporeúomai (G1607) — to go out from, to proceed out of. This verb became the focus of the Filioque controversy (whether the Spirit proceeds from the Father alone or from the Father and the Son). The verse says "from the Father" without "and the Son" — the sending (economic mission) and the eternal procession (ontological origin) are distinct questions. ἐκεῖνος μαρτυρήσει περὶ ἐμοῦ — "that one will testify concerning me." The Spirit's primary role here is witness to Jesus, not independent revelation.
15.27: καὶ ὑμεῖς δὲ μαρτυρεῖτε — "and you also testify." Present imperative — ongoing witness. The disciples bear witness alongside the Spirit: two witnesses, consistent with the requirement of Deut 17:6 and 19:15. The ground for their witness: ὅτι ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἐστε — "because you have been with me from the beginning." Perfect participle implied: their continuous presence from the beginning qualifies them as eyewitnesses. "The beginning" echoes 1:1 and 1 John 1:1 — the Johannine community's foundational claim to original witness.

16.1: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα μὴ σκανδαλισθῆτε — "these things I have spoken to you so that you may not stumble." The perfect lelalēka — completed, standing disclosure. Skandalísthēte (G4624, aorist passive subjunctive of skandalízō) — to stumble, to fall into a trap, to be made to fall. The word skandalon (G4625) is a snare or stumbling-block; here the stumbling is apostasy under pressure. The Farewell Discourse as a whole — the promise of the Paraclete, the vine and branches, the friendship promise — is given with this explicit protective purpose.
16.2: ἀποσυναγώγους ποιήσουσιν ὑμᾶς — "they will make you expelled from the synagogue." Aposynágōgos (G656) — a rare compound occurring only three times in the NT, all in John (9:22; 12:42; 16:2). This was formal exclusion from Jewish communal life — loss of legal standing, social network, family ties, and economic participation.
